12	--- Page 2 ---
13	Chroma Technical Report
14	July 14, 2025
15	Context Rot: How Increasing
16	Input Tokens Impacts LLM
17	Performance
18	Kelly Hong
19	Anton Troynikov
20	Jeff Huber
21	Large Language Models (LLMs) are typically presumed to process
22	context uniformly—that is, the model should handle the 10,000th
23	token just as reliably as the 100th. However, in practice, this
24	assumption does not hold. We observe that model performance
25	varies significantly as input length changes, even on simple tasks.
26	In this report, we evaluate 18 LLMs, including the state-of-the-art
27	GPT-4.1, Claude 4, Gemini 2.5, and Qwen3 models. Our results
28	reveal that models do not use their context uniformly; instead, their
29	performance grows increasingly unreliable as input length grows.
30	[image]
31	[image]
32	Claude Sonnet 4, GPT-4.1, Qwen3-32B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash on
33	Repeated Words Task
34	—Table of Contents
35	--- Page 3 ---
36	•  Introduction
37	•  Related Work
38	•  Needle in a Haystack Extension
39	•  Needle-Question Similarity
40	•  Impact of Distractors
41	•  Needle-Haystack Similarity
42	•  Haystack Structure
43	•  LongMemEval
44	•  Repeated Words
45	•  Limitations & Future Work
46	•  Conclusion
47	•  Footnotes
48	•  References
49	•  Appendix
50	Recent developments in LLMs show a trend toward longer context
51	windows, with the input token count of the latest models reaching
52	the millions. Because these models achieve near-perfect scores on
53	widely adopted benchmarks like Needle in a Haystack (NIAH) [1],
54	it’s often assumed that their performance is uniform across long-
55	context tasks.
56	However, NIAH is fundamentally a simple retrieval task, in which a
57	known sentence (the “needle”) is placed in a long document of
58	unrelated text (the “haystack”), and the model is prompted to
59	retrieve it. While scalable, this benchmark typically assesses direct
60	lexical matching, which may not be representative of flexible,
61	semantically oriented tasks.
62	[image]
63	Example Needle in a Haystack (NIAH) Setup
64	We extend the standard NIAH task, to investigate model behavior in
65	previously underexplored settings. We examine the effects of
66	needles with semantic, rather than direct lexical matches, as well as
67	the effects of introducing variations to the haystack content.
68	Additionally, we include a conversational question-answer
69	evaluation using LongMemEval [2], as well as a synthetic task in
70	which models replicate a series of repeated words. Each task
71	remains intentionally simple and is deliberately controlled to isolate
72	--- Page 4 ---
73	the impact of context length alone.
74	We demonstrate that even under these minimal conditions, model
75	performance degrades as input length increases, often in surprising
76	and non-uniform ways. Real-world applications typically involve
77	much greater complexity, implying that the influence of input
78	length may be even more pronounced in practice.
79	Our in-depth technical report continues below. If you find our work
80	useful, please consider citing us:
81	plaintext
82	@techreport{hong2025context,
83	  title = {Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impa
84	  author = {Hong, Kelly and Troynikov, Anton and Huber, 
85	  year = {2025},
86	  month = {July},
87	  institution = {Chroma},
88	  url = {https://trychroma.com/research/context-rot},
89	}
90	Interested in working on improving retrieval for AI applications?
91	Chroma is Hiring
92	Introduction#
93	It is common for modern LLMs to have input context lengths in the
94	millions of tokens. Gemini 1.5 Pro [3] first introduced their 1M
95	context window in early 2024, followed by the recent GPT-4.1’s 1M
96	context window [4] and Llama 4 with 10M [5]. The use case for
97	long context is compelling: longer context means that the LLM can
98	process more information with each call and generate more
99	informed outputs.
100	Long context evaluations for these models often demonstrate
101	--- Page 5 ---
102	consistent performance across input lengths. However, these
103	evaluations are narrow in scope and not representative of how long
104	context is used in practice. The most commonly used test, Needle in
105	a Haystack (NIAH), is a simple lexical retrieval task often used to
106	generalize a model’s ability to reliably handle long context. Real
107	applications, such as agent tasks or summarization, demand
108	significantly more processing and reasoning over broader, often
109	more ambiguous information.
110	Designing realistic long context benchmarks is challenging. Tasks
111	often grow in complexity as input length increases, making it
112	difficult to isolate whether performance drops are due to longer
113	inputs or inherently harder problems. To address this, our
114	experiments hold task complexity constant while varying only the
115	input length—allowing us to directly measure the effect of input
116	length alone.
117	Contributions#
118	We present the following:
119	•  An evaluation across 18 LLMs, including leading closed-
120	source and open-weights models, revealing nonuniform
121	performance with increasing input length.
122	•  A writeup of observed model-specific behavior patterns when
123	handling distractors and varying question-answer similarity.
124	•  The complete codebase to replicate our results.
125	Related Work#
126	One of the most widely used benchmarks for evaluating a model’s
127	long context capabilities is Needle in a Haystack (NIAH). While
128	useful as a scalable test, it measures a narrow capability: lexical
129	retrieval. Models typically perform well on NIAH, which has led to
130	the perception that long-context is largely solved.
131	--- Page 6 ---
132	However, NIAH underestimates what most long context tasks
133	require in practice. Variants of NIAH, like NoLiMa [6] which
134	include needle-question pairs with non-lexical matches, reveal
135	significant performance drops. Other tasks that appear similar in
136	regards to difficulty, such as AbsenceBench [7] which tests models
137	for recognizing the absence of a given snippet of text, also
138	demonstrate performance degradation with growing input length.
139	Additionally, long context tasks often involve disambiguating
140	amongst distractors as part of the task. One example is Multi-round
141	co-reference resolution (MRCR) [8] [9], which involves retrieving
142	the i-th instance of a specific user ask, amongst similar user asks, in
143	a multi-turn conversation. However, there remains a lack of
144	investigation into the impact of distractors in long context settings.
145	An important factor in long-context tasks is how input length is
146	scaled. Latent List [8] is a task in which the model must perform a
147	fixed number of Python list operations across various input lengths.
148	Various ways to fill irrelevant context are tested, which reveal non-
149	uniform impact on model performance [1]. For instance, adding list
150	operations that locally cancel each other out degrades model
151	performance more significantly compared to adding print
152	statements. This highlights how the type of 'irrelevant content'
153	matters, as some may introduce increasing complexity with input
154	length.
155	Similarly, Graphwalks [10] is a graph traversal task in which the
156	model is given a directed graph composed of hexadecimal hashes,
157	then asked to perform breadth-first search starting from a random
158	node. Increasing input length means increasing the size of the graph
159	to traverse through, which increases task difficulty as a result. It is
160	difficult to disambiguate increasing task complexity from input
161	length, which makes it difficult to isolate the impact on
162	performance due to input length alone. This points to the
163	importance of isolating input length as the variable of interest,
164	which is essential for understanding of how LLMs actually behave
165	with long inputs.
166	--- Page 7 ---
167	Needle in a Haystack
168	Extension#
169	The classic Needle in a Haystack task involves placing a random
170	fact (the 'needle') in the middle of a long context window (the
171	'haystack'), then asking the model about that fact.
172	The original implementation of this task uses a needle-question pair
173	with lexical matches. However, usage of long context in practice
174	often requires semantic understanding of ambiguous tasks.
175	[image]
176	Example Needle in a Haystack (NIAH) Setup with Lexical Matching
177	NoLiMa has demonstrated non-lexical matching to be a challenge
178	for models as context length increases. This task utilizes needle-
179	question pairs that require models to infer latent associations, for
180	example:
181	 
182	Question: Which character has been to Helsinki?
183	 
184	Needle: Actually, Yuki lives next to the Kiasma
185	museum.
186	 
187	NoLiMa - sample Needle-Question pair
188	In order to answer this question, the model would first have to
189	know that Kiasma museum is located in Helsinki, then make that
190	latent association link. This tests the model not only for its non-
191	lexical matching abilities, but also for its world knowledge. 72.4%
192	of needle-question pairs from NoLiMa require such external
193	knowledge, making this benchmark closer to a test of how models
194	handle both tasks at once rather than pure non-lexical matching
195	alone.
196	--- Page 8 ---
197	Testing the impact of non-lexical matching in isolation remains
198	underexplored. Furthermore, this binary distinction of “lexical”
199	versus “non-lexical” oversimplifies the complexity of question-

input.content:
---
id: source-context-rot
date: 2026-05-06
source: raw/papers/context-rot-how-increasing-input-tokens-impacts-llm.pdf
type: 论文/报告
tags: [长上下文, LLM评估, 上下文退化, 注意力机制, Chroma, 技术报告]
---

# Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance

## 一句话总结

Chroma 团队对 18 个主流 LLM（包括 GPT-4.1、Claude 4、Gemini 2.5、Qwen3）的系统评估表明：**即使任务复杂度保持不变，仅增加输入 token 长度就会导致模型性能非均匀地退化**，揭示了"上下文腐烂"（Context Rot）现象——简单扩展上下文窗口并不能可靠地提升模型利用长上下文的能力。

## 基本信息

| 项目 | 内容 |
|------|------|
| **标题** | Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance |
| **作者** | Kelly Hong, Anton Troynikov, Jeff Huber |
| **机构** | Chroma |
| **日期** | July 14, 2025 |
| **类型** | 技术报告（Technical Report） |
| **URL** | https://trychroma.com/research/context-rot |
| **代码** | 完整复现代码已开源 |

## 研究背景与动机

### 核心问题

现代 LLM 的上下文窗口已扩展到百万级 token（Gemini 1.5 Pro 1M、GPT-4.1 1M、Llama 4 10M），业界普遍假设模型能**均匀地处理上下文**——第 10,000 个 token 应该和第 100 个 token 一样可靠。然而，这个假设在实践中并不成立。

### NIAH 的局限性

当前最广泛使用的长上下文基准测试 **Needle in a Haystack (NIAH)** 本质上是一个简单的**词汇匹配检索任务**：将一个已知句子（"针"）放入长文档（"草堆"）中，让模型检索它。虽然可扩展，但 NIAH 仅评估直接词汇匹配能力，不能代表需要灵活语义理解的真实场景。

由于模型在 NIAH 上通常取得接近完美的分数，这导致了一种错误认知：长上下文问题已经"基本解决"。

### Context Rot 是 δ-mem 的核心动机

本文是 [[delta-mem-efficient-online-memory|δ-mem]] 论文引用的核心动机文献之一。δ-mem 的作者明确指出：**简单扩展上下文窗口只是把记忆问题转化为长上下文处理问题，并没有从根本上解决记忆**。Context Rot 的实验证据直接支撑了这一论断——即使上下文窗口足够大，模型也无法可靠地利用其中的信息。

## 核心发现

### 实验设计原则

本报告的关键设计原则是**保持任务复杂度恒定，仅改变输入长度**，从而将输入长度本身作为唯一变量来测量其对性能的影响。这与许多现有基准不同——后者往往让任务复杂度随输入长度一起增长，导致无法区分性能下降是来自更长的输入还是更难的任务。

### 实验一：Needle-Question 相似度（语义匹配）

**实验设置**：
- 使用 Paul Graham  essays 和 arXiv 论文作为两种 haystack 主题
- 对每个主题，人工编写 8 个 needle，通过嵌入模型计算 needle-question 的余弦相似度
- 使用 5 个不同的嵌入模型取平均，确保鲁棒性
- 测试 8 个输入长度 × 11 个 needle 位置
- 评估 18 个模型（包括 thinking/non-thinking 模式）

**关键发现**：
- **相似度越低，退化越快**：随着 needle-question 相似度降低，模型性能随输入长度增加而退化得更显著
- 在短输入长度下，即使是低相似度配对，模型也能表现良好——说明模型**有能力**完成这类任务
- 性能退化**不是**因为 needle-question 配对本身的内在难度，而是因为输入长度增加
- needle 位置对性能没有显著影响（与某些先前研究的发现不同）

### 实验二：干扰项的影响（Distractors）

**实验设置**：
- 选取一个高相似度的 needle-question 配对作为基线
- 人工编写 4 个干扰项（distractors），每个都与 needle 主题相关但不能直接回答问题
- 三种测试条件：仅 needle（基线）、needle + 1 个随机干扰项、needle + 4 个随机干扰项

**关键发现**：
- **单个干扰项就会降低性能**，4 个干扰项进一步加剧退化
- **干扰项的影响是非均匀的**：不同干扰项造成的性能下降程度不同。例如，在 arXiv haystack + PG essay needle 组合中，distractor 3 造成的性能下降明显大于其他干扰项
- **干扰项的影响随输入长度增长而放大**
- **模型家族间存在行为差异**：
  - **Claude 家族**：幻觉率最低，尤其 Claude Sonnet 4 和 Opus 4 在不确定时会选择弃权（abstain），明确声明找不到答案
  - **GPT 家族**：幻觉率最高，在存在干扰项时经常生成自信但错误的回答

### 实验三：Needle-Haystack 相似度

**实验设置**：
- 使用 Paul Graham essays 和 arXiv 论文两种 haystack
- 为每种 haystack 编写对应的 needle，测量 needle 与 haystack 的语义相似度
- 将 PG essay needle 放入 arXiv haystack，反之亦然，形成"相似"与"不相似"的对比

**关键发现**：
- 结果**不一致且非均匀**：
  - 在 PG essay haystack 中，arXiv needle（不相似）表现显著优于 PG essay needle（相似）——即 needle 与 haystack 越不相似，模型表现越好
  - 但在 arXiv haystack 中，两种 needle 的性能差异很小
- 由于仅测试了两个主题，无法得出"高 needle-haystack 相似度必然导致性能下降"的一般性结论
- 但明确揭示了：即使任务结构和 needle-question 相似度保持不变，改变 needle 与 haystack 的语义相似度就能影响结果——这是长上下文基准测试中被忽视的重要维度

### 实验四：Haystack 结构（逻辑连贯性 vs 随机打乱）

**实验设置**：
- **Original**：保留 haystack 中每段摘录的自然逻辑流
- **Shuffled**：将 haystack 中所有句子随机重排，保持相同主题但消除逻辑连续性

**关键发现**（最反直觉的发现）：
- **结构连贯性一致地损害模型性能**
- 在所有 18 个模型和所有 needle-haystack 组合中，模型在**打乱的 haystack 上表现更好**
- 这与直觉相反：如果 haystack 由连贯的 essay 组成，随机插入的 needle 应该更容易被发现（因为它打断了逻辑流）；而在打乱的 haystack 中，needle 应该更容易"混入"
- 实际结果恰恰相反，暗示模型可能以**结构化、顺序敏感**的方式处理上下文，而逻辑连贯的 haystack 可能让模型"陷入"其叙事流中

### 实验五：LongMemEval（对话式问答）

**实验设置**：
- 使用 LongMemEval 基准（平均 113k token 的对话历史）
- 对比两种条件：
  1. **Focused input**（~300 tokens）：仅包含回答问题所需的相关部分
  2. **Full input**（~113k tokens）：完整对话历史，包含大量无关上下文
- 过滤后保留 306 个 prompt，涵盖知识更新、时序推理、多会话三种类型
- 使用 GPT-4.1 作为 LLM judge，对齐度 >99%

**关键发现**：
- **所有模型在 full input 上性能显著下降**
- **Claude 家族的差距最大**：Claude Opus 4 和 Sonnet 4 在 full input 下特别保守，经常因不确定性而弃权。这与它们在 NIAH 干扰项实验中的行为一致
- **Thinking 模式有提升但无法消除差距**：启用 thinking 后，focused 和 full input 的性能都有提升，但两者之间的差距依然存在
- **问题类型排序**（非 thinking 模式）：知识更新 > 多会话 > 时序推理
- **问题类型排序**（thinking 模式）：知识更新 > 时序推理 > 多会话（thinking 帮助时序推理最多）

### 实验六：Repeated Words（重复词复现）

**实验设置**：
- 最简任务：模型被要求**精确复现**一段重复词序列，其中插入一个唯一的不同词
- 7 种词组合（如 "apple" 重复中插入 "apples"）
- 12 个词数级别（25 ~ 10,000 词）
- 1090 种上下文长度 × 唯一词位置 的组合
- 评分使用归一化 Levenshtein 距离

**关键发现**：
- **即使是最简单的复制任务，性能也随上下文长度增加而退化**
- **位置效应**：唯一词出现在序列开头时准确率最高，越靠后越容易出错
- **词数差异**：随着长度增加，模型经常生成到输出 token 上限就停止，导致欠生成
- **模型特异性行为**：
  - **Claude Opus 4**：拒绝率 2.89%（唯一拒绝任务的 Claude 模型），常先观察再决定是否执行，典型拒绝理由包括"可能生成版权材料"
  - **GPT-4.1**：拒绝率 2.55%，从 2500 词开始出现"I'm sorry, but I can't help with that"
  - **Gemini 2.5 Pro**：从 500-750 词开始出现随机词生成（如 "I'-a-le-le-le..."）
  - **Qwen3-8B**：4.21% 非尝试率，从 5000 词开始出现无意义重复文本
  - **GPT-4.1 nano**：在 "San Francisco"/"sf" 组合中输出小写 "san"，在 "Golden Gate Bridge"/"Golden Gate Park" 组合中输出 "Golden Golden"、"Gate Gate" 等不存在于输入中的词

## 关键实验数据

### 评估模型列表（18 个）

| 家族 | 模型 |
|------|------|
| **Anthropic** | Claude Opus 4, Claude Sonnet 4, Claude Sonnet 3.7, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Claude Haiku 3.5 |
| **OpenAI** | o3, GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1 mini, GPT-4.1 nano, GPT-4o, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-3.5 Turbo |
| **Google** | Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.0 Flash |
| **Alibaba** | Qwen3-235B-A22B, Qwen3-32B, Qwen3-8B |

### 实验条件汇总

| 实验 | 变量 | 条件数 | 关键控制 |
|------|------|--------|----------|
| Needle-Question 相似度 | 相似度（8 个 needle） | 8 长度 × 11 位置 | 任务复杂度恒定 |
| 干扰项 | 干扰项数量（0/1/4） | 3 条件 × 4 干扰项 | 高相似度 needle |
| Needle-Haystack 相似度 | haystack 主题 | 2 haystack × 2 needle 类型 | 相同 needle-question 对 |
| Haystack 结构 | 连贯 vs 打乱 | 2 条件 | 相同词汇集合 |
| LongMemEval | 输入长度（focused/full） | 2 条件 × 306 prompt | 相同问题 |
| Repeated Words | 词数（25~10,000） | 12 级别 × 多位置 | 完全相同的任务 |

### 评估方法

- **NIAH 实验**：GPT-4.1 作为 LLM judge，与人类判断对齐度 >99%
- **LongMemEval**：GPT-4.1 judge，同样 >99% 对齐
- **Repeated Words**：归一化 Levenshtein 距离 + 位置准确率 + 词数差异
- **模型调用**：temperature=0（除非不兼容，如 o3）；Qwen 使用 YaRN 扩展到 131,072 tokens

## 影响与启示

### 对 LLM 记忆机制设计的影响

1. **扩展上下文窗口 ≠ 解决记忆问题**：Context Rot 直接证明了即使上下文窗口足够大，模型也无法可靠利用其中的信息。这是 [[delta-mem-efficient-online-memory|δ-mem]] 等记忆机制的核心动机。

2. **上下文工程（Context Engineering）的重要性**：信息在上下文中的呈现方式（位置、结构、与干扰项的关系）比信息是否存在更重要。

3. **干扰项的放大效应**：在实际应用中（如 RAG、Agent 记忆），干扰项几乎不可避免。Context Rot 表明，随着上下文变长，干扰项的危害会被放大。

4. **模型选择需考虑长上下文行为**：不同模型家族在处理长上下文时的行为模式差异显著（Claude 保守弃权 vs GPT 自信幻觉），这会影响应用场景的选择。

### 对长上下文评估的启示

1. **NIAH 不足**：简单的词汇匹配基准不能代表真实的长上下文使用场景。
2. **隔离变量**：评估时必须将"输入长度"与"任务复杂度"分离，否则无法判断性能下降的真正原因。
3. **多维度评估**：需要同时考虑语义匹配、干扰项、上下文结构、对话历史等多个维度。

### 对实际应用的启示

1. **RAG 系统**：即使检索到了正确的文档片段，如果上下文中存在语义相似的干扰项，模型仍可能出错——且错误率随上下文长度增加。
2. **Agent 记忆**：简单地将完整对话历史塞入提示不是可靠的"记忆"方案。需要更智能的上下文管理（如 δ-mem 的在线状态压缩）。
3. **文本复制/生成任务**：即使是简单的文本复现，长序列也会导致可靠性下降——这对代码生成、文档处理等应用有直接影响。

## 提及的实体

- [[Kelly Hong]] — 第一作者，Chroma
- [[Anton Troynikov]] — 共同作者，Chroma
- [[Jeff Huber]] — 共同作者，Chroma
- [[Chroma]] — 向量数据库公司，本报告的发布机构
- [[OpenAI]] — GPT-4.1、o3、GPT-4o 等模型的开发者
- [[Anthropic]] — Claude 4、Claude 3.7/3.5 等模型的开发者
- [[Google]] — Gemini 2.5/2.0 的开发者
- [[Alibaba]] — Qwen3 系列的开发者

## 讨论的概念

- [[Context-Rot]] — 上下文腐烂现象：仅增加输入 token 长度就会导致 LLM 性能非均匀退化的现象
- [[Needle-in-a-Haystack]] — 经典的词汇匹配长上下文基准测试
- [[NoLiMa]] — 非词汇匹配的 NIAH 变体，需要模型推断潜在关联
- [[LongMemEval]] — 长上下文对话问答基准
- [[AbsenceBench]] — 测试模型识别文本缺失能力的基准
- [[Latent-List]] — 固定操作数、变化输入长度的 Python 列表操作任务
- [[Graphwalks]] — 图遍历任务，用于评估长上下文推理
- [[LLM-Judge]] — 使用对齐的 LLM 评估模型输出的方法
- [[YaRN]] — 上下文窗口扩展方法，本报告用于扩展 Qwen 模型

## 推荐参考文献

| # | 论文/资源 | 关联 |
|---|-----------|------|
| 1 | Kamradt (2023). Needle In A Haystack | 原始 NIAH 基准 |
| 2 | Wu et al. (2025). LongMemEval | 对话式长上下文评估 |
| 3 | Modarressi et al. (2025). NoLiMa | 非词汇匹配 NIAH 变体 |
| 4 | Fu et al. (2025). AbsenceBench | 文本缺失识别基准 |
| 5 | Vodrahalli et al. (2024). Michelangelo / Latent List | 固定复杂度、变化输入长度的评估 |
| 6 | Shi et al. (2023). Large Language Models Can Be Easily Distracted by Irrelevant Context | 干扰项对 LLM 的影响 |
| 7 | Peng et al. (2023). YaRN | 上下文窗口扩展 |

## 关联

- [[delta-mem-efficient-online-memory|δ-mem]] — 引用本文作为核心动机，论证简单扩展上下文窗口不能解决记忆问题
- [[LLMs-Get-Lost-in-Multi-turn]] — 互补工作，揭示多轮对话中 LLM 系统性"迷失"的问题
- [[Titans]] — 同样关注测试时记忆，提出将记忆作为模型架构的一等公民
- [[Memorizing-Transformers]] — 外部记忆方案，与 Context Rot 揭示的上下文不可靠性形成对比

---
id: concept-context-rot
date: 2026-05-06
aliases: [上下文腐烂, 上下文退化, Context Degradation]
tags: [概念, 长上下文, LLM评估, 注意力机制]
---

# Context Rot（上下文腐烂）

## 定义

**Context Rot**（上下文腐烂/退化）是指大型语言模型（LLM）在处理长输入时，即使**任务复杂度保持不变**，仅增加输入 token 长度就会导致性能非均匀退化的现象。该术语由 Chroma 团队（Hong et al., 2025）在 2025 年 7 月的技术报告中首次系统提出和验证。

核心洞察：**LLM 不会均匀地使用其上下文**——第 10,000 个 token 的处理可靠性显著低于第 100 个 token，即使两者承载的信息重要性相同。

## 关键属性

### 1. 非均匀性（Non-uniformity）

性能退化不是线性的，而是在某些"临界点"后急剧下降。不同模型、不同任务类型的退化曲线形态各异。

### 2. 任务无关性（Task Independence）

Context Rot 在最简单的任务中也能观察到——包括：
- 非词汇匹配的句子检索（Needle in a Haystack 扩展）
- 精确文本复现（Repeated Words）
- 对话历史中的事实检索（LongMemEval）

这意味着退化不是由任务内在难度导致的，而是由**输入长度本身**导致的。

### 3. 多因素交互（Multi-factor Interaction）

Context Rot 的严重程度受多个因素交互影响：

| 因素 | 影响方向 |
|------|----------|
| **Needle-Question 相似度** | 相似度越低，退化越快 |
| **干扰项（Distractors）** | 干扰项越多、越"逼真"，退化越严重 |
| **Needle-Haystack 相似度** | 结果不一致，但存在非均匀影响 |
| **Haystack 结构** | 逻辑连贯的 haystack 反而损害性能（反直觉） |
| **信息位置** | 在 Repeated Words 中，信息越靠后越容易丢失 |

### 4. 模型家族差异（Model Family Differences）

不同模型家族应对 Context Rot 的策略不同：

| 家族 | 典型行为 | 代表表现 |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Claude** | 保守弃权 | 不确定时明确拒绝回答，幻觉率低 |
| **GPT** | 自信幻觉 | 即使错误也生成自信回答，幻觉率高 |
| **Gemini** | 随机退化 | 长序列中出现无意义随机输出 |
| **Qwen** | 任务放弃 | 达到一定长度后直接不尝试任务 |

## 与相关概念的区别

### Context Rot vs 上下文窗口限制

- **上下文窗口限制**：模型根本"看不到"超出窗口的内容（硬限制）
- **Context Rot**：模型"能看到"所有内容，但**无法可靠处理**（软退化）

即使上下文窗口扩展到百万 token，Context Rot 依然存在。

### Context Rot vs 注意力稀释（Attention Dilution）

- **注意力稀释**：通常指注意力权重在长序列中被分散，每个位置获得的注意力减少
- **Context Rot**：范围更广，包括注意力机制之外的因素（如结构影响、干扰项放大、输出长度限制等）

注意力稀释可能是 Context Rot 的机制之一，但不是唯一机制。

### Context Rot vs 多轮迷失（Multi-turn Lost）

- **多轮迷失**（Laban et al., 2025）：特指多轮对话中模型丢失早期信息的现象
- **Context Rot**：更一般化，适用于任何长输入场景，不限于对话

两者互补：多轮对话中的迷失可以看作是 Context Rot 在对话场景下的具体表现。

## 实验证据

### 来源

主要实验证据来自 [[source-context-rot|Context Rot 技术报告]]，涵盖 18 个主流 LLM 的六个控制实验：

1. **Needle-Question 相似度**：低相似度配对的性能退化更快
2. **干扰项影响**：单个干扰项即可降低性能，4 个干扰项显著加剧退化
3. **Needle-Haystack 相似度**：语义相似度对性能有非均匀影响
4. **Haystack 结构**：打乱的 haystack 反而比连贯的 haystack 表现更好
5. **LongMemEval**：完整对话历史（113k tokens）vs 聚焦输入（300 tokens），所有模型在完整输入上性能下降
6. **Repeated Words**：最简单的文本复现任务，长序列下也出现位置错误和随机输出

## 对 LLM 架构的启示

### 为什么简单扩展上下文不够

Context Rot 揭示了 Transformer 注意力机制的根本局限：

1. **二次计算复杂度**：标准注意力的计算量随序列长度二次增长，限制了可处理的上下文规模
2. **注意力模式固化**：模型学习到的注意力模式可能不适应超长序列
3. **位置编码衰减**：绝对或相对位置编码在极长距离上可能失效
4. **输出长度耦合**：自回归生成中，输出长度也是输入的一部分，长输出进一步加剧问题

### 应对策略谱系

| 策略类型 | 代表方法 | 原理 | 局限 |
|----------|----------|------|------|
| **外部检索（RAG）** | BM25, Dense Retrieval | 只将相关片段放入上下文 | 检索噪声、相关片段可能遗漏 |
| **上下文压缩** | LLMLingua-2, Selective Context | 压缩或筛选上下文 | 信息损失、压缩本身引入错误 |
| **在线记忆状态** | [[δ-mem-在线关联记忆|δ-mem]], Titans | 将历史压缩到固定大小的动态状态 | 状态容量有限 |
| **参数化记忆** | LoRA, Context2LoRA | 将记忆编码到参数中 | 静态的，无法动态演化 |
| **稀疏注意力** | Longformer, BigBird | 降低注意力计算复杂度 | 可能丢失长距离依赖 |
| **线性注意力** | Mamba, RWKV, Kimi Linear | 用线性复杂度替代二次注意力 | 表达能力与标准注意力的差距 |

## 对应用开发的启示

### 上下文工程（Context Engineering）

Context Rot 表明，**信息如何呈现**比**信息是否存在**更重要：

1. **减少干扰项**：在 RAG 中，语义相似的文档片段应被更严格地去重或重排序
2. **结构化呈现**：虽然连贯结构在实验中表现更差，但这可能意味着需要新的结构化方法（如显式标记重要信息）
3. **位置优化**：关键信息应尽可能放在上下文的前部
4. **分块处理**：将长任务分解为多个短上下文调用

### 可靠性评估

在生产环境中部署长上下文应用时：
- 不应仅以 NIAH 分数判断模型的长上下文能力
- 需要在实际数据分布上测试语义匹配、干扰项容忍度等
- 考虑模型家族的特定行为（Claude 的保守性 vs GPT 的自信幻觉）

## 开放问题

1. **机制不明**：Context Rot 的根本原因尚未完全理解。是注意力机制的固有限制？还是训练数据分布的偏差？还是位置编码的衰减？
2. **与模型规模的关联**：更大的模型是否能更好地抵抗 Context Rot？
3. **与架构的关联**：非 Transformer 架构（如 Mamba、RWKV）是否同样受 Context Rot 影响？
4. **动态阈值**：是否存在一个"安全上下文长度"，在此之内 Context Rot 可以忽略？这个阈值是否因任务而异？
5. **与输出长度的交互**：Repeated Words 实验显示输入-输出长度耦合会加剧问题，但具体机制尚不清楚

## 来源

- [[source-context-rot|Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance]] — 首次系统提出和验证该现象
- [[delta-mem-efficient-online-memory|δ-mem]] — 引用 Context Rot 作为核心动机，提出在线记忆状态作为替代方案

## 参见

- [[Needle-in-a-Haystack]] — 经典的长上下文词汇匹配基准
- [[NoLiMa]] — 非词汇匹配的 NIAH 变体
- [[LongMemEval]] — 长上下文对话问答基准
- [[LLMs-Get-Lost-in-Multi-turn]] — 多轮对话中的记忆退化
- [[注意力机制]] — Transformer 的核心机制，Context Rot 的可能根源

# Chroma

## 概述

Chroma 是一家专注于向量数据库和检索基础设施的 AI 公司，为大型语言模型（LLM）应用提供嵌入存储和检索能力。公司以其开源向量数据库 Chroma DB 而闻名，同时也积极从事 LLM 长上下文和记忆机制的研究。

## 关键事实

- **核心产品**：Chroma DB — 开源向量数据库，支持嵌入存储、相似度搜索和元数据过滤
- **研究领域**：LLM 长上下文评估、上下文腐烂（Context Rot）、检索增强生成（RAG）
- **知名报告**：[[source-context-rot|Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance]]（2025 年 7 月）
- **官网**：https://trychroma.com

## 在来源中的出现

- [[source-context-rot|Context Rot 技术报告]] — 发布机构，Kelly Hong、Anton Troynikov、Jeff Huber 均来自 Chroma

## 关系

- [[Kelly Hong]] — Chroma 研究员，Context Rot 第一作者
- [[Anton Troynikov]] — Chroma 研究员，Context Rot 共同作者
- [[Jeff Huber]] — Chroma 研究员，Context Rot 共同作者
- [[向量数据库]] — Chroma 的核心产品类别
- [[RAG]] — Chroma 技术的主要应用场景

## 来源（9）

- [[context-rot-how-increasing-input-tokens-impacts-llm|Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance]] — Chroma 技术报告，18 个主流 LLM 的系统评估，揭示仅增加输入长度就会导致性能非均匀退化的"上下文腐烂"现象 | 2025-07-14 | #长上下文 #LLM评估 #上下文退化

- [[delta-mem-efficient-online-memory|δ-mem: Efficient Online Memory for LLMs]] — 冻结骨干 + 8×8 在线关联记忆状态 + delta-rule 低秩修正，轻量高效 LLM 记忆机制 | 2026-05-13 | #LLM记忆 #在线学习 #delta-rule
- [[swarm-ide|Swarm-IDE: 自组织的 Agent 蜂群]] — 去中心化多 Agent 协作平台，create+send 极简原语，动态嵌套 + 实时 Graph 可视化 + MCP 技能系统 | 2026-01-02 | #Agent #多Agent #开源 #MCP
- [[agent-world|Agent-World: Scaling Real-World Environment Synthesis for Evolving General Agent Intelligence]] — 1978 环境 + 19822 工具的自我进化 Agent 训练平台，8B/14B 超越闭源模型 | 2026-04 | #Agent #RL #MCP
- [[gstc-zero-cost-proxy-nas|Zero-Cost Proxy NAS-Driven Collaborative Deployment Optimization]] — GSTC 零成本代理 + JCQDA 部署 + LAMRA 动态替换，AI 服务全生命周期优化 | 2026-04-21 | #NAS #微服务
- [[joint-deployment-request-routing-microservice-tpds|Joint Deployment and Request Routing for Microservice Call Graphs]] — GMDA-RMPR 两阶段启发式，联合优化微服务部署与路由 | 2023-11 | #微服务 #排队网络

---

## 实体（21）

- [[Chroma]] — 向量数据库公司，Context Rot 技术报告的发布机构
- [[Kelly Hong]] — Chroma 研究员，Context Rot 第一作者
- [[Anton Troynikov]] — Chroma 研究员，Context Rot 共同作者

- [[Bharadwaj Veeravalli]] — 新加坡国立大学教授，TPDS 论文共同作者
- [[华中科技大学]] — 论文1和2的主要研究机构
- [[北京邮电大学]] — 论文3的主要研究机构

## 概念（21）

- [[Context-Rot]] — 上下文腐烂现象：仅增加输入 token 长度就会导致 LLM 性能非均匀退化
- [[LoRA]] — 低秩适配：冻结预训练权重，用低秩矩阵分解参数化权重更新，参数高效微调的经典方法

- [[delta-rule-learning]] — 基于预测误差的在线学习规则，只写入差分信息
- [[Language Server Protocol (LSP)]] — 编辑器与语言服务器之间的开放标准通信协议，基于 JSON-RPC
- [[Model Context Protocol (MCP)]] — AI 模型连接外部工具的统一开放标准协议
- [[Agent RL]] — Agent 在可执行环境中通过闭环交互学习的强化学习方法
- [[GRPO]] — 无需 critic 的组相对策略优化算法
- [[POMDP]] — 部分可观测马尔可夫决策过程，Agent 交互的理论框架
- [[工具依赖图]] — 工具间参数依赖关系的有向加权图，用于任务合成
- [[神经架构搜索 (NAS)]] — 自动化神经网络架构设计
- [[零成本代理]] — 无需训练即可评估架构性能
- [[微服务调用图]] — 微服务间调用关系形成的图结构
- [[M/M/C 排队模型]] — 经典多服务台排队论模型
- [[Jackson 排队网络]] — 可解析求解的开放排队网络
- [[AI as a Service (AIaaS)]] — 6G 网络中的 AI 服务基础设施模式
- [[Lyapunov 优化]] — 将长期随机优化分解为短期确定性子问题
- [[混合整数非线性规划 (MINLP)]] — 联合优化问题的通用数学建模形式
- [[任务卸载]] — 将计算任务从边缘转移到资源充足的节点执行
- [[边缘计算]] — 将计算能力下沉到靠近用户的位置
- [[两时间尺度优化]] — 长期慢变量与短期快变量的协同优化

- [[AI as a Service (AIaaS)]] — 6G 网络中的 AI 服务基础设施模式
- [[Lyapunov 优化]] — 将长期随机优化分解为短期确定性子问题

- [[任务卸载]] — 将计算任务从边缘转移到资源充足的节点执行

arXiv:2505.06120v1  [cs.CL]  9 May 2025
LLMS GET LOST IN MULTI-TURN CONVERSATION
Philippe Laban∗♢
Hiroaki Hayashi∗♣
Yingbo Zhou♣
Jennifer Neville♢
♢Microsoft Research
♣Salesforce Research
{plaban,jenneville}@microsoft.com
{hiroakihayashi,yingbo.zhou}@salesforce.com
ABSTRACT
Large Language Models (LLMs) are conversational interfaces. As such, LLMs have the potential to
assist their users not only when they can fully specify the task at hand, but also to help them define,
explore, and refine what they need through multi-turn conversational exchange. Although analysis of
LLM conversation logs has confirmed that underspecification occurs frequently in user instructions,
LLM evaluation has predominantly focused on the single-turn, fully-specified instruction setting. In
this work, we perform large-scale simulation experiments to compare LLM performance in single-
and multi-turn settings. Our experiments confirm that all the top open- and closed-weight LLMs
we test exhibit significantly lower performance in multi-turn conversations than single-turn, with
an average drop of 39% across six generation tasks. Analysis of 200,000+ simulated conversations
decomposes the performance degradation into two components: a minor loss in aptitude and a
significant increase in unreliability. We find that LLMs often make assumptions in early turns and
prematurely attempt to generate final solutions, on which they overly rely. In simpler terms, we
discover that when LLMs take a wrong turn in a conversation, they get lost and do not recover.

94	Figure 1: In this work, we simulate single- and multi-turn conversations for six generation tasks. The 15 LLMs we test
95	perform much worse in multi-turn settings (-35%) explained by some loss in aptitude, and large losses in reliability.
96	Aptitude is defined as performance in best-case conversation simulation, and unreliability as the gap between best- and
97	worst-case performance. In short, we find that LLMs get lost in multi-turn, underspecified conversation.
98	∗Equal Contributions

100	--- Page 2 ---
101	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
102	PREPRINT
103	1
104	Introduction
105	Today’s large language models (LLMs) function as conversational interfaces (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude), enabling
106	users to interact with the LLM through multiple conversation turns. Such interaction promises to help users not only
107	when they know what they need (i.e., they can fully specify their requirements in an instruction), but also when they
108	don’t. In such cases, users might start with an underspecified instruction and further clarify their needs through turn
109	interactions. Though studies of LLM conversation logs have confirmed that underspecification in user instructions is
110	prevalent [27], LLM systems are typically evaluated in single-turn, fully-specified settings.
111	Even though a growing body of work proposes to evaluate LLMs in a multi-turn fashion, we identify in our review
112	(Section 2) that most prior work treats the conversation as episodic: conversation turns might relate to each other, but
113	the conversation can effectively be decomposed as an array of subtasks that can be evaluated in isolation. We argue that
114	episodic tasks move away from what is prevalent in human conversation: underspecification [91, 27].
115	In this work, we close this gap by creating a simulation environment for multi-turn underspecified conversations –
116	sharded simulation – that leverages existing instructions from high-quality single-turn benchmarks. At a high level,
117	the sharding process we propose transforms existing single-turn instructions into sharded instructions, a set of smaller
118	instructions that jointly deliver the same information as the original instruction. Sharded simulation then ensures that
119	each turn of conversation reveals at most one shard of information per conversation turn, enforcing that the instruction
120	is gradually revealed through the conversation.
121	On the set of tasks that we experimented on, we observed that models engaged in multi-turn underspecified conversations
122	achieved an average performance of 65%–a 25-point drop from single-turn performances of 90% when they receive the
123	entire instruction at the beginning of the conversation. Notably, we observe this drop in performance even in two-turn
124	conversations, and across all LLMs we test, from small open-weights (LLama3.1-8B-Instruct) to state-of-the-art
125	(Gemini 2.5 Pro).
126	Furthermore, we decompose the performance degradation into two components: (1) loss in aptitude, and (2) increase in
127	unreliability. We find that in single-turn settings, models with higher aptitude tend to be more reliable (e.g., GPT-4.1,
128	Gemini 2.5 Pro). On the other hand, all LLMs exhibit very high unreliability in multi-turn settings, regardless of aptitude.
129	We refer to this as the lost in conversation phenomenon: when LLMs take a wrong turn in multi-turn conversation, they
130	get lost and do not recover.
131	We investigate several explanations for this effect and show that the LLMs tend to (1) generate overly verbose
132	responses, leading them to (2) propose final solutions prematurely in conversation, (3) make incorrect assumptions
133	about underspecified details, and (4) rely too heavily on previous (incorrect) answer attempts.
134	Our findings highlight a gap between how LLMs are used in practice and how the models are being evaluated.
135	Ubiquitous performance degradation over multi-turn interactions is likely a reason for low uptake of AI systems
136	[73, 4, 28], particularly with novice users who are less skilled at providing complete, detailed instructions from the
137	onset of conversation [87, 35].
138	The rest of the paper is structured as follows: Section 2 situates our work with respect to prior work on multi-turn
139	evaluation. In Section 3, we describe the simulation environment we built for both single- and multi-turn conversations
140	on a diverse set of generation tasks. We introduce the six tasks and the metrics we use to evaluate the aptitude and
141	reliability of models in Section 4.1. Sections 5-6 define our main experiment involving 15 LLMs, and analyze the main
142	findings. Finally, the Implications section (Section 7) discusses the ramifications of the work, from the perspective of
143	organizations that are building LLM-based conversation products, to that of end-users of the LLM-based systems. We
144	provide actionable recommendations based on small-scale experiments and make a concrete call-to-action to LLM
145	builders, urging them to prioritize multi-turn reliability in conjunction with aptitude in future model iterations.
146	2
147	Background and Related Work
148	Previous-generation language models (e.g., BART [45], GPT-2 [65], or T5 [66]) were not equipped to handle multi-turn
149	conversations, which led evaluation to focus on single-turn tasks [79]. Conversational AI was typically implemented as
150	specialized systems that leveraged language models as components [36], and were evaluated through human protocols
151	[17, 42, 21, 54], or competitions like Amazon’s Alex Prize [67].
152	As the meteoric rise of ChatGPT led to increased interest in multi-turn evaluation, initial popular efforts such as
153	MT-bench [89] leveraged crowd-sourced annotations to evaluate LLM-as-a-judge ability. Follow-up works expanded
154	on MT-bench, for instance to include longer conversations [37, 18], increase evaluation granularity [2], or to tackle
155	different aspects such as naturalness [72] or tool use [85, 80].
156	2
157	
158	--- Page 3 ---
159	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
160	PREPRINT
161	Crucially, such works typically simulate episodic conversations: each turn in the conversation introduces a subtask
162	that relates to previous conversation turns, but can be evaluated in isolation. In this work, we find that episodic
163	tasks overestimate LLM performance in multi-turn conversations (see Section 7.3). In short, although episodic tasks
164	require some level of multi-turn context understanding, they do not involve actively fusing the information to answer
165	underspecified user instructions. Underspecified user instructions are not only common in real-world human-AI
166	communication [27], but also a natural tendency in conversations, termed “the principle of least effort” [91]. We
167	show that underspecification in multi-turn conversations leads to large and universal performance degradations: LLMs
168	make early assumptions to fill in for missing information, prematurely attempt to propose finalized solutions, and have
169	difficulty adapting and course-correcting when provided with new information. We make underspecification the central
170	element of our evaluation setting.
171	Multi-turn episodic evaluation is sometimes framed as a way to evaluate multi-turn model capabilities with higher
172	granularity. Categories of subtasks (such as refinement, follow-up, expansion, etc.) allow for the study of more specific
173	LLM behavior [2, 37, 74, 19, 16, 48, 25]. According to such framing, multi-turn tasks differ from single-turn tasks and
174	are not evaluated on the same set of tasks. We argue that this framing is artificial and limits the scope of multi-turn
175	evaluation, restricting the direct comparison of multi-turn and single-turn abilities of LLMs. In our work, we conduct
176	both single-turn and multi-turn conversation simulations on a common set of tasks: controlled experiments that precisely
177	allow us to identify performance degradations from single- to multi-turn settings.
178	Evaluating LLMs in multi-turn settings is a challenge because conversational trajectories diverge far more than in a
179	single-turn. Thus, most previous studies have focused on classification or short-form tasks, with more straightforward
180	evaluation settings. However, the predominant use cases for LLMs are generative in nature, both for programming (e.g.,
181	coding assistants) and natural language (e.g., writing, summarizing) [88, 26]. Long-form evaluation in the multi-turn
182	setting is therefore essential, as it assesses models’ ability to flexibly adapt and refine the response as the users provide
183	more information. In this work, we focus exclusively on generation tasks that capture widely used scenarios in both
184	programming and natural language domains.
185	Scaling multi-turn experimentation requires simulating a user. Existing studies implemented such user simulation
186	in different ways: relying on templates [12, 68, 39, 16], using an LLM [63, 46, 7, 48], involving human annotators
187	[21, 7], or real users as part of a study [67, 38, 11]. Although involving real users leads to the most natural and realistic
188	conversations, it comes at the cost of scalability and reproducibility. In this work, we adopt an LLM-based simulator to
189	enable controlled flexibility and divergence. Nevertheless, a fully automated simulation limits the scope of our findings:
190	the conversations we simulate are not representative of human-AI conversations. We therefore frame the simulation
191	as a tool to study the LLM behavior in the multi-turn setting rather than user behavior. In addition, as detailed in the
192	Limitations Section (Section 9), we argue that our simulation framework is simplistic and idealized. For example, the
193	conversations are guaranteed to end with sufficient information to solve the tasks, and the simulator limits unexpected
194	behavior (e.g., derailing) that can occur in real-world settings. We suggest these choices imply that degradations
195	observed in this work are most likely underestimates of what occurs in real-world, underspecified multi-turn Human-AI
196	conversations. Appendix A introduces other related work specifically focused on underspecified communication.
197	3
198	Simulating Underspecified, Multi-Turn Conversation
199	To assess LLM performance in multi-turn, underspecified conversation, we develop a simulation environment that
200	repurposes existing tasks from single-turn benchmarks. First, we apply a sharding process to transform original
201	fully-specified instructions into sharded instructions. Second, we implement a sharding simulation environment that
202	carries out a multi-turn conversation based on a sharded instruction.
203	3.1
204	Sharding Process: From Fully-Specified to Sharded Instructions
205	An original, fully-specified instruction from GSM8K [14] and the equivalent sharded instruction are listed in Figure 2.
206	The original instruction is a single, long utterance that introduces all the content at once: a high-level question (i.e.,
207	“How long will it take [...]”), context, and conditions. The sharded instruction is composed of a set of shards, each
208	introducing a single element from the original instruction. More specifically, the first shard (Shard 1) of a sharded
209	instruction always introduces the high-level intent for the instruction, and subsequent shards each provide clarification to
210	the instruction. Taken jointly, the set of shards reflects the same information provided in the fully-specified instruction,
211	with the information explicitly divided across shards.
212	In Appendix B, we provide a more precise and mathematical definition of a sharded instruction in relation to the original
213	fully-specified instruction, and define five key properties a sharded instruction must satisfy to be considered valid.

270	Figure 3: Sharded Conversation Simulation Diagram. The subject for the simulation is highlighted in red.
271	Figure 3 depicts the process of simulating a multi-turn, underspecified conversation based on a sharded instruction. At a
272	high-level, the conversation involves three parties: the assistant is the LLM being evaluated in the simulation, the user
273	(simulated by an LLM) who has access to the entirety of the sharded instruction and is in charge of revealing shards
274	during turns of the conversation, and the system which categorizes and evaluates assistant responses.
275	On the first turn, the user simulator reveals the first shard of the instruction (i.e., Shard 1) to the assistant, which then
276	generates a free text response. The system processes the assistant’s response into one of seven possible response
277	strategies: clarification, refusal, hedging, interrogation, discussion, missing, or answer attempt,2 based on Herlihy et al.
278	[27]’s LLM response categorization. If the assistant generates an answer attempt (i.e., proposing an explicit, full-form
279	solution), then the answer extractor component determines the span that corresponds to the answer within the assistant’s
280	free-form response (e.g., code snippet, number). This step is required because LLMs often pad answer attempts with
281	additional information, such as a natural-language explanation or a follow-up question, which could hinder evaluation.
282	Finally, the extracted answer is scored by a task-specific evaluator function. Subsequent turns follow a similar pattern:
283	at each turn, the user simulator reveals at most one shard of information, the assistant responds freely, which gets
284	evaluated if the response is classified as an answer attempt. The conversation ends if one of two conditions is met:
285	(1) the task-evaluator assesses that an assistant answer attempt is correct, or (2) if at the start of a new turn, the user
286	simulator has run out of shards to reveal in the conversation.
287	Preliminary experiments revealed that during simulation, evaluated assistants often asked clarification questions that
288	related to specific shards of the instruction. As such, deciding which shard to reveal next in the conversation (the role of
289	the user simulator) is non-trivial, as it should take into account the state of the conversation so far. We instantiate the user
290	simulator as a low-cost LLM (specifically, GPT-4o-mini) that has access to the entire sharded instruction and the state of
291	the conversation so far, tasking it with deciding the next shard to reveal that fits most naturally in the ongoing simulated
292	2See Appendix G for the definition and the example for each strategy.
293	4
294	
295	--- Page 5 ---
296	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
297	PREPRINT
298	conversation. The user simulator is also tasked with rephrasing the shard to fit naturally within the conversation without
299	modifying its informational content. See Appendix J for an example simulated sharded conversation.
300	Besides user messages, the assistant receives a minimal system instruction (before the first turn) that provides the
301	necessary context to accomplish the task (such as a database schema or a list of available API tools). Importantly,
302	the assistant is not explicitly informed that it is participating in a multi-turn, underspecified conversation and is not
303	encouraged to pursue specific conversational strategies. Although such additional instructions would likely alter model
304	behavior, we argue that such changes are not realistic, as such information is not available a priori in practical settings.
305	In summary, we provide no information about the setting to the evaluated assistant model during simulation, aiming to
306	assess default model behavior.
307	Apart from the user simulator, the strategy classifier and answer extractor components are also implemented with
308	prompt-based GPT-4o-mini. While the choice of LLM-based components in the simulator allows for dynamic choices
309	that provide a more realistic simulation, they also unavoidably lead to simulation errors, which can affect the validity of
310	experiments. To understand the scope of simulation errors and their effect on simulation validity, we conducted an
311	in-depth manual annotation of several hundred simulatesouthworth2023developingd conversations. The annotation
312	effort and its findings are detailed in Appendix D. In summary, we found that errors introduced by the user simulator,
313	strategy classifier, or answer extraction occurred in less than 5% of inspected conversations and that these errors
314	disfavored the assistant model in less than 2% of the conversations. We believe the process described above can
315	accurately simulate multi-turn, underspecified conversations based on sharded instructions, and we rely on it to simulate
316	conversations for our experiments.

338	instructions. Once an original fully-specified instruction
339	(blue block) is sharded (set of yellow blocks), the “shards”
340	can be used to simulate single-turn (FULL, CONCAT) or
341	multi-turn (SHARDED, RECAP, SNOWBALL) conversations,
342	affecting the pace of information disclosure.
343	We leverage sharded instructions to simulate five types
344	of single- or multi-turn conversations, as illustrated in
345	Figure 4. We now introduce each one and explain its
346	purpose in our experiments.
347	FULLY-SPECIFIED
348	(short-form: FULL) simulates
349	single-turn, fully-specified conversations in which the
350	original instruction is provided to the LLM in the first
351	turn. This simulation type evaluates baseline model per-
352	formance on the tasks.
353	SHARDED
354	simulates multi-turn, underspecified con-
355	versations as outlined above. SHARDED simulations are
356	our primary tool to evaluate model performance in under-
357	specified, multi-turn conversations.
358	CONCAT
359	simulates single-turn, fully-specified con-
360	versation based on the sharded instruction. The shards
361	are concatenated into a single instruction in bullet-point
362	form (with one shard per line), preceded by an instruction to complete the task taking into account all bullet-points.
363	The CONCAT simulation is a logical mid-point between full and sharded, in which underspecification is removed (like
364	FULL) but the rephrasing that occurred during instruction sharding is preserved (like SHARDED). CONCAT is intended
365	as a verification baseline: a model that succeeds at both FULL and CONCAT, but not at SHARDED, struggles specifically
366	because of underspecification and the multi-turn nature of the conversation, and not due to the rephrasing that occurred
367	during the sharding process, which may have led to information loss.
368	RECAP
369	simulates a SHARDED conversation, and adds a final recapitulation turn which restates all the shards
370	of the instruction in a single turn, giving the LLM one final attempt at responding. RECAP is a combination of the
371	SHARDED simulation followed by a CONCAT turn, and is explored as a method in Section 7.1 to evaluate whether such
372	a conceptually simple agent-like intervention can mitigate the loss in performance observed in SHARDED conversations.
373	SNOWBALL
374	takes the RECAP simulation a step further, implementing turn-level recapitulation. At each turn, the
375	user simulator introduces a new shard, but also restates all the shards that have been revealed so far in the conversation,
376	producing a snowball effect as each turn reveals all the information from the previous turn, plus one additional shard.
377	The redundancy implemented in the SNOWBALL simulation is also explored as a method in Section 7.1 to study whether
378	turn-level reminders help alleviate the need for LLMs to recall information across multiple turns of context.

513	4
514	Task and Metric Selection
515	4.1
516	Task Selection
517	We constructed sharded instructions for six tasks that we use in a large-scale simulation experiment. For each task, we
518	selected instructions from one or two high-quality single-turn, fully-specified benchmarks, and implemented a semi-
519	automatic sharding process. The process relied first on an LLM (GPT-4o) to propose and verify sharding candidates,
520	which were then reviewed and edited (when necessary) by the authors of the work. The sharding process (outlined
521	in detail in Appendix C) allowed us to scale the construction of sharded instruction corpora while ensuring validity
522	of the underlying instructions. For each task, we prepared 90-120 sharded instructions (each paired with the original
523	single-turn instructions), which required between 1-4 hours of manual inspection and annotation.
524	We carefully selected popular and diverse generation tasks across programming and non-programming use cases.
525	Figure 5 provides an example of an original and sharded instruction for each task, which we now introduce.
526	Code
527	The assistant must help the user write a function in the Python programming language. The original
528	instructions were sourced from the HumanEval [10] and LiveCodeBench [31] datasets, two popular benchmarks used
529	to evaluate LLM programming aptitude.
530	Database
531	The assistant is provided with the schema of an SQL database and a user query in natural language,
532	and must produce an SQL query that retrieves the requested information from the database (a.k.a., text-to-SQL). The
533	original instructions and databases were sourced from the popular Spider dataset [86].
534	Actions
535	The assistant is provided with a set of API (Application Programming Interface) schemas, and a user
536	instruction that requires API use, and must generate the programmatic API commands that match the user request. We
537	sourced API schemas and user instructions from the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) [85], a popular
538	benchmark used to measure LLM ability at API function calling.
539	Math
540	The assistant is provided with an elementary math word problem, and must perform a series of calculations
541	using basic arithmetic operations to reach a numerical answer. We sourced problems from the GSM8K dataset [14].
542	6
543	
544	--- Page 7 ---
545	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
546	PREPRINT
547	Data-to-text
548	The assistant is provided tabular data and several elements of related metadata, and must produce a
549	caption (natural language sentence) describing the underlying data. We leverage the ToTTo [59] dataset to formulate
550	sharded instructions.
551	Summary
552	The assistant receives a corpus of around twenty documents and a user query, and must generate a
553	summary with citations that addresses the query based on the documents. We re-purpose the instructions from Summary
554	of a Haystack [40]. The summary task is the only task we include that tests long-context capabilities, with instructions
555	spanning several tens of thousands of tokens, which is known to deteriorate model performance [29, 32, 33].
556	For each task, we reuse the metrics used in the original benchmarks. More specifically, the first four tasks (Code,
557	Database, Actions, and Math) are evaluated for binary correctness, either by executing an answer attempt (code, SQL
558	query), or validating semantic equivalence to a reference answer (API call, numerical answer). The last two tasks
559	(Data-to-Text and Summary) are refinement tasks, which get scored on a continuous range (0-100). Data-to-text uses the
560	BLEU metric [58], and Summary uses a custom LLM-as-a-judge metric (“Joint Score”) built to measure information
561	coverage and attribution accuracy of the summary [40]. We map binary accuracy in the range of 0-100 (0 = failure, 100
562	= success) so that all tasks produce scores on a common scale, facilitating aggregation.
563	Appendix I lists implementation details of the sharding process for each task, including the sample selection process and
564	any task-specific logic that was implemented to facilitate reproducibility. Even though we intended for the six selected
565	tasks to be representative of a wide range of LLM use cases, we put effort into making the sharding process efficient
566	and reproducible, as we see the process itself as a contribution of our work. We envision that future LLM evaluation
567	practitioners can shard their own dataset artifacts to study LLM multi-turn behavior in more diverse and unique settings.
568	4.2
569	Metric Selection
570	LLMs employ a stochastic process to generate text. When setting LLM generation parameters to their default (e.g.,
571	T=1.0), LLMs generate many distinct responses for a fixed conversation state. We leverage this property to conduct
572	repeated simulations for a given instruction and observe the variations that occur. Each simulation yields a score Si
573	ranging from 0-100 that assesses the level of success of the LLM in completing the task by the end of the simulation.
574	Based on the set of scores S = {Si}N
575	i=1 obtained from running N simulations for an instruction, we define three
576	metrics: averaged performance (P), aptitude (A90), and unreliability (U 90
577	10 ):
578	P =

580	X
581	i=1

583	
584	N
585	A90 = percentile90(S)
586	U 90
587	10 = percentile90(S) −percentile10(S).
588	Average performance P is an unbiased estimate of a model’s mean score on an instruction in a given simulation type.
589	Aptitude A90 is an estimate of a model’s 90th percentile score on a given instruction, a best-case metric that estimates
590	scores obtained in the top 10% of simulations conducted. Unreliability is an interpercentile range estimate, between the
591	90th and 10th percentile estimates, measuring the gap between best-case and worst-case simulations, giving a sense of
592	level of degradation that occurs in response quality due to stochasticity in the LLM.
593	Each of the metrics is computed on a per-instruction basis and can be averaged across a corpus of instructions to obtain
594	corpus-level metrics. In the rest of the paper, we refer to reliability and unreliability interchangeably, with reliability
595	defined as R90
596	10 = 100 −U 90
597	10 . We also simplify the notations to A for aptitude and U for unreliability, though the
598	metrics can be generalized to other percentile thresholds (e.g., A80 or U 95
599	5 ).
600	In Appendix E, we go over a concrete example of how an average degradation in performance (P) from 90% to 60%
601	could be due to a loss in aptitude, reliability, or a combination. Finally, Figure 6a visually connects the aptitude and
602	unreliability metrics to score box-plot visualizations. In summary, the height of the upper whisker of the box plot
603	represents aptitude (A), and the distance between the upper and lower whiskers of the plot represents Unreliability (U).
604	5
605	Simulation Scale and Parameters
606	In the main simulation experiment, we leveraged the totality of instructions we sharded across six tasks (a total of
607	600 instructions), and simulated conversations across three types:
608	FULL,
609	CONCAT, and
610	SHARDED. We
611	experimented with 15 LLMs, running N = 10 simulations for each pair of model and simulation type, totaling
612	more than 200,000 simulated conversations. All simulations were conducted with a default temperature of T = 1,
613	however, we conducted a supplementary experiment (Section 7.2) that explores the effect of temperature on aptitude
614	and reliability.

LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
PREPRINT
Table 2 summarizes the results on all instructions for four tasks (Code, Database, Math, Actions) for two tested
models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini). Both RECAP and SNOWBALL demonstrate some level of success, with improvements
over SHARDED simulations, but the performance still lags behind FULL or CONCAT. While RECAP outperforms
SNOWBALL, we note that RECAP is an unrealistic setting because the intervention is conducted on the last turn of the
conversation, which is not known a priori when conversation unfolds with a real user. SNOWBALL gives a sense of
realistic performance gains achievable through user-turn repetition: it can mitigate the FULL-to-SHARDED performance
deterioration by 15-20%. In short, relying on an agent-like framework to process information might be limiting, and we
argue LLMs should natively support multi-turn interaction.
7.2
Implications for LLM Builders
A lot of effort has been put in improving LLM aptitude: demonstrating that LLMs can accomplish tasks of increasing
intellectual complexity, with recent results showing LLMs can compete in mathematics Olympiads, or solve Ph.D.-level
technical questions in a benchmark aptly named Humanity’s Last Exam [62].
In this work, we call on LLM builders to prioritize reliability of the models they build, as our experiments demonstrate
that the randomness involved in generating text with LLMs leads to catastrophic unreliability in all the models we
tested, degrading the quality of responses the average LLM users see.
LLMs are probabilistic systems, with parameters such as temperature that can adjust the degree of randomness that
occurs while generating text. A possible argument is therefore: does setting the temperature to its lowest setting (T = 0)
effectively resolve the reliability concern, as it makes the generation process more (but not entirely) deterministic?

Utterance-level errors did not always affect the validity of the overall simulation. In some cases, we observed that
the user simulator would correct an error in an early turn, subsequently in the conversation, or that an error in answer
extraction on the wrong answer attempt would occur at a turn, but the extraction would be successful later on. In
summary, we empirically find that the simulation environment is largely accurate: though some errors occur, large
drops of performance in the SHARDED setting (beyond 2%) are not due to errors caused by the simulator.

3	PREPRINT
4	Table 2 summarizes the results on all instructions for four tasks (Code, Database, Math, Actions) for two tested
5	models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini). Both RECAP and SNOWBALL demonstrate some level of success, with improvements
6	over SHARDED simulations, but the performance still lags behind FULL or CONCAT. While RECAP outperforms
7	SNOWBALL, we note that RECAP is an unrealistic setting because the intervention is conducted on the last turn of the
8	conversation, which is not known a priori when conversation unfolds with a real user. SNOWBALL gives a sense of
9	realistic performance gains achievable through user-turn repetition: it can mitigate the FULL-to-SHARDED performance
10	deterioration by 15-20%. In short, relying on an agent-like framework to process information might be limiting, and we
11	argue LLMs should natively support multi-turn interaction.
12	7.2
13	Implications for LLM Builders
14	A lot of effort has been put in improving LLM aptitude: demonstrating that LLMs can accomplish tasks of increasing
15	intellectual complexity, with recent results showing LLMs can compete in mathematics Olympiads, or solve Ph.D.-level
16	technical questions in a benchmark aptly named Humanity’s Last Exam [62].
17	In this work, we call on LLM builders to prioritize reliability of the models they build, as our experiments demonstrate
18	that the randomness involved in generating text with LLMs leads to catastrophic unreliability in all the models we
19	tested, degrading the quality of responses the average LLM users see.
20	LLMs are probabilistic systems, with parameters such as temperature that can adjust the degree of randomness that
21	occurs while generating text. A possible argument is therefore: does setting the temperature to its lowest setting (T = 0)
22	effectively resolve the reliability concern, as it makes the generation process more (but not entirely) deterministic?
23	To evaluate this argument, we conducted a supplementary experiment in which the assistant’s temperature for generating
24	responses (AT) was varied to three values: 1.0, 0.5, and 0.0. Additionally, since SHARDED simulation uses an LLM-
25	based user simulator, we also varied the user’s temperature (UT) with the same three values. Further details on the
26	experiment, including sample selection and simulation scale, are in Appendix L.

78	Table 3 summarizes the experimental findings. Looking at the FULL
79	and CONCAT settings (first two rows), both GPT-4o-mini and GPT-
80	4o observe a large improvement in reliability when temperature is
81	decreased, with a drop in unreliability (U 90
82	10 ) of 50-80% when the
83	assistant temperature decreases. Results from SHARDED simula-
84	tions are more alarming: GPT-4o-mini does not see improvements
85	in reliability as AT is decreased (in all user-temperature settings), and
86	GPT-4o only sees minor improvements, on the order of 15-20%. Even
87	when both the user and assistant temperatures are set to 0.0, there
88	remains a large unreliability of around 30%. Even though language
89	models are supposed to be deterministic at T = 0.0, this is known
90	to practically not be the case for modern LLMs (see Appendix N for
91	discussion). At a high level, single-turn conversations have limited
92	scope for deviation, whereas one token difference in an early turn of a multi-turn conversation can lead to cascading
93	deviations, which we observe as stagnated unreliability. For settings that involve multi-turn interaction, we find that
94	lowering the temperature of the LLM when generating responses is ineffective in improving system reliability.
95	We invite and challenge LLM builders to jointly optimize model aptitude and reliability. A reliable LLM should: (1)
96	achieve similar aptitude in single- and multi-turn settings, (2) have small unreliability (U 90
97	10 < 15) in multi-turn settings,
98	(3) achieve these at unmodified temperature (T = 1.0), demonstrating that the underlying language model can handle
99	variations that naturally occur in language generation.
100	7.3
101	Implications for NLP Practitioners
102	Our experiments demonstrate that model behavior in single- and multi-turn settings on the same underlying set of
103	instructions can diverge in important ways, for example, with large observed degradations in performance and reliability.
104	We selected the initial six tasks to span a wide range of generation tasks, from programming to multi-document
105	summarization. Yet this set of tasks is limited across multiple dimensions, such as focusing on English-language
106	instructions and analytical (i.e., non-creative) tasks. We put effort into making the sharding process scalable by
107	automating portions that could be handled by an LLM, while manually validating and finalizing samples for quality
108	control. The sharding process – detailed in Appendix C – required an average of three hours of manual work (prompt
109	engineering or inspection) from an author to prepare and finalize 100 sharded instructions.
110	11
111	
112	--- Page 12 ---
113	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
114	PREPRINT
115	We encourage NLP practitioners to experiment with sharding and release sharded versions of their tasks and instructions
116	alongside fully specified ones.
117	Translation

128	Performance on the
129	translation task for
130	FULL,
131	CONCAT, and
132	SHARDED
133	simulations.
134	To illustrate the feasibility of sharding new tasks, and understand compatibility
135	requirements for sharding, we prepared sharded instructions for a seventh task:
136	Translation. The task consists of translating an entire document (10 sentences) from
137	German to English, leveraging paired documents from WMT 2019 on document-
138	level translation [70]. In the SHARDED setting, each turn reveals two additional
139	sentences from the source document and requires the assistant to translate all
140	sentences provided so far, whereas the FULL and CONCAT settings reveal the entire
141	document in the first turn. Evaluation is conducted with the standard BLEU metric
142	[58]. We describe practical implementation details in Appendix I.
143	Results from FULL, CONCAT, and SHARDED simulations are summarized in Table 4.
144	Both models we tested – GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o – do not exhibit degradation in performance in the SHARDED setting,
145	with BLEU scores being within 10% difference of each other in all settings. We believe this result reflects that the task
146	can largely be accomplished at the sentence-level despite some prior work has framed translation at the document-level
147	[64], and that the BLEU score does not adequately capture document-level nuances [52]. In other words, if a task is
148	episodic (i.e., it can be decomposed into turn-level subtasks), the models can avoid getting lost in conversation by
149	completing each subtask without having to handle multi-turn context. In short, the SHARDED Translation task simulates
150	multi-turn conversations that are not underspecified.
151	We now list task properties we believe are important in leading models to get lost in conversation in multi-turn settings.
152	First, generative tasks (i.e., unlike extractive QA or classification) are more prone to model confusion, as they typically
153	involve editing and refinement of new content. Second, the generative tasks should be sufficiently complex, involving
154	multiple explicit specifications that will yield a multitude of shards. For example, an instruction: “Write a Python
155	program that calculates 1 + 1” is too simple to shard. Third, the solution or answer should be non-decomposable, such
156	that revealing a shard modifies the entire solution (unlike the translation task, where each additional shard only asks
157	to translate and append to the ongoing solution). We hypothesize that LLMs tested on tasks with the aforementioned
158	three properties will likely get lost in conversation, evidenced by a large drop in averaged performance and reliability in
159	SHARDED simulations.
160	7.4
161	Implications for Users of Conversational Systems
162	Users of LLM-based products should be aware of the lack of reliability of LLMs, particularly when used in multi-turn
163	settings. Generally available generative technology is new, and prior work has identified the randomness in LLM-
164	generated text as a point of confusion for users [55, 81, 77, 43]. We make two practical recommendations that can help
165	users of LLM-based systems get the most out of their exchanges.
166	If time allows, try again.
167	If a conversation with an LLM did not lead to expected outcomes, starting a new conversation
168	that repeats the same information might yield significantly better outcomes than continuing an ongoing conversation.
169	This is because current LLMs can get lost in the conversation, and our experiments show that persisting in a conversation
170	with the model is ineffective. In addition, since LLMs generate text with randomness, a new conversation may lead to
171	improved outcomes.
172	Consolidate before retrying.
173	Since LLMs are ineffective at dealing with information dispersed across multiple turns,
174	consolidating instruction requirements into a single instruction is an effective strategy to improve the model’s aptitude
175	and reliability (as shown by the CONCAT experiments). When a user notices that a model is lost in conversation, they
176	can ask the LLM: “Please consolidate everything I’ve told you so far,” then bring the response to a new conversation,
177	alleviating the need for manual consolidation. In practice, there is anecdotal evidence that early adopters of LLM-based
178	applications are aware that LLMs get lost in conversation. For example, users of the Cursor LLM-based coding
179	environment report that frequently creating new conversations “whenever they can” is a recommended strategy to
180	ensure high quality responses even though the tool allows to keep conversations going indefinitely.3
181	These two recommendations remain cumbersome for users and can only offer patched solutions rather than a principled
182	approach. Once future LLMs can more reliably handle multi-turn conversations, the need for such recommendations
183	should be alleviated, allowing users to communicate underspecified instructions over multiple turns naturally with less
184	risk of the model getting lost in conversation.
185	3https://www.reddit.com/r/cursor/comments/1j72r8d/when_to_start_a_new_chat/
186	12
187	
188	--- Page 13 ---
189	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
190	PREPRINT
191	8
192	Conclusion
193	In this work, we conduct a large-scale simulation of single- and multi-turn conversations with LLMs, and find that on a
194	fixed set of tasks, LLM performance degrades significantly in multi-turn, underspecified settings. LLMs get lost in
195	conversation, which materializes as a significant decrease in reliability as models struggle to maintain context across
196	turns, make premature assumptions, and over-rely on their previous responses. Additional experiments reveal that
197	known remediations that work for simpler settings (such as agent-like concatenation or decreasing temperature during
198	generation) are ineffective in multi-turn settings, and we call on LLM builders to prioritize the reliability of models in
199	multi-turn settings.
200	9
201	Limitations
202	A first limitation of our work is the reliance on fully automated simulation. By relying on an LLM to simulate user
203	utterances, we can scale our experiments, including running the same simulation multiple times, which would be
204	cost-prohibitive with real users. However, the simulations we obtain are not representative of natural human-AI
205	conversation. The properties of the sharding process (defined in Appendix C) and of the simulation environment
206	(see Section 3.2) ensure that the simulated conversations follow a rather narrow structure, likely not modeling the
207	full range of conversation dynamics that occur with a large, diverse user population. For example, the simulation
208	process ensures a new shard of information is revealed at each turn, and that the last turn of the conversation has
209	specified all the information needed to complete the task which might not happen with real users. Properties P1, P2,
210	and P5 of the sharding process also restrict the scope of the conversation, as sharded instructions closely match an
211	existing fully-specified instruction, with the high-level intent always identified in the conversation’s first turn. The
212	minimal nature of shards is also unrealistic and potentially adversarial, though the gradual sharding experiment finds
213	that different levels of shard granularity lead to similar performance degradations, as soon as conversations occur
214	over two turns or more. Apart from sharding granularity, automatic simulation also lacks the nuance that can occur
215	when a human is involved in conversation, from misunderstandings over terminology, giving up due to frustration with
216	system failures [82], or the lack of a feasible end goal for certain conversations (e.g., the user wanting a solution to an
217	unsolved problem). Because of these factors, we believe conducted simulations represent a benign testing ground for
218	LLM multi-turn capabilities. Because of the overly simplified conditions of simulation, we believe the degradation
219	observed in experiments is most likely an underestimate of LLM unreliability, and how frequently LLMs get lost
220	in conversation in real-world settings. The experiments serve as a scalable, low-cost experimental environment for
221	studying LLMs in multi-turn settings.
222	A second limitation of our work is the focus on analytical tasks. Although we selected a diverse set of both programming
223	and natural language tasks, we restricted experiments to tasks that involve an analytical solution. This restriction limits
224	the scope of our findings, as we do not establish whether models get lost in conversation on more open-ended tasks,
225	such as creative writing [5]. This was a conscious choice: though there has been some progress on creative writing
226	evaluation, it is still an active area of research [6], and we relied on more established tasks and metrics for the initial set
227	of experiments. Determining whether degradation occurs – and if so, identifying the magnitude – on creative tasks is an
228	important direction for future work.
229	A third limitation of the work is the focus on text-only tasks in the English language. Establishing whether models get
230	lost in conversation in other languages, or in tasks that involve multiple modalities in either user or assistant utterances,
231	could help establish the scope of the degradation observed in LLM multi-turn capabilities.

476	Appendices
477	Appendix A
478	Related work on Underspecification
479	The Background (Section 2) reviews the most directly related prior work, focused on multi-turn evaluation. We now
480	cover other related prior works that have studied underspecification.
481	Prior work on communication and linguistics has identified underspecification as a common feature of human language
482	[41, 20, 22, 61].
483	Understanding how LLMs handle underspecified instructions is crucial towards improving conversational capabilities.
484	To this end, Herlihy et al. [27] identified common response patterns such as hedging, refusal, clarification, and
485	interrogation when underspecified queries are presented to conversational LLM systems, and proposed mechanisms
486	to recover from them. Malaviya et al. [53] highlighted the importance of supporting context for more accurate and
487	principled evaluation of LLM responses on underspecified queries, and Sarkar et al. [69] showed that a system that
488	proactively rewrites user instructions to account for underspecification leads to improved LLM response. Shaikh et al.
489	[71] studied the degree of grounding (i.e., clarifications and follow-up questions) that LLMs perform in conversation
490	logs and observed that they significantly lack in generating follow-up questions, where humans are 15 times more likely
491	to do so. Chang et al. [7] hired annotators to manually reproduce fully-specified instructions through a chat interface,
492	and found that the users reveal the entirety of the instruction in 34% of the time, leaving some detail underspecified a
493	majority of the time.
494	Several works have explored direct tasks to evaluate model ability when dealing with underspecification. Liu et al. [49]
495	introduced AmbiEnt, a natural language inference benchmark, which revealed that understanding ambiguous statements
496	is still a challenge even to the state-of-the-art LLMs. Wildenburg et al. [83] created the DUST task, which requires the
497	language model to determine the relative levels of specifications between two sentences, finding that when interpreting
498	underspecified sentences, LMs exhibit little uncertainty. Vijayvargiya et al. [78] evaluated LLM agents for GitHub issue
499	resolution in an underspecified setting, showing that follow-up interactions to supplement information helps improve
500	the resolve rate but detecting the ambiguities in the instructions remains a challenge.
501	Prior work has classified different root causes for underspecification. First, task underspecification occurs when humans
502	provide incomplete descriptions of the task at hand, which is prominent in “specification-heavy tasks” [60]. Second,
503	intent misalignment occur when the AI fails to understand the user’s intent or motivation, and is one of the common
504	sources of user dissatisfaction [34, 76]. Finally, Chaturvedi et al. [9] discuss location and and reference ambiguity, in
505	emboddied settings that involve physical spaces such as a Minecraft game.
506	Appendix B
507	Precise Definition of Sharded Instructions
508	Section 3.1 introduces the concept of sharding at a high level. This Appendix offers a more precise definition by first
509	defining mathematical terminology, and then defining properties that a sharded instruction must satisfy to be considered
510	valid.
511	Let q refer to a single-turn complex query with intended (i.e., correct) output Y ∗
512	q . We refer to the atomic content units
513	(ACU) [51] of the query as
514	I(q) = [I, (c1, · · · , cm)]
515	where I is the primary intent of the query and (c1, · · · , cm) are the sufficient set of clarifications that specify details of
516	how to compute Y ∗
517	q conditioned on I. For I(q) to be considered atomic, any rephrasing of I(q) should produce the
518	same target output. Ie. for all q′ s.t. I(q′) = I(q), then Y ′∗
519	q
520	= Y ∗
521	q .
522	Given the above definition, the aim of the sharding process, for a given query q, is to identify the atomic content units
523	I(q) and construct a set of shorter instruction shards s:
524	q′ = [s1, · · · sk] s.t. I(q) = I(q′)
525	where the shards sj can be used to simulate multi-turn conversation, with the same intended output as q.
526	A sharded instruction q′ is considered valid for an original query q if it fulfills the following properties:
527	P1: Information Preservation.
528	I(q) = I(q′) No information from the original instruction necessary for the
529	completion of the instruction should be lost during the sharding process.
530	P2: Clear Initial Intent.
531	Iq = Iq′ and s1 = Iq. The first shard plays a distinctive role of being the initial query
532	within the shard set. The initial query defines the high-level objective for the entire conversation. (e.g., “write a Python
533	function”).
