587	Figure 7: Process diagram of the four-step semi-automatic process to transform fully-specified instructions into a
588	sharded instruction. The first three steps (segmentation, rephrasing, verification) are automated, while the fourth (inspect
589	and edit) was manually completed by the authors of the work. The last row represents the rejection criteria for a sample.
590	P3: Order Insensitive.
591	Apart from the first shard, the other shards should be decontextualized [13] and not refer to
592	each other in a way that implies an order. As a result, the shard set presented in any order reveals equivalent information.
593	Let ρ(s2..k) refer to a permutation of the shard ordering, then I(q) = I(˜q) ∀˜q = [s1, ρ(s2..k)]
594	P4: Maximal Sharding.
595	The sharding process should strive to maximize the number of shards extracted from the
596	original instruction (maximize k). This can be achieved by producing shards that introduce a single, specific piece of
597	information.
598	P5: Minimal Transformation.
599	The sharded instruction should maintain the instruction language and avoid simpli-
600	fying, altering, or interpreting elements of the original instruction as much as possible. Apart from modifications to
601	satisfy properties P1-P4, the sharding process should attempt to limit modifications such that the shards ([s1, · · · sk] are
602	semantically similar to the atomic content units I(q).
603	Appendix C
604	Semi-Automatic Sharding Process
605	We rely on a semi-automatic process to transform fully-specified instructions into their sharded equivalents. The process
606	– illustrated in Figure 7 – consists of a sequence of three automated steps (Segmentation, Rephrasing, Verification)
607	followed by a manual step that was conducted by an author of the paper.
608	We now detail each step of the process, then go over task-specific details we implemented as needed. We note that as
609	part of our open-source release, we provide all the prompts used in the first three LLM-based steps.
610	Step 1: Segmentation
611	Given an original fully-specified instruction (left-most column in Figure 7), the LLM is
612	prompted to extract segments of the instructions. Segments are intended to correspond to the atomic content units
613	(defined in Appendix B). In particular, the prompt indicates that segments must not overlap, and that not all words in
614	the original instruction must belong to a segment. Prompts are task-specific and incorporate at least three few-shot
615	examples of segmentation, to allow for the concept of segmentation to be illustrated through examples. At this stage,
616	any instruction that yields fewer than three segments are filtered out and does not proceed to the next stage.
617	Step 2: Rephrasing
618	Given the original fully-specified instruction and the extracted segments, this stage consists in
619	rewriting each segment to be decontextualized [13] and conversational. In other words, dependencies between segments
620	are resolved, and the ordering is changed such that obtained shards adhere to properties P2 and P5. In the example
621	above, the fourth segment (highlighted in orange) becomes the first shard as it reveals the overall intent, and light
622	rephrasing occur in other shards. The rephrasing prompt is task-specific, and includes few-shot examples of rephrasing
623	segmented instructions.
624	Step 3: Verification
625	Steps 1-2 produce a sharded instruction that can be used to simulate SHARDED and CONCAT
626	conversations. To verify the property P1 (Information Preservation) that no information has been lost during segmenta-
627	tion and rephrasing, we conduct preliminary simulations to evaluate the original and sharded instruction side-by-side.
628	Specifically for each pair of the original and the sharded instruction, we simulate ten FULL conversations with the
629	original instruction, ten CONCAT conversations with the sharded instruction (by concatenating the shards), and ten
630	19
631	
632	--- Page 20 ---
633	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
634	PREPRINT
635	SHUFFLE-CONCAT conversations. SHUFFLE-CONCAT is a variant of the CONCAT simulation in which all shards
636	(except Shard 1) are randomly permuted before being concatenated. This variant can be seen as a more adversarial
637	version of CONCAT, verifying the property P3 (Order Insensitive). For each simulation type, we calculate the averaged
638	performance P over ten runs and filter out instructions that are below an acceptable degradation threshold. Specifically,
639	instructions are acceptable if the following conditions are met:
640	P CONCAT ≥0.8 P FULL
641	P SHUFFLE-CONCAT ≥0.8 P FULL,
642	where P X denotes the averaged performance of the simulation type X. If more degradation is observed (i.e., below
643	80%), it indicates a potential loss of information during sharding, or that decontextualization was not implemented
644	accurately.
645	Step 4: Inspect and Edit
646	Even though the first three steps define the sharding process and implement some level of
647	quality assurance, they do not guarantee the level of quality required for precise and large-scale experiments due to
648	relying on LLM outputs. To obtain high-quality shards, we reserve step 4 for manual inspection and validation. To
649	facilitate the procedure, we developed a web-based annotation interface. In the interface, an annotator can review a pair
650	of fully-specified and sharded instructions, edit, add, or remove individual shards, and decide to accept or reject sharded
651	instructions. Sharded instructions included in our experiments were all manually reviewed by two authors of the work.
652	The amount of editing and filtering required in this final stage varied by task.
653	Inspecting and editing an auto-generated instruction typically requires 1-3 minutes per instruction, an order of magnitude
654	less than it would require for authors to write the sharded instructions de-novo from a given fully-specified instruction.
655	As part of our open-source release, we provide all the prompts used during sharding, which we hope can facilitate the
656	sharding of additional tasks.

699	The sharding simulation environment (described in Sec-
700	tion 3) relies on LLM components to simulate the user,
701	classify assistant responses, and extract answers from
702	free-text responses. LLM-based components are likely
703	to fail, and we performed an inspection of 200 simulated
704	SHARDED conversations to understand the level of sim-
705	ulation error and the potential effect on estimating the
706	performance of the assistant LLMs due to the error.
707	For each inspected conversation, we annotated user turns,
708	assistant turns, and the overall conversation with five
709	specific elements.
710	For user utterances, we annotated whether the utterance revealed exactly the information from one shard in the sharded
711	instruction (Shard Fully Revealed). Specifically, we flagged turns that revealed more than one shard, and turns that
712	revealed a shard only partially. We also annotated each user’s turn for whether it is appropriately contextualized in
713	the conversation (Shard Contextualized). For example, if the previous assistant’s turn asked a binary clarification
714	question (yes/no), then proper contextualization would require a Yes/No response to directly address the assistant’s
715	response.
716	For assistant utterances, we annotated whether the classified strategy was accurate (Strategy Accuracy). For example,
717	if the response is labeled as a clarification, we confirm if it poses a clarification question to the user. When assistant
718	utterances were labeled as answer attempts, we further labeled whether the answer extraction step was successful
719	(Extraction Success).
720	Upon completing the inspection of each user and assistance utterance, we assigned a global label to the entire
721	conversation on whether or not the errors that occurred during simulation (if any) affected the overall validity of the
722	simulation. If not, the simulation was marked as successful (Overall Success).
723	We inspected conversations for four tasks: Actions, Code, Math and Database. The other two (Summary and Data-to-
724	text) are refining tasks that require an answer attempt at each turn, and do not rely on an LLM-based user simulator. As
725	such, they have limited scope for simulation error.
726	Table 5 summarizes the results of the inspection annotation. Overall, the simulation environment is highly reliable,
727	with roughly 98% of inspected conversations labeled as successful. Some errors occur in each component. With user

167	Appendix F
168	Qualitative Analyses of Simulation Logs
169	In the following subsections, we report qualitative analyses on the corpus of simulations from the main experiment
170	(Section 6.1). The purpose of the analyses is to discern root causes in model behavior that lead to performance
171	degradation. We identify four behaviors below and provide the analysis for each item in the rest of the section:
172	1. LLMs attempt to answer the entire problem prematurely.
173	2. LLMs overly rely on previous (incorrect) answer attempts, leading to lengthier “bloated” answers.
174	3. LLMs overly adjust their answers based on the last conversation turn, materialized by a pronounced forgetting
175	of middle-turns.
176	4. LLMs produce answers that are overly verbose, which likely introduce problem assumptions that detract
177	attention from user-utterances.

291	Table 6: Averaged performance (P) breakdown,
292	based on how early in the conversation the LLM
293	makes its first answer attempt. Analysis con-
294	ducted on simulations of two tasks: Code and
295	Math.
296	During SHARDED simulation, responses are classified according to
297	a seven-class conversation strategy categorization. In particular, each
298	assistant response is tagged as being a formal answer attempt or
299	not (as answer attempts require further processing: extraction and
300	evaluation by the task-specific evaluator).
301	On the onset of conversation, LLMs have the least amount of in-
302	formation (highest level of underspecification), and are least likely
303	to formulate correct answer attempts. Proposing a solution early
304	might therefore plant certain incorrect elements in it, which wrongly
305	influences the interaction later in the conversation.
306	To evaluate this hypothesis, we bin all simulated conversations from
307	our experiments based on how early in the conversation the first
308	answer attempt is generated by the LLM. Specifically, we create five
309	bins: 0-20% if the first answer attempt occurs within the first 20%
310	turns of the conversation, and 20-40%, 40-60%, 60-80%, and 80-100%
311	if it occurs in later turns of the conversation. Of the six tasks included
312	in our experiments, only two (Math and Code) observed a significant
313	range in LLM behavior for answer attempt timing. For the other four
314	tasks, models attempt an answer from the first turn in most of the
315	time, rendering analysis on this parameter impossible.
316	Analysis results for the two remaining tasks are presented in Table 6. We observe that for every single model,
317	conversations with a later first answer attempt lead to higher averaged performance. Across all models, conversations
318	with the first attempt being made in the first 20% of conversations achieve a score of 30.9, less than half of the 64.4
319	when the LLM waits for the last 20% of the conversation to make an answer attempt.
320	In other words, we find that premature answer attempts detract LLM performance. Conversations where the model
321	clarifies user instructions or discusses the problem at a high-level before moving to generating complete answer attempts
322	lead to higher performance. We hypothesize that this is due to the model making incorrect assumptions in premature
323	solutions, which conflict with subsequent user instructions in later turns.

390	Summary
391	Figure 9: Average length (in number of characters) of answer attempts across four tasks (Code, Database, Data-to-text,
392	and Summary) in SHARDED conversations. Answer attempts in the FULL and CONCAT settings tend to be shorter on
393	average than those from SHARDED setting. SHARDED answer attempts increase in length as the LLMs make more
394	answer attempts.
395	In multi-turn conversation simulations, the LLM might make multiple answer attempts, with each subsequent attempt
396	being potentially based on previous attempts. In contrast, single-turn conversations constrain conversation dynamics,
397	with the LLM making a single, first-and-final answer attempt.
398	To understand multi-turn conversation dynamics, we calculate the average length of answer attempts in each simulation
399	type. For the SHARDED setting, we calculate average length for each attempt within a simulation (i.e., average length
400	of the first attempt, second attempt, third attempt, etc.). We note for readers here that the analysis is conducted on
401	extracted answer attempts (output of the Answer Extractor module in Figure 3) rather than the entire assistant responses.
402	The extracted answer more accurately measures dynamics in answer attempts (i.e., generated SQL query, or Python
403	function) rather than the entire responses, which might contain varying amounts of unrelated content.
404	Results of the analysis are plotted in Figure 9. Across the four tasks, we find that answer lengths in the FULL and
405	CONCAT settings tend to be similar, typically within 2-10% of each other. On three of the analyzed tasks (Code,
406	Database, Summary), the first answer attempt in the SHARDED setting has a similar length to FULL and CONCAT
407	counterparts, yet for each subsequent answer attempt, we observe an increase in average answer length. The effect
408	is such that the final answer attempts in SHARDED conversations (right portion of the four plots) tend to be 20-300%
409	longer than the solutions generated in the FULL and CONCAT settings. We name this observation the answer bloat
410	effect: as a multi-turn conversation progresses, the LLM generates incorrect answer attempts, making assumptions
411	about portions of the instruction that remain unspecified. As the user reveals additional information in succeeding turns,
412	the LLM does not successfully invalidate its prior assumptions and overly relies on its previous attempts. Answer bloat
413	in multi-turn, underspecified conversation leads to longer solutions compared to single-turn equivalents.
414	We perform an additional analysis, focusing only on the Code and Database tasks and filtering to simulations where the
415	LLM reaches an entirely correct solution (score of 100.0). For Code task, correct programs obtained from SHARDED
416	setting are on average 850 characters long, which is 27% more characters than the correct solutions generated in the
417	FULL setting (668 characters on average). For Database, correct SQL queries in the SHARDED setting are on average
418	129 characters, 14% more characters than those from the FULL setting (113 characters). In summary, LLMs are less
419	likely to reach a correct solution in multi-turn settings (lower P), and when they do, the final solutions they reach are
420	longer (bloated), hinting that the solutions are qualitatively worse.
421	F.3
422	Over-adjust based on Last Turn of Conversation
423	Because the summary task requires the assistant to attribute its summary back to documents through citation, the task
424	offers a unique opportunity to analyze what turns of information LLMs pay attention to as the multi-turn conversation
425	progresses. As a reminder, the summary task involves a user introducing new documents at each turn. The focus of
426	our analysis is therefore to understand whether document introduction order (across turns) affects the likelihood of the
427	LLM citing a document.
428	In Figure 10, we plot the the results of our analysis. Each row corresponds to the analysis of summaries gen-
429	erated at a given turn in the sharded simulation. At turn 1 (top row), 96% of the cited documents were intro-
430	duced in the first turn. The missing 4% correspond to hallucinated citation to documents that were not intro-
431	duced, and explains why none of the rows’ distribution sum to 100%. At turn two (second row from the top),
432	summaries include citation in roughly equal proportion for turn-1 and turn-2 documents (i.e., 48% and 49%).

493	summaries generated by LLMs with the
494	SHARDED simulation. At each turn, the
495	LLM generates an updated summary (y-
496	axis), which includes citations from the
497	documents that have been revealed up to
498	this turn. Percentages in a row do not add
499	up to 100% due to citation hallucinations
500	that occur for some models.
501	We interpret this to mean that in 2-turn conversations, LLMs pay roughly
502	equal attention to documents in either turn. Analysis of summaries gener-
503	ated in turns 3-8 of sharded simulations reveal an imbalance in the docu-
504	ments the LLM cites to. In eighth-turn summaries, 20% of citations are to
505	documents introduced in turn 8, compared to 8% from turn 2 and 3 (150%
506	difference). At a high-level, as the conversation progresses, LLMs are most
507	likely to cite either documents in the first or last turns, and less likely to
508	cite documents introduced in intermediary (middle) turns. This finding
509	mirrors findings of a loss-in-the-middle phenomena of LLMs paying more
510	attention to documents at the start or end of their provided context, at the
511	cost of middle-context content [29, 50, 40]. In short, we observe that the
512	lost-in-the-middle phenomena occurs not only in single-turn long-context
513	settings, but also in multi-turn conversation. We name this phenomenon
514	loss-in-middle-turns.
515	We note that the analysis presented in Figure 10 averages numbers across
516	the 15 LLMs included in our main experiment. Even though we observe
517	some loss-in-middle-turns in all models, the magnitude of the effect varies
518	across models, typically with more performant models having a more muted
519	effect, showing they have better capabilities of handling attribution across
520	multiple turns of context. We do not include model-specific analyses in this
521	work and leave it for future work.

580	Table 7: Averaged performance (P) of LLMs on the six
581	experimental tasks, arranged based on model relative ver-
582	bosity (length of response). Performance degrades when
583	models generate longer responses on five of the six tasks.
584	When simulating multiple conversations based on a com-
585	mon instruction, we observe variation in responses, par-
586	ticularly in the length of the response generated by the
587	LLM. To understand how verbosity (length of a response)
588	affects model performance, we perform a verbosity anal-
589	ysis.
590	One difficulty with assessing verbosity is that different
591	tasks and instructions might require different levels of
592	verbosity. For example, generating a Python function
593	likely requires a longer than generating an SQL query. In
594	order to regularize for task-specific variations, we assign
595	a verbosity tag calculated for each (LLM, instruction)
596	tuple. For each simulated sharded conversation involving
597	an LLM on an instruction, we calculate the average length
598	of the per-turn response (number of total characters in
599	assistant responses divided by number of turns). We then
600	bin conversations into quintiles according to this metric.
601	More specifically, since we simulated N = 10 conversations for each (model, instruction) pair, we assign 2 simulations
602	per quintile, which we name: shortest, short, median, long, and longest. We then calculate averaged performance (P)
603	on the six experimental tasks, arranged based on this verbosity tag. Results are summarized in Table 7.
604	On five of the six tasks, performance is 10-50% higher in simulated conversations with shortest response length,
605	compared to conversations with longest response length. As assistant responses get longer (left to right in the Table),
606	performances gradually drop. The Actions task is the only task where such an effect is not observed, and where shortest
607	response length from the assistant is detrimental.
608	Predominantly however, models achieve higher performance when they generate shorter responses. We hypothesize
609	that deterioration due to over-verbosity is due to longer responses typically containing more assumptions or hypotheses
610	from the assistant, which can lead to confusion in following turns. On the other hand, short turns tend to be focused
611	(e.g, a single clarification question), and keep the conversation on track.
612	Deterioration due to over-verbosity is note-worthy, as besides deteriorating underlying model performance, longer
613	responses also take longer for users to read, which is undesirable. The finding therefore indicates that longer LLM
614	responses are bad both for models and end-users.

664	Appendix G
665	Assistant Response Categorization
666	We categorize each assistant response into one of the seven categories to capture the answer attempt and evaluate if
667	that is the case, as well as to understand the model behavior tendency. Herlihy et al. [27] defined seven turn categories
668	for LLM responses and classified them using LLM, uncovering that GPT-4 prefers answering directly even when
669	the query is underspecified. Motivated by this study, we similarly define seven response categories which we list in
670	Table 8, together with example responses. Key differences are discussion and answer attempt; we observed many
671	responses containing large body of text formulating the question in our preliminary experiments, which led to redefining
672	“Miscellaneous” from [27] into “Discussion” in our experiment. “Direct Response” in [27] corresponds to our “Answer
673	Attempt.”
674	Appendix H
675	Model Access
676	We accessed models that were used in the experiments from various vendors. The short form names we used throughout
677	the paper, the corresponding versions, and the providers are summarized in Table 9. Except for the exploration with
678	various temperatures (Section 7.2), we set the temperature to T = 1.0 and used the default values for the rest of
679	configurable hyperparameters. We set the maximum response length to 1,000 tokens for all models, and did not
680	observe models exceeding this limit frequently when generating responses. For thinking models (o3, Deepseek-R1), we
681	increased the limit to 10,000 tokens to account for the additional test-time compute (thinking tokens).

758	I.1
759	Code
760	The Code instructions are sourced from a combination of HumanEval [10], a dataset of 164 basic Python program-
761	ming problems given the function header and the docstring that specifies the problem, and LiveCodeBench [31], an
762	evolving dataset of Python algorithmic challenges. In particular, we source from the “call-based” problem subset in
763	LiveCodeBench v5, with the difficulty of either “Easy” and “Medium”, to align the solution formats between the two
764	sources.
765	We first sharded all HumanEval problems following the protocol mentioned in Appendix C, obtaining 45 high quality
766	sets of shards that meet the criteria. The rest of the dataset were discarded because of being simplistic, leaving little
767	room to construct sufficient number of shards for a problem. Subsequently, we shuffled and sharded the aforementioned
768	subset from LiveCodeBench until obtaining 100 valid sharded instructions.
769	We follow the original prompts used by the benchmark authors as much as possible for the single-turn (FULL and
770	CONCAT) evaluation. Specifically, FULL prompt from HumanEval includes the function header and the docstring
771	provided as prompt in HumanEval dataset, and FULL & CONCAT from LiveCodeBench includes starter_code
772	consisting of the function signature.
773	Both HumanEval- and LiveCodeBench-derived problems come with test cases which we use to compute the functional
774	accuracy of the answer attempt by the LLMs. We re-use the evaluation codebase maintained by Jain et al. [31], which
775	(1) wraps the candidate function in a test module, (2) execute given the inputs, and (3) checks the equivalence of
776	the output from the expected output, with a default timeout set to prevent the evaluator from getting trapped during
777	evaluation (e.g., brute-force implementation may not pass under the set time budget). In case when multiple code blocks
778	are present in a response, the answer extraction module selects the last function definition in the last markdown code
779	block.
780	I.2
781	Database
782	The Database instructions are sourced from the validation portion of the Spider dataset [86]. We note that though a
783	more recent version of Spider has been released (Spider 2.0 [44]), the instructions in the second iteration are more
784	advanced and represent less typical database use, and we select instructions from the more realistic Spider 1.0.
785	The authors of Spider categorized queries into four levels of difficulty (EASY, MEDIUM, HARD, XHARD), based on
786	the syntax complexity of a reference SQL query. We filtered out queries of EASY complexity, as they tended to yield
787	fewer than three shards when processed. The rest of the 433 natural language queries in Spider were gradually sharded
788	until reaching a total of 107 valid sharded instructions.
789	26
790	
791	--- Page 27 ---
792	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
793	PREPRINT
794	Each original instruction in Spider supplies a database schema, represented in SQL as a series of table schema (i.e.,
795	each define a series of columns including name, type, and optional index). We include the database schema as part
796	of the system message (i.e., prior to the first turn of conversation), and informing the LLM that users will provide
797	natural-language queries that must be answered using a database with the provided schema.
798	Each original instruction in Spider is paired with a reference SQL solution. We follow Zhong et al. [90] for the
799	evaluation methodology. For a given original instruction, the candidate and reference SQL queries are executed on a
800	fixed set of databases, and exact match of the results on all databases is required to mark the candidate as successful
801	(Score = 100). If a discrepancy is observed on any test database, the candidate is incorrect (Score = 0). One limitation of
802	SQL execution is that false positives can occur: two queries can return the same output on a given database, even when
803	they are not semantically equivalent. Zhong et al. [90] found that by evaluating on an increased number of databases,
804	false positives become negligible. Finally, any invalid candidate that does not successfully execute (e.g., syntax error) is
805	considered incorrect (Score = 0).
806	I.3
807	Actions
808	The Actions instructions are sourced from the released test portion of the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3
809	(BFCL) [85]. BFCL V3 consists of three sub-genre of instructions: (1) Parallel, (2) Multiple, and (3) Multiple-Parallel.
810	Initial experimentation with the sub-genres identified Parallel as the most suited for sharding, as Parallel instructions
811	specify multiple subtasks that should be used and combined into a single action that accomplishes the entirety of the
812	instruction. We shuffled all the BFCL V3 Parallel instructions, and sharded gradually until we obtained 105 valid
813	sharded instructions.
814	We note that though a more recent iteration of BFCL includes multi-turn instructions, it differs from sharding experiments
815	as it does not involve underspecification, with each turn having an independent intermediate solution (which we call
816	episodic multi-turn conversations). Our implementation in comparison shards original instructions allowing us to
817	simulate multi-turn underspecified conversations for this task setting. The Background section (Section 2) discusses the
818	relationship between episodic and underspecified multi-turn conversation more in-depth.
819	Each instruction in BFCL comes with tool set documentation, a JSON object that specifies the set of available actions
820	(APIs) for the assistant to complete user instructions. We include the tool set documentation as part of the system
821	message, along with a message indicating that user queries will require the use of the provided tools to be completed.
822	Each instruction in BFCL comes with a reference answer, consisting of the API calls that should be called to accomplish
823	the user instruction. The maintainers of BFCL have released an evaluation toolkit that assesses semantic equivalence
824	between a candidate answer and the reference answer. We leverage the official evaluation toolkit, assigning a score of
825	S=100 for candidate answers that are considered semantically equivalent to the reference answer, and a score of S=0
826	otherwise. When the evaluation toolkit is not able to parse a candidate answer (e.g., a syntax error), the candidate is
827	considered incorrect (S=0).
828	I.4
829	Math
830	The Math instructions are sourced from the “main” portion of the GSM8K dataset [14]. We did not perform a filter
831	on the original 8,700 instructions. We shuffled the instructions and sharded incrementally until we obtained 103 valid
832	sharded instructions. Each GSM8K is paired with a numerical reference answer. We used the official toolkit released
833	alongside GSM8K to standardize numerical answers (i.e., strip formatting, etc.). Standardized candidate numerical
834	answers can then be compared through exact match to the reference answer. If the toolkit detects a match, the candidate
835	answer is considered correct (Score=100), and incorrect otherwise (Score = 0). A short, single-sentence system prompt
836	is used to indicate to the assistant that it will be solving mathematical problems.
837	I.5
838	Data-to-Text
839	The Data-to-Text instructions are based on instructions in the released test set ToTTo dataset [59]. In ToTTo, fully-
840	specified instructions have the following information elements: (1) a HTML-formatted table extracted from a Wikipedia
841	page, (2) a subset of cells in the table that have been highlighted, (3) the name of the Wikipedia page that included
842	the Table, (4) the name of the Section in the Wikipedia page that included the Table. Given these elements, the task
843	objective is to generate a caption for the Table specifically focusing on the highlighted cells and considering the available
844	meta-data. Instructions were shuffled and sharded incrementally until we obtained 120 valid sharded instructions.
845	For each instruction, we generate sharded instructions by assigning different information elements to individual shards.
846	The first shard consists of the initial HTML-formatted table without highlighting. The second shard provides an updated
847	27
848	
849	--- Page 28 ---
850	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
851	PREPRINT
852	table with the highlighting present, the third shard provides the Wikipedia page name, the fourth shard provides the
853	Wikipedia Section name. Finally, a fifth shard provides a fixed set of 10 randomly-selected example captions from the
854	training set of the ToTTo dataset.
855	Each instruction in ToTTo is assigned one to three reference captions that were collected by authors of the original
856	dataset. Evaluation on a candidate caption calculates the BLEU score [58] between the candidate and the set of available
857	references, following the evaluation methodology from the original paper.
858	The Data-to-Text is a refinement task; at each turn, the model is provided an additional shard of information, and is
859	explicitly told to update its response considering all the information provided so far. As a refinement task, assistant
860	responses at each turn are automatically categorized as answer attempts, and the extracted answer is considered to be
861	the entire response. The system instruction informs the model that its response should consist solely of a table caption,
862	without additional text (such as intro, outro, or politeness wording).
863	I.6
864	Summary
865	The Summary instructions are based on samples of the Summary of a Haystack dataset [40]. We reuse the entire
866	instructions from Summary of a Haystack to produce 92 sharded instructions. The original instructions each consist
867	of a haystack – 100 documents for a total of 100,000 tokens of content – and a user query. The goal of the task is to
868	generate a bullet-point-formatted summary of the query-relevant insights that occur in the collection of documents, and
869	use citation to attribute information in each of the bullet points back to the source documents.
870	The original setting of the Summary of a Haystack purposefully includes a large amount of redundancy (each insight
871	is repeated across at least 6 documents) to evaluate LLMs’ ability to thoroughly cite sources. However, we simplify
872	the task for the multi-turn setting, as the 100,000-token haystacks restrict the variety of models we can evaluate. We
873	instead follow subsequent work in selecting smaller Haystacks (“mini-Haystacks”) [3]. Mini-Haystacks consist of
874	20 documents and ensure that each reference insight is repeated across three documents. For each instruction, we
875	produce ten shards by randomly assigning two documents per shard. The initial shard further specifies high-level task
876	instruction, by specifying the user query, the expected bullet-point format, with a formatted citation.
877	Summary of a Haystack relies on an LLM-based metric (Joint Score) to compute the quality of the summary in terms
878	of both the relevance of the candidate bullet points (coverage) and the quality of the generated attribution within the
879	bullet points (citation). The authors note that the metric is recall-based, such that longer summaries are likely to score
880	higher than shorter ones. To account for length bias, the original task instructs models to generate summaries of at
881	most 300 words, which we include in our experiments as well. Specifically, models are instructed in all settings to
882	generate summaries of up to 300 words. We observed that in multi-turn settings, models often forget this instruction,
883	leading to non-adherence to the instruction. To avoid penalizing models that correctly remain within the 300-word
884	limit, we truncate summaries that go beyond the limit, removing words in equal proportion from summary bullet points,
885	such that evaluated summaries all respect the 300-word limit. We note that this tendency for LLMs to go beyond is
886	further discussed in Appendix F, where we observe that across tasks, model answer attempts get “bloated” over turns of
887	conversations. In single-turn settings (full, concat), LLMs largely respect the 300-word length limit.
888	The summary task is a refinement task. Assistant responses at each turn are automatically categorized as answer
889	attempts, and the entire response is considered to be the extracted answer.
890	I.7
891	Translation
892	The Translation instructions were collected from the WMT 2019 task on document-level translation [70]. Specifically,
893	we selected 30 documents German-English pairs. Document pairs are aligned at the sentence level (i.e., English and
894	German documents in a pair have the same number of sentences). We truncated the selected pairs to their first ten
895	sentences, and sharded the document instruction such that each shard would introduce exactly two sentences from
896	the document, for a total of five shards. We provided shards in German, and the task consisted in translating into
897	English (i.e., German→English). Hence, Shard 1 introduces the first two German sentences, Shard 2 introduces German
898	sentences 3-4, etc. In the sharded setting, the task requires the LLM to translate the document with all the provided
899	sentences so far. In the full settings, the LLM is provided the entire document (10 sentences) in the first turn. In the
900	concat setting, the LLM is also provided all sentences in the first turn, but separated into the shards (two sentences at a
901	time).
902	In initial experiments, we experimented with other sharding strategies, including breaking shards at a specific number
903	of words (rather than sentence boundary), and increasing the length of documents (from 10 to 20 sentences), without
904	observing significant differences in results. This led us to adopt the setting we describe: sharding every two sentences,
905	and truncating at 10-sentences.

1005	instruction is 85,000 calories.
1006	We evaluated performance with the BLEU metric[58], the standard metric for translation tasks, which was used as well
1007	in the original WMT 2019 competition.
1008	Appendix J
1009	Example Simulated Conversation
1010	Figure 11 provides an example conversation that was simulated during our experiments in the sharded setting. The
1011	simulation was conducted on the Math task, with a 6-shard instruction, and using the Llama3.1-8B-Instruct as the
1012	assistant. This conversation illustrates the following properties described in the rest of the paper: (1) the LLM makes
1013	assumptions early in the conversation (in Turn 1, describing four pastries that are irrelevant), (2) although it correctly
1014	interprets user-provided information, it also unnecessarily updates the information for assumptions it made (Turn 4),
1015	(3) this leads to unnecessary complexity, and the model ultimately forgets that the initial instruction was to calculate
1016	total calorie count, and returns only half of the calculation (just for Mini Blueberry Muffin). In short, this conversation
1017	illustrates the lost in conversation phenomenon: when the user instruction is underspecified (Turns 1-4), the LLM makes
1018	assumptions that detract from the conversation and lead to incorrect or incomplete answers.
1019	Appendix K
1020	Gradual Sharding Implementation
1021	To evaluate the effect of instruction granularity on performance degradations, we conducted the gradual sharding
1022	experiment.
1023	We selected sharded instructions that had exactly eight shards, leading to a total of eight instructions across three tasks
1024	(Code, Math, Data-to-Text). We then leveraged an LLM (GPT-4o) to expand each instruction into 7 variants with
1025	differing number of shards. The LLM was instructed to merge the original sharded instruction into a smaller sharded
1026	instruction with two to seven shards. The instruction authorized minor rephrasing to allow for individual shards to be
1027	fluent, but encouraged the LLM to remain as close as possible to the original instruction in wording.
1028	As such, each of the original instruction can be paired to: (1) a concat instruction (one-shard), and (2) 7 sharded
1029	instructions, ranging from two to eight shards. Applying this method to the 31 instructions yields a total of 248
1030	instructions, with an equal number for the number of shards (from 1 to 8) and on the identical underlying problems.
1031	We ran simulations using the 248 instructions, simulating 10 conversations per instruction and model for two models:
1032	GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini. Findings of the gradual sharding experiment are described in Section 6.3.
1033	29
1034	
1035	--- Page 30 ---
1036	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
1037	PREPRINT
1038	Appendix L
1039	Temperature Experiment Implementation
1040	To evaluate the effect of temperature on aptitude and reliability of LLMs in single- and multi-turn settings, we conducted
1041	the following temperature experiment.
1042	We selected 10 instructions from each of four tasks: Code, Database, Actions, and Math (for a total of 40). We ran
1043	experiments with two models (GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini). For each instruction and each temperature combination,
1044	we conducted simulations for three conversation settings: full, concat, and sharded. For each conversation setting, we
1045	varied temperature parameters to three values: 0.0, 0.5, and 1.0. For the full and concat setings, this corresponds to
1046	three temperature combinations (as only the assistant temperature can be modified), whereas there are a total of nine
1047	combinations for the sharded setting, as both the assistant and user temperature is varied.
1048	We chose to increase the number of simulations to 20 runs per condition (compared to 10 in the main experiment), as
1049	the focus of the experiment is to measure variations in model aptitude and reliability, and added simulation runs lead to
1050	better percentile estimates used in calculating metrics. This added requirement was not computationally expensive as
1051	the temperature experiment involved a limited number of models (2 vs. 15) and instructions (40 vs. 600) in comparison
1052	to our main experiment.
1053	Findings of the experiments are described in Section 7.2.
1054	Appendix M
1055	Recap & Snowball Experiment Implementation
1056	We leverage SHARDED conversation logs to simulate RECAP setting, since RECAP only differs from SHARDED
1057	in terms of an additional recapitulation turn that gathers all the previous user utterances. This implementation
1058	also allows us to directly compare the effect of the approach against the SHARDED results. Specifically, for each
1059	SHARDED simulation run, we appended the “recap” turn and run the simulation one more turn. Since it requires
1060	stacking the past turns every turn, we simulate the entire conversations from scratch for SNOWBALL simulations.
1061	The prompt concatenates the previous turn user utterances as bullet points, followed by the text for the current turn:
1062	Just to reiterate:\n - [past utterance 1]\n- [past utterance 2]\n\n Also,\n[current utterance]. We
1063	note that what is accumulated for both RECAP and SNOWBALL are verbalized utterances from the user simulator,
1064	not the original shards themselves. For both simulation settings, we run N = 10 simulations on all of the sharded
1065	instructions on four tasks (Code, Database, Math, Actions) and report the mean of averaged performance over the tasks,
1066	which is shown in Table 2.
1067	Appendix N
1068	On obtaining deterministic outputs from LLMs
1069	As we demonstrated in our experimental results, setting the temperatures to zero still leads to high unreliability, due to
1070	compounding effect of subtle non-determinism over tokens and turns.
1071	In theory, greedy decoding (i.e., T = 0) will always pick the argmax over the vocabulary distribution. However, it is
1072	reported that hardware limitations on floating point operations cause slightly different intermediate values, which results
1073	in a ripple effect of larger value changes and therefore different tokens being selected.
1074	Notable model providers acknowledge the non-determinism implicitly or explicitly; Anthropic recommends sampling
1075	multiple times to cross-validate output consistency,4 Google also highlights that their model outputs are mostly
1076	deterministic,5 and OpenAI recommends setting seed parameter to further reduce the non-determinism.6
1077	Nevertheless, we caution users that multi-turn conversations can be increasingly unreliable owing to divergent LLM
1078	responses.
1079	4https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-guardrails/reduce-hallucinations.
1080	5https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/learn/prompts/adjust-parameter-values#temperature.
1081	6https://platform.openai.com/docs/advanced-usage#reproducible-outputs.
1082	30
1083	
1084	--- Page 31 ---
1085	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
1086	PREPRINT
1087	Appendix O
1088	Prompts
1089	O.1
1090	Sharding
1091	We show the prompts for the sharding process below, using Math as an example task. Double-bracketed terms are
1092	placeholders that get replaced with the actual data. Other tasks share the same outline with different exemplars and
1093	rules to enforce stable outputs. We refer the readers to the GitHub repository for the exact prompts on other tasks.
1094	Segmentation
1095	You are a given a fully specified instruction, and your task is to segment the instruction into a
1096	units of information that each reveal a single piece of information of the instruction.
1097	You must output a list of segments in the following JSON format:
1098	[
1099	{"segment": "[exact excerpt from the instruction]"},
1100	{"segment": "[exact excerpt from the instruction]"},
1101	...
1102	]
1103	Rules:
1104	- [Non-overlapping] The segments must be non-overlapping and cover the entire instruction. You can
1105	optionally leave some gaps for non-essential portions of the original instruction (delimiters,
1106	headers, etc.)
1107	- [Minimalistic] You should split the information in the segments to as small as possible. If you
1108	have a compound expression (X and Y), you should split it into two segments. Each segment should
1109	represent a unit of information.
1110	Example Query:
1111	What are the names and locations of the stadiums that had concerts that occurred in both 2014 and
1112	2015?
1113	Output:
1114	{"segments": [
1115	{"segment": "names and locations"},
1116	{"segment": "stadiums"},
1117	{"segment": "concerts"},
1118	{"segment": "in both 2014"},
1119	{"segment": "and 2015"}
1120	]}
1121	Now complete the task for the following fully specified instruction:
1122	[[INSTRUCTION]]
1123	31
1124	
1125	--- Page 32 ---
1126	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
1127	PREPRINT
1128	Rephrasing
1129	You are given segments of a fully specified instruction, and your task is to: (1) choose one that
1130	will be the initial shard of a multi-step query, and then (2) rephrase each segment into a
1131	conversational version that are provided to the system in a follow-up turn of the conversation.
1132	Your output should be a JSON object in the following format:
1133	{
1134	"initial_segment": "[exact excerpt from the instruction]",
1135	"initial_shard": "conversational version of the initial segment",
1136	"shards": [
1137	{"segment": "[exact excerpt from the instruction]", "shard": "conversational version of the
1138	segment taking the rest of the instruction into account"}
1139	]
1140	}
1141	Example:
1142	Full Query:
1143	What are the names and locations of the stadiums that had concerts that occurred in both 2014 and
1144	2015?
1145	Segments:
1146	[
1147	{"segment": "names and locations"},
1148	{"segment": "stadiums"},
1149	{"segment": "concerts"},
1150	{"segment": "in both 2014"},
1151	{"segment": "and 2015"}
1152	]
1153	Output:
1154	{
1155	"initial_segment": "stadiums",
1156	"initial_shard": "popular stadiums",
1157	"shards": [
1158	{"segment": "concerts", "shard": "the stadiums should have concerts during a period"},
1159	{"segment": "in both 2014", "shard": "the concerts should have occurred in 2014 in the
1160	stadiums"},
1161	{"segment": "and 2015", "shard": "the concerts should have also occurred in 2015 in the same
1162	stadiums"},
1163	{"segment": "names and locations", "shard": "for the stadiums, returned both the name and
1164	location"}
1165	]
1166	}
1167	Rules:
1168	- [Transform each segment] Make sure each segment is included either as the initial shard or in the
1169	rest of the shards. Do not forget any segments.
1170	- [Short initial shard] Make the initial shard short, not a full sentence, similar to how users use a
1171	search engine like Google.
1172	- [Order of shards] Order the shards in order of importance, from most to least important to the
1173	initial shard. You do not need to keep the order the segments that are provided in.
1174	Now complete the task for the following fully specified instruction and segments:
1175	Fully Specified Instruction:
1176	[[QUESTION]]

1178	[[SEGMENTS]]
1179	32
1180	
1181	--- Page 33 ---
1182	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
1183	PREPRINT
1184	Verification
1185	You are given an instruction that fully specifies a problem, and a list of shards. Your task is to
1186	decide whether all the information from the full instruction is captured by the shards.
1187	If not, you should output the information unit from the instruction that is not captured by the
1188	shards.
1189	Example 1:

1196	"in both 2014 and 2015", "shard": "the concerts should have occurred in both 2014 and 2015"},
1197	{"segment": "names and locations", "shard": "for the stadiums, returned both the name and
1198	location"}]}
1199	Output:
1200	{"converage": "complete"}
1201	Example 2:
1202	Instruction:
1203	Which Asian countries have a population that is larger than any country in Africa?
1204	Shards:
1205	{"initial_shard": "I'm interested in learning about countries in Asia", "shards": [{"shard":
1206	"consider the population size of these Asian countries"}, {"shard": "the population should be
1207	compared in size"}, {"shard": "specifically, compare to the population of African countries"}]}
1208	Output:
1209	{"coverage": "incomplete", "missing_segment": "the shards do not specify that the population of the
1210	Asian countries should be *larger* than the population of any African countries"}
1211	You must output in JSON format as shown in the examples above.
1212	Now complete the task for the following fully specified instruction and shards:
1213	Instruction:
1214	[[QUERY]]
1215	Shards:
1216	[[SHARDS]]
1217	33
1218	
1219	--- Page 34 ---
1220	LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
1221	PREPRINT
1222	O.2
1223	Experiments
1224	The experiments involve several LLM calls with specific prompts to simulate the conversation, which we list below. We
1225	refer readers to the GitHub repository for how they are incorporated.
1226	User simulator
1227	You are simulating a user of an interactive LLM system (like ChatGPT).
1228	The user is inherently lazy, and answers in short form, providing only minimal information to the
1229	system. You should not be proactive.
1230	Here's the conversation so far:
1231	[[CONVERSATION_SO_FAR]]
1232	Here are the shards that have already been revealed:
1233	[[SHARDS_REVEALED]]
1234	Here are all the shards that have not been revealed yet:
1235	[[SHARDS_NOT_REVEALED]]
1236	You must generate a response to the conversation so far. Here are the rules:
1237	- [Providing a shard] You can reveal the content of a shard to the system in your response if it will
1238	help the system move closer to answering the problem. You should select the shard to reveal that is
1239	most "basic" and currently the most relevant.
1240	- [One Shard at a Time] You should only reveal at most one shard at a time.
1241	- [Reveal Entire Shard] If you reveal a shard, you must make sure to include *all the information in
1242	the shard*. For example, if the shard is "your symptoms are that you have a headache in the
1243	mornings", your response can't just be ``yeah I have headaches'', you must say ``yup mostly headaches
1244	in the mornings``.
1245	- [Irrelevant Clarifications] If the system asks you a question irrelevant to the shards, asks you a
1246	generic question (``Can you give me a hint?``), you should respond with an answer that does not
1247	provide a shard. (``I don't know``, ``Is that really important?``, etc.) You should not reveal any
1248	information beyond what is available in the shards.
1249	- [No Repeated Shards] You should not reveal the same shard more than once. Carefully review the
1250	already revealed shards, and only reveal a shard if its `shard_id` is not on the list.
1251	- [Rephrase Shards] If you reveal a shard, you should rephrase it in a conversational way. Do not
1252	copy the shard verbatim.
1253	- [Do Not Ask Questions] Your response should always be declarative sentences, and not questions.
1254	- [Brevity of Response] You should favor being succint. Your answer can also have typos, improper
1255	grammar, capitalization, etc. You are simulating a real person talking to an AI, who is in a hurry.
1256	- [Format] Your response should be formatted as a JSON object with the following keys:
1257	- `response`: The response to the conversation so far.
1258	- `shard_id`: The shard you are revealing to the system. The shard_id can be an integer, or -1 if
1259	you did not reveal any shards.
1260	For example:
1261	{"response": "I don't know", "shard_id": -1}
1262	or:
1263	{"response": "yeah I want it to [...]", "shard_id": 1}
1264	34

1266	--- Page 35 ---

1269	Response strategy categorization
1270	You are reviewing a multi-turn conversation between a user and an assistant, and are given the last
1271	turn of the conversation.
1272	Here is the full specification of the problem the system is attempting to solve:
1273	[[INITIAL_SHARD]]
1274	Specification:

1276	You must classify the response of the assistant according to the response type:
1277	- `answer_attempt`: The response contains a complete answer attempt to the user's question (not
1278	templated or hypothetical), that can be extracted verbatim. See the task-specific answer description
1279	for more details.
1280	- `clarification`: The response
1281	is short (less than 100 words) and contains a single question
1282	addressed to the user that directly inquires about an aspect of the user's query. A clarification
1283	turn cannot be long (see `discussion`), cannot contain a vague question (see `discussion`) and cannot
1284	contain multiple questions (see `interrogation`).
1285	- `interrogation`: The response contains multiple questions addressed to the user, sometimes
1286	organized in a list or bullet-points.
1287	- `discussion`: The response discusses the question in detail, without providing a final answer,
1288	asking a specific clarification question, or a refusal to answer. The response may or may not contain
1289	a vague question (e.g., “What else can I help you with?”).
1290	- `hedge`: The response contains multiple answer candidates based on hypotheticals (ifs) or branching
1291	(case 1, case 2) with corresponding descriptions.
1292	- `refuse`: The response contains an explicit or implicit refusal to answer the user's question
1293	without a follow-up question or a request.
1294	- `missing`: The response is empty/blank.
1295	You must output your answer in the following JSON format:
1296	{"response_type": "refuse|missing|answer_attempt|hedge|clarification|interrogation|discussion"}

1299	select `answer_attempt` if the conversation could end at this stage with the user having an entirely
1300	final answer to the problem they've formulated.
1301	- [Task Specific Answer] [[ANSWER_DESCRIPTION]]

input.content:
---
id: source-llms-get-lost
date: 2026-05-06
source: raw/papers/llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation.pdf
type: 论文
tags: [LLM评估, 多轮对话, 欠规范, 可靠性, 对话迷失, 生成任务]
---

# LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation

## 一句话总结

通过大规模模拟实验（20万+对话）系统揭示：所有主流 LLM 在多轮欠规范对话中性能平均下降 39%，表现为轻微的能力下降（-15%）和剧烈的可靠性恶化（+112%），且一旦"走错路"就无法自我修正——即"对话迷失"现象。

## 基本信息

| 项目 | 内容 |
|------|------|
| **标题** | LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation |
| **作者** | Philippe Laban (Microsoft Research), Hiroaki Hayashi (Salesforce Research), Yingbo Zhou (Salesforce Research), Jennifer Neville (Microsoft Research) |
| **日期** | 2025-05-09 (arXiv) |
| **arXiv** | 2505.06120v1 |
| **机构** | Microsoft Research, Salesforce Research |
| **代码/数据** | Microsoft/lost_in_conversation, datasets/Microsoft/lost_in_conversation |

## 研究背景与动机

LLM 作为对话界面（如 ChatGPT、Gemini、Claude），承诺帮助用户不仅能在任务完全明确时提供帮助，还能在用户需求不明确时通过多轮对话来探索和细化需求。然而存在一个根本性矛盾：

1. **评估与现实脱节**：对话日志分析已证实用户指令中的欠规范（underspecification）非常普遍，但 LLM 评估几乎全部集中在单轮、完全指定的指令设置上。
2. **情景式评估的局限**：现有的多轮评估（如 MT-bench）大多将对话视为情景式（episodic）——每一轮引入可独立评估的子任务——这偏离了人类对话中常见的欠规范特性。
3. **缺乏系统性比较**：缺乏在相同任务上直接比较单轮与多轮性能的受控实验。

本文的核心理念是：**将欠规范（underspecification）作为评估的核心要素**，而非回避它。

## 核心发现

### 1. 性能大幅下降：平均 -39%

所有 15 个测试的 LLM（从 Llama-3.1-8B 到 Gemini 2.5 Pro）在多轮欠规范对话中均表现显著下降：
- 单轮完全指定平均得分：**90%**
- 多轮欠规范平均得分：**65%**
- 平均下降：**25 个百分点（约 39%）**
- 即使仅两轮对话也能观察到显著下降

### 2. 性能下降的双分量分解

论文提出将性能退化分解为两个正交维度（参见 [[能力-可靠性分解（aptitude-reliability）]]）：

- **能力下降（Aptitude Loss）**：平均下降 **15%**——即最佳情况下的表现也变差了
- **可靠性恶化（Unreliability Increase）**：平均增加 **112%**——即最佳与最差情况之间的差距剧烈扩大

关键观察：在单轮设置中，高能力的模型往往也更可靠（如 GPT-4.1、Gemini 2.5 Pro）；但在多轮设置中，**所有模型（无论能力高低）都表现出极高的不可靠性**。

### 3. "对话迷失"现象

论文最核心的发现——**当 LLM 在多轮对话中走了错误的方向，它们就会迷路且无法恢复**（when LLMs take a wrong turn, they get lost and do not recover）。具体表现为四个行为模式：

1. **过早尝试回答**（Premature Answer Attempts）：LLM 在信息不足时就尝试生成完整解答，做出错误假设。首次回答尝试越早，性能越低——在前 20% 轮次就尝试回答的平均得分仅 30.9，而在最后 20% 轮次才回答的平均得分达 64.4。

2. **答案膨胀**（Answer Bloat）：后续回答尝试的长度逐轮增长，最终答案比单轮等价答案长 20-300%。即便最终答案正确，多轮解答也比单轮解答平均长 14-27%。

3. **过度依赖最后一轮**（Loss-in-Middle-Turns）：在摘要任务中，LLM 更倾向于引用首轮或末轮引入的文档，中间轮次的文档被引用的概率显著降低（比首末轮低 150%）。这是 [[lost-in-the-middle]] 现象在多轮对话中的体现。

4. **过度冗长的回复**（Overly Verbose Responses）：在六个任务中的五个，回复越短性能越高——最短回复组的性能比最长回复组高 10-50%。冗长的回复引入更多假设，分散注意力。

## 实验设计与方法

### 指令分片（Instruction Sharding）

论文的核心方法论创新——参见 [[指令分片（instruction-sharding）]]。将完全指定的指令拆分为一组"分片"（shards），每个分片只引入一条信息。然后在多轮对话中逐轮揭示分片，模拟用户逐步提供信息的场景。

分片指令需满足五个属性：
- P1 信息保持：不丢失原始信息
- P2 明确初始意图：第一分片定义高层目标
- P3 顺序无关：非首分片可任意排列
- P4 最大分片：尽可能细粒度
- P5 最小变换：尽量保持原始用语

### 五种对话模拟类型

| 模拟类型 | 简写 | 说明 |
|----------|------|------|
| Fully-Specified | FULL | 单轮完全指定（基线） |
| Sharded | SHARDED | 多轮欠规范（核心实验） |
| Concatenated | CONCAT | 单轮但用分片拼接（验证基线） |
| Recap | RECAP | SHARDED + 末轮总结全部信息 |
| Snowball | SNOWBALL | 每轮都重复已揭示的所有分片 |

### 六项任务

| 任务 | 领域 | 来源 | 指令数 | 评估方式 |
|------|------|------|--------|----------|
| Code | 编程 | HumanEval + LiveCodeBench | 145 | 功能准确性 |
| Database | 编程 | Spider | 107 | SQL 执行匹配 |
| Actions | 编程 | BFCL V3 | 105 | API 语义等价 |
| Math | 编程 | GSM8K | 103 | 数值精确匹配 |
| Data-to-Text | 自然语言 | ToTTo | 120 | BLEU |
| Summary | 自然语言 | Summary of a Haystack | 92 | 覆盖度+引用准确性 |

### 15 个测试模型

涵盖从小型开源到 SOTA 的完整谱系：Llama-3.1-8B, OLMo2-13B, Phi-4, Claude 3 Haiku, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, o3, Llama-3.3-70B, Llama-4-Scout, Command-A, Deepseek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro。

## 关键实验数据

### 主实验结果（200,000+ 对话）

| 指标 | FULL（单轮） | SHARDED（多轮） | 变化 |
|------|-------------|----------------|------|
| 平均性能 P | ~90% | ~65% | -25pp |
| 能力 A90 | ~92% | ~77% | -15pp |
| 不可靠性 U | ~12% | ~25% | +112% |

### 缓解策略效果

| 策略 | 效果 |
|------|------|
| RECAP（末轮总结） | 部分改善但仍远低于 FULL |
| SNOWBALL（逐轮重复） | 缓解 FULL→SHARDED 退化的 15-20% |
| 降低温度至 T=0 | 单轮改善 50-80%，多轮几乎无效（不可靠性仍约 30%） |

### 翻译任务的反例

翻译任务在 SHARDED 设置下**未观察到性能退化**，因为翻译可以在句子级别独立完成——这验证了论文的假设：**当任务本质上是情景式（可分解为独立子任务）时，模型不会迷失**。导致迷失的三个任务属性：生成性（非抽取式）、足够复杂（多规格）、解答不可分解。

## 影响与启示

### 对 LLM 构建者的呼吁

论文明确呼吁 LLM 开发者在优化能力（aptitude）的同时**优先考虑可靠性（reliability）**，并提出可操作的挑战目标：
1. 单轮和多轮设置下应达到相近能力
2. 多轮设置下不可靠性 U < 15
3. 以上目标应在默认温度 T=1.0 下达成

这对 [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] 等 LLM 记忆机制的设计具有直接启示——需要能够有效管理和整合多轮对话中逐步揭示的信息，避免上下文丢失和错误累积。

### 对用户的建议

1. **如果时间允许，重新开始**：开新对话比继续迷失的对话更有效
2. **重试前先整合**：让 LLM "请整合到目前为止我告诉你的所有内容"，然后把整合结果带入新对话

### 对 NLP 研究者的启示

论文鼓励研究者为自己的任务创建分片版本，以评估模型在多轮欠规范场景下的表现，并提供了可复用的分片工具和流程。

## 推荐参考文献

- Herlihy et al. (2024) — LLM 在欠规范查询下的响应模式分类
- Liu et al. (2024) — "Lost in the Middle"：LLM 对长上下文中间部分的注意力衰减
- Laban et al. (2024) — Summary of a Haystack：长文档摘要评估基准
- Zipf (1949) — "最小努力原则"：人类语言沟通中欠规范的理论基础

## 关联

- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — δ-mem 论文引用本文作为动机文献之一，LLM 在多轮对话中的信息丢失问题正是记忆机制需要解决的核心挑战
- [[对话迷失现象（lost-in-conversation）]] — 本文提出的核心概念
- [[指令分片（instruction-sharding）]] — 本文提出的评估方法
- [[能力-可靠性分解（aptitude-reliability）]] — 本文提出的性能分析框架
- [[lost-in-the-middle]] — 本研究发现其在多轮对话中的延伸表现

input.content:
---
id: concept-lost-in-conversation
date: 2026-05-06
aliases: [对话迷失, lost in conversation, getting lost in conversation]
tags: [概念, LLM评估, 多轮对话]
---

# 对话迷失现象（Lost in Conversation）

## 定义

对话迷失现象是指 LLM 在多轮欠规范对话中，一旦在早期轮次做出错误假设或生成错误解答，就无法自我修正、恢复正确的现象。形象地说：**当 LLM 在对话中走错了路，它们就会迷路且无法找回方向**。

这一概念由 Laban, Hayashi, Zhou & Neville (2025) 在论文 [[llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation|LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation]] 中系统提出。

## 关键属性

- **普遍性**：所有测试的 15 个 LLM（从 Llama-3.1-8B 到 Gemini 2.5 Pro）均表现出此现象，无一例外
- **严重性**：多轮欠规范对话中平均性能下降 39%，其中可靠性恶化 +112%
- **早期性**：即使仅两轮对话即可观察到显著退化
- **不可恢复性**：核心特征——模型无法从错误路径中自我修正

## 四个表现形式

1. **过早回答尝试**（Premature Answer Attempts）
   - LLM 在信息不足时就生成完整解答
   - 越早尝试回答，性能越差（前 20% 轮次回答：30.9 分 vs. 最后 20% 轮次：64.4 分）
   - 过早回答中做出的错误假设会污染后续对话

2. **答案膨胀**（Answer Bloat）
   - 后续回答尝试越来越长（比单轮答案长 20-300%）
   - LLM 不愿抛弃之前的（错误的）假设，而是在其上堆砌
   - 即便最终正确，多轮解答也更冗长（长 14-27%）

3. **中间轮次遗忘**（Loss-in-Middle-Turns）
   - 首轮和末轮信息获得最多注意力，中间轮次被"遗忘"
   - 这是 [[lost-in-the-middle]] 现象在多轮对话中的自然延伸
   - 在摘要引用分析中，中间文档被引用率比首末轮低 150%

4. **过度冗长**（Overly Verbose Responses）
   - 回复越长，性能越差（5/6 个任务验证）
   - 冗长回复引入更多未验证假设，加剧迷失

## 导致迷失的三个任务属性

并非所有多轮任务都会导致迷失。论文发现导致迷失的充分条件：
1. **生成性**（Generative）：需要生成和精炼新内容（非抽取式 QA 或分类）
2. **足够复杂**（Complex）：涉及多个明确的规格说明，可产生多个分片
3. **不可分解**（Non-decomposable）：新信息的揭示需要修改整体解答（非简单追加）

翻译任务满足 1 和 2 但不满足 3（每轮只需追加翻译），因此不发生迷失。

## 缓解策略及局限

| 策略 | 效果 | 局限 |
|------|------|------|
| 末轮总结（RECAP） | 有改善但不充分 | 不现实——需要预知哪轮是最后一轮 |
| 逐轮重复（SNOWBALL） | 缓解 15-20% 退化 | 信息冗余、token 浪费 |
| 降低温度（T→0） | 单轮大幅改善，多轮几乎无效 | 多轮下仍有约 30% 不可靠性 |
| 开新对话重试 | 最实用 | 用户体验差，治标不治本 |

## 相关概念

- [[指令分片（instruction-sharding）]] — 研究此现象所开发的核心实验方法
- [[能力-可靠性分解（aptitude-reliability）]] — 量化此现象的分析框架
- [[lost-in-the-middle]] — 对话迷失在注意力层面的对应现象
- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — 可能的解决方向：通过外部记忆机制整合跨轮信息

## 来源

- [[llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation|LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation]] — 提出此概念的原始论文

input.content:
---
id: concept-instruction-sharding
date: 2026-05-06
aliases: [分片, sharding, sharded instruction, sharded simulation]
tags: [概念, LLM评估, 多轮对话, 方法论]
---

# 指令分片（Instruction Sharding）

## 定义

指令分片是一种将完全指定的单轮指令拆分为一组较小指令单元（称为"分片"或 shards）的方法，使得这些分片的并集等价于原始指令的信息内容。分片用于模拟多轮欠规范对话——每轮对话最多揭示一个分片，从而实现信息在对话中的渐进式披露。

这一方法由 Laban, Hayashi, Zhou & Neville (2025) 在论文 [[llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation|LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation]] 中提出，作为研究 [[对话迷失现象（lost-in-conversation）]] 的核心实验工具。

## 关键属性

### 五个必须满足的属性

| 属性 | 编号 | 说明 |
|------|------|------|
| 信息保持 | P1 | 分片联合不丢失原始指令的任何必要信息 |
| 明确初始意图 | P2 | 第一个分片定义高层目标（如"写一个 Python 函数"） |
| 顺序无关 | P3 | 除首分片外，其余分片可任意排列而不改变信息 |
| 最大分片 | P4 | 尽可能细粒度——每个分片只引入一条信息 |
| 最小变换 | P5 | 尽量保持原始用语，不过度解读或简化 |

### 数学定义

设 q 为原始完全指定指令，其原子内容单元为：

```
I(q) = [I, (c1, ..., cm)]
```

其中 I 是主要意图，(c1, ..., cm) 是充分说明细节。分片过程旨在构造：

```
q' = [s1, ..., sk] 使得 I(q) = I(q')
```

### 示例

原始指令（GSM8K）：
> Jay is making snowballs to prepare for a snowball fight with his sister. He can build 20 snowballs in an hour, but 2 melt every 15 minutes. How long will it take before he has 60 snowballs?

等效分片指令：
- Shard 1：How long before Jay's ready for the snowball fight?
- Shard 2：He's preparing for a snowball fight with his sister.
- Shard 3：He can make 20 snowballs per hour.
- Shard 4：He's trying to get to 60 total.
- Shard 5：The problem is that 2 melt every 15 minutes.

## 半自动分片流程

1. **分割**（Segmentation）：LLM 将原始指令拆分为非重叠的信息片段
2. **重述**（Rephrasing）：LLM 将每个片段重写为独立的、自然的对话单元
3. **验证**（Verification）：通过模拟实验验证 CONCAT 和 SHUFFLE-CONCAT 性能不低于 FULL 的 80%
4. **人工审核**（Inspect & Edit）：作者通过网页标注界面审核和编辑

每个任务平均需 3 小时人工审核，可产出约 100 条高质量分片指令。

## 五种对话模拟类型

| 类型 | 简写 | 信息披露方式 | 用途 |
|------|------|-------------|------|
| Fully-Specified | FULL | 单轮全部给出 | 基线 |
| Concatenated | CONCAT | 单轮但用分片拼接 | 验证重述无损 |
| Sharded | SHARDED | 多轮逐轮揭示 | 核心实验 |
| Recap | RECAP | SHARDED + 末轮总结 | 缓解策略评估 |
| Snowball | SNOWBALL | 每轮重复所有已揭示分片 + 新增一个 | 缓解策略评估 |

input.content:
---
id: concept-aptitude-reliability
date: 2026-05-06
aliases: [aptitude, reliability, unreliability, 能力-可靠性, 能力与可靠性]
tags: [概念, LLM评估, 指标体系, 多轮对话]
---

# 能力-可靠性分解（Aptitude-Reliability Decomposition）

## 定义

能力-可靠性分解是一种将 LLM 性能退化解构为两个正交维度的分析框架：
- **能力（Aptitude, A）**：模型在最佳情况下的表现，用 90 百分位数估计（A90）
- **不可靠性（Unreliability, U）**：最佳与最差表现之间的差距，用 90 百分位与 10 百分位的差距估计（U90_10）

这一框架由 Laban, Hayashi, Zhou & Neville (2025) 在论文 [[llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation|LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation]] 中提出，用于精确刻画 [[对话迷失现象（lost-in-conversation）]] 的本质。

## 关键属性

### 三个核心指标

对于 N 次模拟对话的得分集合 S = {S1, ..., SN}：

| 指标 | 符号 | 定义 | 含义 |
|------|------|------|------|
| 平均性能 | P | mean(S) | 无偏估计的期望表现 |
| 能力 | A90 | percentile90(S) | 最佳 10% 模拟的表现 |
| 不可靠性 | U90_10 | percentile90(S) - percentile10(S) | 最佳与最差之间的差距 |

可靠性定义为 R90_10 = 100 - U90_10。

### 与箱线图的关系

在得分箱线图中：
- 上须高度 = 能力 A（90 百分位）
- 上下须之间的距离 = 不可靠性 U（90 百分位 - 10 百分位）

### 三种退化模式（等平均性能 P=60%）

论文用理想化例子说明相同平均性能下可对应截然不同的退化模式：

1. **纯能力下降**（A=60%, U=0%）：某些指令完全失败，其他完全成功——可靠性不变但能力下降
2. **纯可靠性下降**（A=90%, U=90%）：每条指令都有时对有时错——能力不变但极度不可预测
3. **混合下降**（A=80%, U=60%）：同时丧失部分能力和部分可靠性——**实际观察到的主要模式**

## 核心发现

在多轮欠规范对话中，论文发现：
- 能力下降相对温和：**-15%**
- 不可靠性剧烈增加：**+112%**
- **所有模型**（无论单轮能力高低）在多轮设置下都表现出极高不可靠性

这意味着 [[对话迷失现象（lost-in-conversation）]] 的本质不是模型"变笨了"，而是"变得不可预测了"。

### 单轮 vs 多轮的可靠性与能力关系

- **单轮**：高能力模型往往也更可靠（正相关）——GPT-4.1、Gemini 2.5 Pro 等表现最好
- **多轮**：能力与可靠性脱钩——即使是高能力模型也高度不可靠

### 温度对可靠性的影响

| 设置 | T=1.0 → T=0 的不可靠性改善 |
|------|---------------------------|
| 单轮 FULL | 改善 50-80% |
| 多轮 SHARDED | 改善 0-20% |

即使 T=0（理论上确定性），多轮设置仍保留约 30% 不可靠性——因为微小 token 差异在多轮中会级联放大。

## LLM 构建者的挑战目标

论文提出三个量化目标，一个"可靠"的 LLM 应同时满足：
1. 单轮与多轮能力相近
2. 多轮不可靠性 U < 15
3. 以上在默认温度 T=1.0 下达成

## 相关概念

- [[对话迷失现象（lost-in-conversation）]] — 此框架所分析的核心现象
- [[指令分片（instruction-sharding）]] — 产生此分析所需的实验数据
- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — 降低不可靠性的潜在途径

## 来源

- [[llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation|LLMs Get Lost in Multi-turn Conversation]] — 提出此框架的原始论文

## 完成内容

### 2. 创建的文件

**来源摘要页**（1 个）：
- `D:\Item\repo\llm-wiki\wiki\sources\llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation.md` -- 详细的中文研究报告，包含研究背景、核心发现、实验设计、六项任务说明、15 个模型测试结果、关键数据表格、缓解策略评估、对 LLM 构建者/用户/研究者的启示、推荐参考文献，以及与 delta-mem 等已有知识库页面的交叉引用。

**概念页**（3 个）：
- `D:\Item\repo\llm-wiki\wiki\concepts\对话迷失现象-lost-in-conversation.md` -- 核心概念：定义、四个表现形式（过早回答、答案膨胀、中间轮次遗忘、过度冗长）、导致迷失的三个任务属性、缓解策略及局限
- `D:\Item\repo\llm-wiki\wiki\concepts\指令分片-instruction-sharding.md` -- 方法论：五个属性定义、数学形式化、半自动流程、五种模拟类型
- `D:\Item\repo\llm-wiki\wiki\concepts\能力-可靠性分解-aptitude-reliability.md` -- 分析框架：三个核心指标定义、与箱线图的关系、三种退化模式、温度影响、LLM 构建者挑战目标

### 3. 更新的文件

- `D:\Item\repo\llm-wiki\wiki\index.md` -- 新增来源和 3 个概念条目
- `D:\Item\repo\llm-wiki\log\2026\05-06.md` -- 追加摄入日志记录

### 核心发现摘要

论文通过 20 万+ 对话模拟实验，系统证明所有主流 LLM（从 Llama-3.1-8B 到 Gemini 2.5 Pro）在多轮欠规范对话中性能平均下降 39%。这一退化可分解为轻微的能力下降（-15%）和剧烈的可靠性恶化（+112%）。论文将此现象命名为"对话迷失"（lost in conversation）-- 当 LLM 在对话早期做出错误假设后，无法自我修正，只会越走越偏。降低温度等常规缓解手段在多轮场景下几乎无效。这对 delta-mem 等在线记忆机制的设计提出了明确的需求。

--- Page 1 ---
LORA: LOW-RANK ADAPTATION OF LARGE LAN-
GUAGE MODELS
Edward Hu∗
Yelong Shen∗
Phillip Wallis
Zeyuan Allen-Zhu
Yuanzhi Li
Shean Wang
Lu Wang
Weizhu Chen
Microsoft Corporation
{edwardhu, yeshe, phwallis, zeyuana,
yuanzhil, swang, luw, wzchen}@microsoft.com
yuanzhil@andrew.cmu.edu
(Version 2)
ABSTRACT
An important paradigm of natural language processing consists of large-scale pre-
training on general domain data and adaptation to particular tasks or domains. As
we pre-train larger models, full ﬁne-tuning, which retrains all model parameters,
becomes less feasible. Using GPT-3 175B as an example – deploying indepen-
dent instances of ﬁne-tuned models, each with 175B parameters, is prohibitively
expensive. We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pre-
trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each
layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable pa-
rameters for downstream tasks. Compared to GPT-3 175B ﬁne-tuned with Adam,
LoRA can reduce the number of trainable parameters by 10,000 times and the
GPU memory requirement by 3 times. LoRA performs on-par or better than ﬁne-
tuning in model quality on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3, despite hav-
ing fewer trainable parameters, a higher training throughput, and, unlike adapters,
no additional inference latency. We also provide an empirical investigation into
rank-deﬁciency in language model adaptation, which sheds light on the efﬁcacy of
LoRA. We release a package that facilitates the integration of LoRA with PyTorch
models and provide our implementations and model checkpoints for RoBERTa,
DeBERTa, and GPT-2 at https://github.com/microsoft/LoRA.
1
INTRODUCTION
Pretrained

Figure 1: Our reparametriza-
tion. We only train A and B.
Many applications in natural language processing rely on adapt-
ing one large-scale, pre-trained language model to multiple down-
stream applications. Such adaptation is usually done via ﬁne-tuning,
which updates all the parameters of the pre-trained model. The ma-
jor downside of ﬁne-tuning is that the new model contains as many
parameters as in the original model. As larger models are trained
every few months, this changes from a mere “inconvenience” for
GPT-2 (Radford et al., b) or RoBERTa large (Liu et al., 2019) to a
critical deployment challenge for GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) with
175 billion trainable parameters.1
Many sought to mitigate this by adapting only some parameters or
learning external modules for new tasks. This way, we only need
to store and load a small number of task-speciﬁc parameters in ad-
dition to the pre-trained model for each task, greatly boosting the
operational efﬁciency when deployed. However, existing techniques
∗Equal contribution.
0Compared to V1, this draft includes better baselines, experiments on GLUE, and more on adapter latency.
1While GPT-3 175B achieves non-trivial performance with few-shot learning, ﬁne-tuning boosts its perfor-
mance signiﬁcantly as shown in Appendix A.
1
arXiv:2106.09685v2  [cs.CL]  16 Oct 2021

--- Page 2 ---
often introduce inference latency (Houlsby et al., 2019; Rebufﬁet al., 2017) by extending model
depth or reduce the model’s usable sequence length (Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Ham-
bardzumyan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021) (Section 3). More importantly, these method often fail to
match the ﬁne-tuning baselines, posing a trade-off between efﬁciency and model quality.
We take inspiration from Li et al. (2018a); Aghajanyan et al. (2020) which show that the learned
over-parametrized models in fact reside on a low intrinsic dimension. We hypothesize that the
change in weights during model adaptation also has a low “intrinsic rank”, leading to our proposed
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) approach. LoRA allows us to train some dense layers in a neural
network indirectly by optimizing rank decomposition matrices of the dense layers’ change during
adaptation instead, while keeping the pre-trained weights frozen, as shown in Figure 1. Using GPT-3
175B as an example, we show that a very low rank (i.e., r in Figure 1 can be one or two) sufﬁces even
when the full rank (i.e., d) is as high as 12,288, making LoRA both storage- and compute-efﬁcient.
LoRA possesses several key advantages.
• A pre-trained model can be shared and used to build many small LoRA modules for dif-
ferent tasks. We can freeze the shared model and efﬁciently switch tasks by replacing the
matrices A and B in Figure 1, reducing the storage requirement and task-switching over-
head signiﬁcantly.
• LoRA makes training more efﬁcient and lowers the hardware barrier to entry by up to 3
times when using adaptive optimizers since we do not need to calculate the gradients or
maintain the optimizer states for most parameters. Instead, we only optimize the injected,
much smaller low-rank matrices.
• Our simple linear design allows us to merge the trainable matrices with the frozen weights
when deployed, introducing no inference latency compared to a fully ﬁne-tuned model, by
construction.
• LoRA is orthogonal to many prior methods and can be combined with many of them, such
as preﬁx-tuning. We provide an example in Appendix E.
Terminologies and Conventions
We make frequent references to the Transformer architecture
and use the conventional terminologies for its dimensions.
We call the input and output di-
mension size of a Transformer layer dmodel.
We use Wq, Wk, Wv, and Wo to refer to the
query/key/value/output projection matrices in the self-attention module. W or W0 refers to a pre-
trained weight matrix and ∆W its accumulated gradient update during adaptation. We use r to
denote the rank of a LoRA module. We follow the conventions set out by (Vaswani et al., 2017;
Brown et al., 2020) and use Adam (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019; Kingma & Ba, 2017) for model
optimization and use a Transformer MLP feedforward dimension dffn = 4 × dmodel.
2
PROBLEM STATEMENT
While our proposal is agnostic to training objective, we focus on language modeling as our motivat-
ing use case. Below is a brief description of the language modeling problem and, in particular, the
maximization of conditional probabilities given a task-speciﬁc prompt.
Suppose we are given a pre-trained autoregressive language model PΦ(y|x) parametrized by Φ.
For instance, PΦ(y|x) can be a generic multi-task learner such as GPT (Radford et al., b; Brown
et al., 2020) based on the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017). Consider adapting this
pre-trained model to downstream conditional text generation tasks, such as summarization, machine
reading comprehension (MRC), and natural language to SQL (NL2SQL). Each downstream task is
represented by a training dataset of context-target pairs: Z = {(xi, yi)}i=1,..,N, where both xi and
yi are sequences of tokens. For example, in NL2SQL, xi is a natural language query and yi its
corresponding SQL command; for summarization, xi is the content of an article and yi its summary.
2

--- Page 3 ---
During full ﬁne-tuning, the model is initialized to pre-trained weights Φ0 and updated to Φ0 + ∆Φ
by repeatedly following the gradient to maximize the conditional language modeling objective:

One of the main drawbacks for full ﬁne-tuning is that for each downstream task, we learn a different
set of parameters ∆Φ whose dimension |∆Φ| equals |Φ0|. Thus, if the pre-trained model is large
(such as GPT-3 with |Φ0| ≈175 Billion), storing and deploying many independent instances of
ﬁne-tuned models can be challenging, if at all feasible.
In this paper, we adopt a more parameter-efﬁcient approach, where the task-speciﬁc parameter


(2)
In the subsequent sections, we propose to use a low-rank representation to encode ∆Φ that is both
compute- and memory-efﬁcient. When the pre-trained model is GPT-3 175B, the number of train-
able parameters |Θ| can be as small as 0.01% of |Φ0|.
3
AREN’T EXISTING SOLUTIONS GOOD ENOUGH?
The problem we set out to tackle is by no means new. Since the inception of transfer learning, dozens
of works have sought to make model adaptation more parameter- and compute-efﬁcient. See Sec-
tion 6 for a survey of some of the well-known works. Using language modeling as an example, there
are two prominent strategies when it comes to efﬁcient adaptations: adding adapter layers (Houlsby
et al., 2019; Rebufﬁet al., 2017; Pfeiffer et al., 2021; R¨uckl´e et al., 2020) or optimizing some forms
of the input layer activations (Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2020;
Liu et al., 2021). However, both strategies have their limitations, especially in a large-scale and
latency-sensitive production scenario.
Adapter Layers Introduce Inference Latency
There are many variants of adapters. We focus
on the original design by Houlsby et al. (2019) which has two adapter layers per Transformer block
and a more recent one by Lin et al. (2020) which has only one per block but with an additional
LayerNorm (Ba et al., 2016). While one can reduce the overall latency by pruning layers or exploit-
ing multi-task settings (R¨uckl´e et al., 2020; Pfeiffer et al., 2021), there is no direct ways to bypass
the extra compute in adapter layers. This seems like a non-issue since adapter layers are designed
to have few parameters (sometimes <1% of the original model) by having a small bottleneck di-
mension, which limits the FLOPs they can add. However, large neural networks rely on hardware
parallelism to keep the latency low, and adapter layers have to be processed sequentially. This makes
a difference in the online inference setting where the batch size is typically as small as one. In a
generic scenario without model parallelism, such as running inference on GPT-2 (Radford et al., b)
medium on a single GPU, we see a noticeable increase in latency when using adapters, even with a
very small bottleneck dimension (Table 1).
This problem gets worse when we need to shard the model as done in Shoeybi et al. (2020); Lep-
ikhin et al. (2020), because the additional depth requires more synchronous GPU operations such as
AllReduce and Broadcast, unless we store the adapter parameters redundantly many times.
Directly Optimizing the Prompt is Hard
The other direction, as exempliﬁed by preﬁx tuning (Li
& Liang, 2021), faces a different challenge. We observe that preﬁx tuning is difﬁcult to optimize
and that its performance changes non-monotonically in trainable parameters, conﬁrming similar
observations in the original paper. More fundamentally, reserving a part of the sequence length for
adaptation necessarily reduces the sequence length available to process a downstream task, which
we suspect makes tuning the prompt less performant compared to other methods. We defer the study
on task performance to Section 5.
3
