34	1
35	INTRODUCTION
36	Large Language Models (LLMs) have driven the development of autonomous agents capable of
37	solving diverse tasks through advanced reasoning and tool use [1–3]. However, a significant limita-
38	tion emerges when these agents engage in sequential tasks: each interaction is treated independently.
39	They approach tasks as isolated episodes, suffering from operational amnesia and failing to learn
40	from past successes or avoid prior mistakes[4]. This inability to leverage experience fundamentally
41	hinders their development toward greater autonomy and intelligence.
42	Humans, by contrast, learn through a continuous lifecycle, leveraging both successes and failures to
43	refine strategies over time [5]. For example, a student solving math problems reflects on recurring
44	errors and successful approaches to extract general problem-solving strategies. This cycle of inter-
45	action, reflection, and abstraction is the cornerstone of developing expertise [6]. Endowing LLM
46	agents with a comparable lifecycle is the key to bridging the gap between episodic problem-solving
47	and sustainable self-improvement. While existing frameworks like Retrieval-Augmented Genera-
48	tion (RAG) effectively address knowledge gaps, they fail to solve a more fundamental limitation:
49	the agent’s inability to systematically learn from the consequences of its own interactions [7].
50	As Figure 1 shows, prior works have attempted to address this limitation, but with critical shortcom-
51	ings. Researchers store natural language reflections across tasks with a powerful external LLM in an

120	Figure 1: An illustration of four major paradigms for LLM agent learning. (1) Stateless Execution:
121	Standard agents discard experiences after each task; (2) Learning by Raw Trajectories: Agents
122	retrieve raw, un-distilled past trajectories; (3) Learning via External Scribing: Agents rely on an
123	external teacher model to distill insights; (4) EvolveR (Ours): A complete, self-contained lifecycle
124	where the agent autonomously distills its own experiences into principles and evolves its policy.
125	external memory [8, 9]. While resource-efficient, this approach treats such reflections as a transient
126	hint, leaving the agent’s intrinsic policy unchanged. On the other hand, learning by recalling raw
127	cases retrieves entire past trajectories to directly guide decision-making. However, this reliance on
128	raw cases struggles to generalize and, more importantly, fails to abstract. The agent merely mimics
129	past solutions instead of distilling the reusable strategic principles that made them successful [10].
130	To overcome these challenges, we introduce EvolveR, a framework that enables agents to self-evolve
131	by utilizing their own experiences. EvolveR implements a full experience lifecycle, in which agents
132	collect trajectories through Online Interaction, distill them into a library of abstract strategic princi-
133	ples during Offline Self-Distillation, and subsequently learn to apply these principles to new tasks.
134	Crucially, EvolveR completes the experience lifecycle with a reinforcement learning mechanism
135	that enables the agent to utilize experience. The agent does not merely mimic its past interactions;
136	it evolves based on what it has learned. EvolveR maintains a dynamic experience base where newly
137	distilled principles are semantically deduplicated and continuously evaluated via a metric score that
138	tracks historical effectiveness.
139	We demonstrate EvolveR’s effectiveness on complex question-answering benchmarks, where it sig-
140	nificantly outperforms strong agentic baselines. Our contributions can be summarized as follows:
141	• We propose the Experience-Driven Self-Evolution Paradigm, a novel, closed-loop lifecycle
142	for LLM agents. In contrast to agents that forget past interactions, EvolveR systematically
143	integrates a complete cycle of online interaction, offline experiences self-distillation and policy
144	evolution. This process enables the agent to continuously transform raw trajectories into a
145	curated repository of strategic principles, establishing a foundation for adaptive agents.
146	• We introduce a complete system for dynamic experiences curation. This system goes far
147	beyond simple experience storage. It features: (1) a self-distillation mechanism, where the
148	agent autonomously distills principles from previous interactions; and (2) a full maintenance
149	pipeline, including semantic deduplication, integration, and quality control guided by a dy-
150	namic metric score.
151	• We provide extensive empirical validation of the EvolveR paradigm across multiple model
152	scales. Our experiments on a diverse suite of complex QA benchmarks demonstrate the effec-
153	2
154	
155	--- Page 3 ---
156	Knowledge
157	Lab
158	tiveness of our approach. Detailed ablation studies confirm that the synergy of our proposed
159	curation and self-distillation mechanisms is critical to the framework’s success, revealing a
160	key insight: while the self-distillation mechanism is less effective on smaller-scale models, it
161	surpasses distillation by a stronger, external teacher model at the 3B scale, validating the
162	importance of cognitive alignment.
163	2
164	RELATED WORK
165	2.1
166	CONTINUAL LEARNING AND SELF-EVOLVING AGENTS
167	Continual learning (CL) aims to enable models to learn sequentially while mitigating catastrophic
168	forgetting [11, 12]. While various replay-based and regularization methods have been proposed,
169	most CL paradigms assume predefined task boundaries and focus on knowledge preservation rather
170	than active acquisition in open-ended environments [13–16]. The pursuit of self-evolving agents
171	moves beyond these limitations by enabling systems to grow autonomously from experience. Frame-
172	works such as Reflexion and Generative Agents explore self-improvement through self-play and
173	reflective reasoning, often storing past trajectories as memory to guide future actions [4, 17–20].
174	However, these systems either store raw, unstructured data or rely on memory mechanisms that are
175	not designed for the systematic, long-term distillation and refinement of abstract strategic knowl-
176	edge. Instead of relying on external data streams, our agent autonomously generates and refines its
177	own experiences through an iterative cycle of online interaction and offline reflection.
178	2.2
179	LLM AGENTS AND REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
180	LLM agents have been widely explored through frameworks such as ReAct, which interleaves rea-
181	soning and actions, and Reflecion, which improves task performance via self-reflection [4, 17].
182	While these approaches are primarily prompt-based and stateless, they prevent long-term accumu-
183	lation of strategic knowledge. External memory frameworks like ExpeL address this limitation by
184	reusing past trajectories, but they do not enable systematic self-improvement across tasks [8]. While
185	effective, these methods often rely on simple prompting and are inherently stateless, limiting their
186	ability to internalize knowledge across tasks. Recent work has increasingly turned to reinforcement
187	learning (RL) to train agents for long-horizon, multi-turn tasks. However, applying RL is challeng-
188	ing due to sparse rewards and the need for stable training signals. Search-R1 [21], O2-Searcher [22],
189	and AutoRefine [23] all use RL to train LLMs to generate and interact with external search tools.
190	While these works successfully optimize the LLM’s interaction with external factual knowledge,
191	they do not address the broader challenge of an agent’s self-improvement through its own internal
192	experience.
193	3
194	METHOD
195	In this section, we present EvolveR, a novel framework designed to enable agent self-evolution
196	through a complete, closed-loop experience lifecycle. Inspired by the human cycle of work and
197	reflection, our approach is structured around three core, interconnected components, as depicted in
198	Figure 2. First, in the Offline Experience Self-Distillation phase, the agent’s policy parameters
199	are frozen, and it systematically distills raw trajectories into a curated base of strategic principles.
200	Second, during the Online Interaction phase, the agent applies this distilled wisdom to guide its
201	deliberative reasoning and action, generating new, high-quality interaction data. Finally, the entire
202	cycle is driven by a Policy Evolution mechanism, where the trajectories collected online are used to
203	update the agent’s policy parameters via reinforcement learning, thus closing the loop. This iterative
204	process allows the agent to continuously transform its interactions into evolving expertise.
205	3.1
206	PRELIMINARIES: FORMALIZING AGENT INTERACTION
207	At each state t, the agent, situated in an unknown state st, selects an action at ∈A based on
208	its policy. Our agent’s action space A is designed for complex, knowledge-intensive tasks and
209	comprises three key operations:

296	3.2
297	THE EVOLVER LIFECYCLE: FROM INTERACTIONS TO PRINCIPLES
298	3.2.1
299	OFFLINE EXPERIENCE SELF-DISTILLATION
300	The core of EvolveR is a self-perpetuating lifecycle designed to transform raw interaction data into
301	a strategic principle. This process is divided into two distinct, alternating phases: an offline self-
302	distillation phase for distilling the principle, and an online interaction phase for applying the princi-
303	ple and gathering new interaction data.
304	Principle from Self-Distillation.
305	The process begins with self-distillation.
306	We leverage the
307	agent’s own policy model πθ to analyze its past interaction trajectories. By adopting the persona
308	of an expert through carefully designed prompts, the model reviews each trajectory and, based on
309	its outcome, distills the core strategic insight into a concise natural language statement. This results
310	in either a guiding principle from a success or a cautionary principle from a failure.
311	Inspired by structured memory frameworks such as Mem0 [24] and G-Memory [25], each principle
312	consists of two components: a natural language description paired with several structured knowledge
313	triples, as illustrated in Figure 2. This self-distillation approach enables the agent to autonomously
314	generate reusable knowledge.
315	Deduplication and Integration.
316	To maintain a high-quality experience base (E), we do not add
317	every distilled principle. Instead, each new principle undergoes a rigorous integration process. First,
318	to handle redundancies arising from similar trajectories (e.g., from GRPO sampling), we perform a
319	deduplication step. We use the agent model πθ to pair-wise check for semantic equivalence among
320	newly generated principles that originate from the same problem, keeping only one representative
321	from each semantically equivalent cluster.
322	4
323	
324	--- Page 5 ---
325	Knowledge
326	Lab
327	Second, for each unique principle, we apply a two-stage matching procedure: we first retrieve the
328	most similar existing principles from E via embedding similarity, then prompt the agent model to
329	provide a binary semantic equivalence judgment. If a principle is novel, it is added as a new entry
330	in E; otherwise, the new trajectory is merged under the existing principle, enriching it without
331	introducing redundancy.
332	Let pcand be a new candidate principle distilled from trajectory τsrc. We update the experience base
333	E as follows:
334	E ←
335	E ∪{pcand}
336	if maxp∈E sim(pcand, p) < θsim
337	Merge(E, p∗, τsrc)
338	otherwise
339	(1)
340	where sim(·, ·) is the cosine similarity between principle, θsim is a similarity threshold, and p∗=
341	argmaxp∈Esim(pcand, p). The Merge operation links τsrc to its best match p∗.
342	This two-level check ensures that E grows with novel insights while strengthening existing ones
343	with new evidence.
344	Quality Control via Dynamic Scoring.
345	As the experience base accumulates principles over time,
346	it becomes essential to evaluate their practical utility and prioritize the most effective strategies. To
347	this end, each principle tracks its usage and success counts, enabling the computation of an empirical
348	score that reflects historical performance. We quantify the empirical utility of each principle using a
349	metric score, which is updated as:
350	s(p) = csucc(p) + 1
351	cuse(p) + 2
352	(2)
353	where csucc(p) and cuse(p) are the success and usage counts for a given principle p, s(p) is the metric
354	score.
355	This score provides a reliable measure of a principle’s historical effectiveness. To ensure the long-
356	term health of the experience base, we periodically prune principles whose scores fall below a thresh-
357	old θprune. This systematic process of distillation, integration, and quality control ensures that the
358	agent’s wisdom remains a compact and high-quality repository of its most effective strategies.
359	3.2.2
360	ONLINE INTERACTION
361	The online phase serves as the interactive testbed where the agent applies its distilled principles to
362	solve problems. The agent operates within a deliberative reasoning loop (e.g., Think-Act-Observe),
363	which enables it to engage in multi-turn, autonomous tool use.
364	However, the core novelty of
365	EvolveR’s online phase is not the loop itself, but how the principles retrieved from the experience
366	base (E) fundamentally alter the agent’s behavior within it.
367	Experience as a Strategic Principle.
368	Unlike standard agents that must discover reasoning pat-
369	terns from scratch through trial and error, an EvolveR agent is guided by a strategic wisdom
370	provided by its own past experiences. At any point in its reasoning loop, the agent can issue a
371	<search experience> action. The retrieved principles Pk do not merely provide factual informa-
372	tion; they offer heuristic guidance that shapes the agent’s subsequent reasoning. For instance, re-
373	trieving a principle such as “For comparison questions, gather data on both items before concluding,”
374	can directly influence the agent’s internal monologue (<think>) and steer its subsequent potential
375	<search knowledge> actions. This makes the agent’s exploration more efficient and less prone to
376	common pitfalls, as it learns to follow the wisdom in its own distilled principles.
377	Generating High-Quality Trajectories for Future Distillation.
378	The ultimate purpose of the on-
379	line phase, within the EvolveR paradigm, extends beyond solving the immediate task. It is respon-
380	sible for generating high-quality data for the next cycle of offline reflection. Because the agent’s
381	actions are guided by proven principles, the resulting trajectories, τnew, are not random walks but
382	are instead rich recordings of structured, experience-guided problem-solving. These trajectories
383	capture the interplay between distilled principles, internal reasoning, and external tool use (e.g.,
384	5
385	
386	--- Page 6 ---
387	Knowledge
388	Lab
389	<search knowledge>), and serve as valuable input for the offline phase, enabling EvolveR to refine
390	existing principles and discover more effective strategies in a virtuous cycle.
391	3.3
392	POLICY EVOLUTION: CLOSING THE LOOP WITH REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
393	To enable the agent to learn from its actions and evolve its policy πθ, we employ a reinforcement
394	learning framework. The learning process is guided by a composite reward function and a policy
395	optimization algorithm that leverages the trajectories collected during the online phase.
396	Reward Function.
397	We design a composite reward function R(τ) for a given trajectory τ that
398	balances task success with procedural correctness. It is a weighted sum of an outcome reward and a
399	format reward: R(τ) = woRoutcome(τ) + wfRformat(τ).
400	• Outcome Reward Routcome, is a sparse, binary reward based on the final answer’s correctness.
401	Following prior work, it is determined by an exact match with the ground truth:
402	Routcome(τ) = EM(apred, agold)
403	(3)
404	where apred is the final answer extracted from the trajectory τ and agold is the ground truth
405	answer.
406	• Format Reward Rformat, is a dense shaping reward that evaluates the quality of the rea-
407	soning process. Let Nthink(τ), Nexp(τ) and Nknow(τ) denote the counts of valid <think>,
408	<search experience> and <search knowledge> actions within τ. Rformat is composed of a
409	think score Rthink, rewarding a balanced number of reasoning steps, and a search score Rsearch
410	promoting search experience and knowledge. The final format reward is calculated as:
411	Rformat(τ) = I(τcomplete) · Rthink(τ) + Rsearch(τ)
412	2
413	(4)
414	where I(τcomplete) is an indicator function that is 1 only if the trajectory contains at least one
415	of each required action type (<think>, any search, and <answer>), and 0 otherwise. This
416	ensures that only structurally complete trajectories receive a format reward.

465	4
466	EXPERIMENTS
467	4.1
468	EXPERIMENTAL IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS
469	4.1.1
470	TASKS AND DATASETS
471	To comprehensively evaluate the EvolveR paradigm, we assess its performance on seven question-
472	answering benchmarks, encompassing both in-domain and out-of-domain datasets. Following prior
473	work [21, 22], the in-domain datasets, whose training splits are used to build the experience base,
474	include Natural Questions (NQ) [27] and the multi-hop benchmark HotpotQA [28]. The out-of-
475	domain datasets, used exclusively for evaluating generalization, encompass the general QA bench-
476	marks TriviaQA [29] and PopQA [30], as well as the more complex multi-hop challenges 2Wiki-
477	MultiHopQA [31], Musique [32], and Bamboogle [33].
478	4.1.2
479	BASELINE METHODS
480	Following prior works, we compare against a comprehensive suite of baselines built upon the
481	Qwen2.5 foundational models. The baselines represent three primary paradigms. First, prompting-
482	based methods, which require no parameter updates, include Direct Inference, Chain-of-Thought
483	(CoT) [18], Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [34], and advanced variants like IRCoT [35]
484	and Search-o1 [36]. Second, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) methods represent approaches that learn
485	from static expert data, including standard SFT [37] and Rejection Sampling [38]. Finally, the most
486	direct competitors are RL methods, against which we benchmark extensively. This category is pri-
487	marily composed of Search-R1 [21], DeepSeek-R1 [39], which are also trained with trajectory-level
488	feedback. Specifically, DeepSeek-R1 performs reasoning and answer steps without a search engine,
489	whereas Search-R1 incorporates an external local or web search engine. Together, these baselines
490	provide a challenging evaluation landscape for our proposed paradigm.
491	4.1.3
492	EVALUATION METRICS
493	To ensure a direct and fair comparison with prior work in our main results, our primary evaluation
494	metric is Exact Match (EM), a strict measure that requires the predicted answer to exactly match
495	the ground truth after standard normalization. We also report the F1 Score in the analysis of model
496	scales’ generalizability, which provides a more comprehensive and robust measure of performance,
497	particularly since ground truths may contain multiple valid answers or aliases.
498	4.1.4
499	IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS
500	Our experiments are conducted on the Qwen2.5 model family. Inspired by DeepSeek-R1 [39],
501	we introduce a cold-start stage to stabilize early RL training by first fine-tuning the base model
502	on a small, curated dataset of CoT interaction trajectories. Following the setup of Search-R1, we
503	construct this dataset from approximately 700 samples from the NQ and HotpotQA training sets. We
504	utilize the LLama Factory [40] to fine-tune the model with LoRA. For the agent evolution phase,
505	we employ GRPO for optimization. At each RL step, we sample a batch of 128 prompts, generating
506	G = 8 trajectories for each. The agent is then updated, again using Adam, but with a reduced
507	learning rate of 1 × 10−6, a warm-up step of 20 and a mini-batch size of 128. All training is
508	conducted on 8 A100 GPUs, leveraging the Verl framework 1 for efficient implementation. We will
509	show more details in Appendix 4.1.

750	5
751	FURTHER ANALYSIS
752	5.1
753	ANALYSIS OF MODEL SCALES GENERALIZABILITY
754	To validate that our EvolveR framework is a generalizable paradigm rather than a method tailored to
755	a specific model size, we evaluated its performance across a spectrum of open-source model scales.
756	As presented in Figure 4, we applied EvolveR to Qwen2.5 models of 0.5B, 1.5B, and 3B parameters.
757	The results reveal a clear and consistent positive trend: as the parameter count of the base model
758	increases, the performance of the EvolveR agent improves significantly on every benchmark. The
759	average performance rises monotonically from 0.150 on the 0.5B model to 0.270 on the 1.5B model,
760	and further to 0.382 on the 3B model. This scaling behavior demonstrates that our experience-driven
761	lifecycle effectively harnesses the superior reasoning and instruction-following capabilities inherent
762	in larger foundational models. It confirms that EvolveR acts as a synergistic layer on top of the base
763	model, and suggests that its performance will continue to improve with future advancements in the
764	open-source LLM landscape.

835	ABLATION STUDIES: DISSECTING THE EVOLVER FRAMEWORK
836	5.2.1
837	VALIDATING THE SELF-DISTILLATION MECHANISM
838	Figure 4: Performance of EvolveR across various model
839	scales.
840	A central claim of our work is that an
841	agent can learn effectively through
842	self-distillation. To rigorously inves-
843	tigate this, we compare our standard
844	EvolveR (self-distill)
845	against
846	a
847	strong
848	alternative,
849	EvolveR(teacher-distill),
850	which

853	powerful

857	The results, presented in Table 2, re-
858	veal a nuanced, scale-dependent re-
859	lationship. For smaller models like
860	the 0.5B variant, the stronger exter-
861	nal teacher provides a clear benefit,
862	as the base model’s own distillation
863	capabilities are limited. However, as the model scales to 3B, a reversal occurs: our EvolveR
864	(self-distill) (0.382 avg.) outperforms the teacher-guided variant (0.370 avg.). This is a
865	critical finding, suggesting that as an agent’s own reasoning becomes more sophisticated, principles
866	distilled from its own internal policy are ultimately more effective due to better ”cognitive align-
867	ment”. This validates self-distillation as a core, scaling strength of the EvolveR paradigm.
868	5.2.2
869	THE ROLE OF EXPERIENCE RETRIEVAL
870	To quantify the direct benefit of providing the agent with access to its distilled principles at inference
871	time. To achieve this, we compare our full EvolveR model against an ablated variant, EvolveR
872	w/o exp-retrieve. It is critical to note that both models undergo the identical experience-
873	driven RL training process. The sole difference is that the w/o exp-retrieve variant is denied
874	access to the experience base during evaluation.
875	The results in Table 3 show a stark performance degradation across all model scales when experience
876	retrieval is disabled. For the 3B model, for instance, the average performance drops significantly
877	from 0.382 to 0.340. This substantial gap underscores a key finding: an agent trained with our
878	EvolveR framework, while powerful on its own, achieves its full potential only when it can access
879	and condition on the relevant principles from its past. This demonstrates that experience retrieval is
880	a critical and indispensable component of the EvolveR paradigm for optimal performance.

982	The results yield several key observations: First, while RL alone provides a baseline improvement
983	(0.325), it is insufficient to reach optimal performance. Second, experience retrieval contributes a
984	substantial direct gain, improving the RL-only baseline from 0.340 to 0.382. Third, RL further en-
985	hances experience-conditioned training, as evidenced by the improvement from the non-RL retrieval
986	setting (0.357) to the full framework (0.382). Overall, principle retrieval and RL act complementar-

1015	ANALYSIS OF PRINCIPLE QUALITY AND SCALABILITY
1016	Characterizing Principle Quality and Failure Modes. To systematically evaluate the quality and
1017	potential failure modes of the distilled principles, we conducted a manual annotation of 100 sampled
1018	principles from the experience base. We categorized them into three mutually exclusive types:
1019	• Ideal (Correct & Actionable): Logically sound and providing concrete, executable guidance.
1020	• Vague (Correct but not Actionable): Logically correct but too generic to effectively guide
1021	the model’s actions (e.g., “read the context carefully”).
1022	• Incorrect / Misleading: Factually wrong or overly specific/harmful rules that could systemat-
1023	ically mislead the reasoning process.
1024	We stratified the samples by their assigned metric scores (Low-Score vs. High-Score tiers) to assess
1025	the efficacy of our dynamic scoring mechanism.
1026	Table 6: Manual annotation of principle quality by score tier.
1027	Score Tier
1028	Ideal
1029	Vague
1030	Incorrect / Misleading
1031	Low-Score (0.30 - 0.44)
1032	26%
1033	50%
1034	24%
1035	High-Score (0.44 - 0.75)
1036	82%
1037	10%
1038	8%
1039	The results in Table 6 reveal that the system’s primary failure mode in the lower-score tier is the
1040	generation of vague advice (50%) rather than strictly incorrect rules. More importantly, the data
1041	confirms that the utility-based scoring-and-pruning mechanism effectively filters out misleading or
1042	incorrect principles (reducing them from 24% to 8%) while significantly increasing the concentra-
1043	tion of highly actionable principles (from 26% to 82%).
1044	Long-Term Noise Accumulation and Scalability. A critical challenge for experience-augmented
1045	agents is the potential for performance degradation and latency bottlenecks as the experience base
1046	grows and accumulates noise over time. To investigate the system’s robustness to long-term expe-
1047	rience expansion, we tracked the Qwen2.5-3B agent’s search latency and reasoning performance as
1048	the experience base naturally accumulated during training. Evaluations were conducted on a 10%
1049	validation subset randomly sampled across the six benchmarks.

1072	As shown in Table 7, even when the experience base scales to nearly 50,000 principles, the retrieval
1073	latency remains negligible (0.20s), indicating that the framework does not face severe computa-
1074	tional bottlenecks. Furthermore, despite the massively expanded candidate pool and the inherently
1075	increased risk of retrieving noisy principles, the agent’s performance does not collapse. Instead,
1076	it remains highly stable and peaks at the 45k scale. This provides strong empirical evidence that
1077	the combination of semantic deduplication, dynamic scoring, and RL-conditioned policy is highly
1078	robust to substantial experience growth and long-term noise.

1080	CONCLUSION
1081	In this work, we introduced EvolveR, a novel paradigm for self-evolving LLM agents centered on
1082	a complete, closed-loop experience lifecycle. Our extensive experiments demonstrate the effec-
1083	tiveness of this approach, showing that EvolveR consistently and significantly outperforms a wide
1084	11
1085	
1086	--- Page 12 ---
1087	Knowledge
1088	Lab
1089	range of strong baseline methods on a comprehensive suite of QA benchmarks. Furthermore, our
1090	detailed ablation studies rigorously validate the core tenets of our framework, confirming the sig-
1091	nificant value of the agent’s self-distilled experiences and demonstrating the high efficacy of the
1092	self-distillation mechanism itself. While the quality of distilled principles is inherently tied to the
1093	base model’s capabilities, pointing to promising future work, EvolveR provides a concrete blueprint
1094	for agents that learn from the consequences of their own experiences, shifting the focus from merely
1095	accessing knowledge to actively building and evolving expertise.

1276	APPENDIX
1277	A.1
1278	EXPERIMENTAL IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS
1279	We provide a comprehensive list of hyperparameters and implementation details used in our experi-
1280	ments to ensure full reproducibility.
1281	General Setup.
1282	Across all experiments, we use models from the Qwen2.5 family [41] with their
1283	corresponding tokenizers. The maximum sequence length is set to 8192 tokens for all inputs, and
1284	the maximum response sequence length is set to 1024 tokens. The GPT-4o-mini model is used as the
1285	teacher model in the corresponding ablation study. We use BGE-M3 [42] as our embedding model.
1286	Cold-start Stage.
1287	This initial SFT stage is designed solely to teach the model the required interac-
1288	tion format(e.g., producing well-structured <think> and <search> actions). The model is trained
1289	for 3 epochs using the Adam optimizer, with an initial learning rate of 1 × 10−4, a warm-up ratio of
1290	0.1, and a batch size of 16.
1291	Online Interaction Phase.
1292	For each <search knowledge> action, we retrieve the top-kd = 3
1293	documents from the external knowledge base, following the prior work [21]. Similarly, for each
1294	<search experience> action, we retrieve the top-ke = 3 principles from the experience base E.
1295	Offline Distill Phase.
1296	The self-distill mechanism utilizes the agent’s own policy model πθ to dis-
1297	till principles. The temperature is set to 1 during this phase. For the deduplication and integration
1298	process, we first use a semantic similarity pre-filter with a threshold of θsim = 0.85 before pass-
1299	ing candidates to the LLM-based equivalence check. The periodic principle sweep removes any
1300	principle from E whose metric score falls below the pruning threshold of θprune = 0.3.
1301	Reward Function Details.
1302	As described in the Section 3.3, the Format Reward is an average of
1303	a think score and a search score. We detail their specific calculation here. The think score Rthink is
1304	determined by a discrete mapping based on the number of <think> actions, Nthink: it scales from 0.2
1305	(for Nthink = 1) to a maximum of 1.0 (for Nthink = 6), and is capped at 0.5 for excessive reasoning
1306	(Nthink > 8) to encourage conciseness. The search score Rsearch is the sum of a diversity score and
1307	a quantity bonus. The diversity score is 0.5 if both <search experience> and <search knowledge>
1308	are used, 0.2 if only one type is used, and 0 otherwise. A quantity bonus of 0.1 is added for each
1309	additional search action beyond the first, up to a maximum bonus of 0.5 (for a total of 6 searches).
1310	Policy Optimization.
1311	The composite reward function is weighted with wo = 1.0 for the outcome
1312	reward and wf = 0.1 for the format reward. For the GRPO objective function (Equation 5), the
1313	clipping parameter is set to ϵ = 0.2 and the KL-divergence coefficient is β = 0.001. During the
1314	training procedure, we adopt vLLM to accelerate LLM rollouts. The tensor parallel size is set to 1,
1315	and the GPU memory utilization ratio is set at 0.6. For rollout sampling, we use a temperature of
1316	1.0 and a top-p value of 0.95.
1317	Computational Cost Analysis.
1318	We provide detailed computational resources required for training
1319	and experience retrieval latency to demonstrate the efficiency of EvolveR.
1320	• Training Cost: The full training lifecycle for the Qwen2.5-3B model, which includes the cold-
1321	start SFT phase and the subsequent RL policy evolution via GRPO, requires approximately
1322	39.4 hours on a server equipped with 8 NVIDIA A100 (80GB) GPUs.
1323	• Experience Retrieval Latency: A key concern for retrieval-augmented systems is the added
1324	latency during inference. We implement the Experience Base using Milvus for efficient vec-
1325	tor similarity search. Our empirical measurements show that even with an experience base
1326	containing approximately 14,000 principles, the latency for retrieving the top-3 relevant prin-
1327	ciples is approximately 0.06 seconds. That is imperceptible compared to LLM generation time,
1328	ensuring that EvolveR maintains high throughput during deployment.
1329	16
1330	
1331	--- Page 17 ---
1332	Knowledge
1333	Lab
1334	SFT-only Baseline Details.
1335	We used the exact same set of successful trajectories generated during
1336	the Online Interaction stage. Both <experience> and <information> tokens were masked during
1337	training. We utilised the LLaMAFactory [40] to fine-tune the Qwen2.5-3B model using LoRA
1338	(Low-Rank Adaptation). The LoRA rank is set to 8, the learning rate is 1 × 10−4, and the model is
1339	trained for 5 epochs with a batch size of 1. The warmup ratio is 0.1, the maximum sequence length
1340	is 4096 tokens, gradient accumulation steps are set to 8, and the learning rate scheduler is Cosine.
1341	A.2
1342	ADDITIONAL EXPERIMENTAL ANALYSIS
1343	A.2.1
1344	NECESSITY OF THE RL (GRPO) STAGE
1345	To assess the necessity of the Reinforcement Learning stage in EvolveR, we conduct an ablation
1346	experiment on the Qwen2.5-3B model. Specifically, we reuse the exact same set of successful
1347	trajectories collected during online interaction, but train the policy via standard SFT rather than
1348	GRPO. The full SFT hyperparameter configurations are provided in Section A.1.
1349	As shown in Table 8, the RL-based variant substantially outperforms the SFT-only version on the
1350	3B model, achieving a 7% relative improvement. This result highlights the fundamental limitation
1351	of SFT: it merely encourages the model to reproduce surface-level action sequences from success-
1352	ful trajectories, without understanding the underlying utility or expected reward of actions such as
1353	<search experience>. In contrast, our RL approach (GRPO) leverages both successful and failed
1354	trajectories, enabling the agent to learn what to do from positive rollouts and what to avoid from
1355	negative ones, which is essential for developing robust retrieval and reasoning strategies.
1356	Table 8: Comparison between SFT-only and RL/GRPO training in EvolveR on the Qwen2.5-3B

1445	A.2.3
1446	LONGITUDINAL ANALYSIS OF LEARNING DYNAMICS
1447	To provide a deeper understanding of how the EvolveR agent improves over time, we present a
1448	longitudinal analysis of its behavior during the RL training process. We focus on two key aspects:
1449	the evolution of action frequencies and the improvement in the quality of distilled principles.

1468	• Early Phase (Interval 1): Thanks to the cold-start SFT, the agent begins with reasonable tool

1474	• Convergence Phase (Interval 4): Crucially, the action counts do not increase indefinitely.
1475	They converge to a stable, efficient range (approx.

1496	Intrinsic Improvement in Principle Distillation.
1497	To verify that the agent is indeed improving
1498	its ability to distill high-quality principles (rather than just filtering out bad ones), we analyzed
1499	the quality of principles grouped by their distilled time. We divided the training process into four
1500	intervals and calculated the average metric score of the principles generated in each interval
1501	that remain in the Experience Base.

1528	counts (> 12) that ensure statistical stability, this trend indicates that the agent’s intrinsic ability to
1529	distill high-quality strategies improves over time.
1530	A.3
1531	EXPLORING THE INFLUENCE OF EXPERIENCE INTERNALIZATION
1532	In our proposed framework, all retrieved information (both from the external knowledge base (<in-
1533	formation>) and our internal experience base (<experience>)) is treated as context, with loss
1534	masked during the model update phase. A natural question arises from this design: while it is
1535	sensible to avoid learning the content of transient external documents, could the agent benefit from
1536	directly ‘absorbing‘ its own distilled wisdom into its parameters?
1537	To explore this, we conducted a supplementary experiment on the Qwen2.5-3B model. We created
1538	a variant, EvolveR w/ exp-absorb, where we selectively unmasked the loss for the retrieved
1539	<experience> tokens, allowing the learning signal to flow through them. Our hypothesis was that
1540	this might enable the agent to internalize the strategic logic of its principles. The results, presented
1541	in Table 11, were insightful. The EvolveR w/ exp-absorb variant exhibited a slight perfor-
1542	mance degradation compared to our standard approach. We posit that this is due to the challenge of
1543	noise in the training signal. In our current implementation, the agent retrieves a set of top-k princi-
1544	ples at each step. Not all of these principles may be perfectly relevant to the immediate context. By
1545	directly internalizing all retrieved principles without a dynamic quality filter, the agent risks being
1546	updated with noisy or even counter-productive signals. This finding suggests that for direct internal-
1547	ization to be effective, it may require more sophisticated mechanisms, such as an auxiliary model
1548	to weigh the relevance of each principle before absorption. We believe that developing such mecha-
1549	nisms is a promising future direction towards achieving a fully autonomous cycle of self-exploration,
1550	self-distillation, and self-absorption.

1596	of two distinct prompts is used to guide the agent’s own model (πθ) to distill a principle. The first

1	---
2	id: concept-agent-rl
3	date: 2026-04-21
4	aliases: [Agent RL, Agentic Reinforcement Learning, Agent 强化学习]
5	tags: [概念]
6	---
7	
8	# Agent 强化学习（Agent RL）
9	
10	## 定义
11	让 LLM Agent 在可执行环境中通过"生成→执行→反馈"的闭环交互来学习策略的强化学习方法。与传统的静态 SFT 训练不同，Agent RL 利用环境的真实反馈信号来优化模型行为。
12	
13	## 关键属性
14	- **闭环交互**：Agent 生成动作 → 环境执行 → 返回观测 → Agent 决定下一步
15	- **可验证奖励**：不同于 LLM-as-judge 的主观评分，使用沙箱执行、rubric 检查等可程序化验证的奖励
16	- **长链推理**：Agent 需要在多步交互中维护状态一致性，挑战远大于单轮问答
17	- **探索-利用平衡**：训练过程中需要平衡探索新工具组合和利用已知有效策略
18	
19	## 相关概念
20	- [[GRPO]] — 常用的 Agent RL 策略优化算法
21	- [[POMDP]] — Agent 交互的理论建模框架
22	- [[Model Context Protocol (MCP)]] — 为 Agent RL 提供标准化环境接口
23	
24	## 来源
25	- [[agent-world]] — 核心方法之一，多环境 Agent RL
26	
27	## 参见
28	- [[微服务部署与路由优化概览]] — 优化方法对比（RL vs 启发式 vs Lyapunov）

1	---
2	id: concept-grpo
3	date: 2026-04-21
4	aliases: [GRPO, Group Relative Policy Optimization]
5	tags: [概念]
6	---
7	
8	# GRPO（Group Relative Policy Optimization）
9	
10	## 定义
11	DeepSeek 提出的一种无需 critic 模型的强化学习算法。对每个问题采样一组回答，在组内计算相对优势（advantage），用 clipped ratio + KL 惩罚更新策略。
12	
13	## 关键属性
14	- **无需 value model / critic**：与传统 PPO 不同，GRPO 通过组内相对比较计算优势，省去 critic 网络
15	- **组相对优势**：对同一任务采样 G 个轨迹，每个 token 的优势 = 组内归一化（自身 reward - 组均值）/ 组标准差
16	- **clipped ratio**：类似 PPO，限制新旧策略的重要性比率在 [1-ε, 1+ε] 内，防止策略更新过大
17	- **KL 惩罚**：约束策略不要偏离参考模型太远
18	
19	## 数学形式
20	$$GRPO(\pi) = \mathbb{E} \left[ \frac{1}{G} \sum_{g=1}^{G} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{Y}|} \sum_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{Y}|} \min\left( \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y_i)}{\pi_{old}(y_i)} \hat{A}_i, \text{clip}(\cdot, 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}_i \right) - \beta \, KL(\pi_{\theta} \| \pi_{ref}) \right]$$
21	
22	## 相关概念
23	- [[Agent RL]] — GRPO 是 Agent RL 训练中常用的策略优化算法
24	- [[Lyapunov 优化]] — 同为在线优化方法，但 Lyapunov 用于队列稳定性，GRPO 用于策略学习
25	
26	## 来源
27	- [[agent-world]] — 使用 GRPO 作为 Agent RL 的策略优化算法
28

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	---
2	id: concept-delta-mem
3	date: 2026-05-15
4	aliases: [δ-mem, delta-mem]
5	tags: [概念, LLM记忆, 在线学习, 注意力机制]
6	---
7	
8	# δ-mem（在线关联记忆）
9	
10	## 定义
11	
12	δ-mem 是一种轻量级的 LLM 记忆机制，在冻结的全注意力 Transformer 骨干网络上附加一个紧凑的**在线关联记忆状态**（Online State of Associative Memory, OSAM）。该状态通过 delta-rule 学习持续更新，并在推理时以低秩修正的方式直接参与注意力计算。
13	
14	## 核心思想
15	
16	传统方法要么把历史塞进上下文（代价高、效果差），要么用外部模块检索（与骨干脱节）。δ-mem 走了第三条路：
17	
18	> **把历史压缩进一个固定大小的矩阵，让这个矩阵的读出信号直接修正注意力的 query 和 output。**
19	
20	这就像给模型的注意力机制装了一个"记忆透镜"——透镜本身很小，但能根据历史信息动态调整焦距，让模型"看到"原本看不到的历史关联。
21	
22	## 关键属性
23	
24	### 状态矩阵 S
25	
26	- **大小**：固定为 $r \times r$（论文中 $r=8$，即 8×8 = 64 个浮点数）
27	- **更新规则**：门控 delta-rule
28	  $$S_t = \text{Diag}(\lambda_t) S_{t-1} + \text{Diag}(\beta_t)(v_t - S_{t-1} k_t) k_t^\top$$
29	- **三项分解**：保留旧状态 → 擦除旧预测 → 写入新值
30	
31	### 三步循环
32	
33	1. **读（Read）**：$r_t = S_{t-1} q_t^m$ — 用当前输入查询记忆状态，代价与历史长度无关
34	2. **引导（Steer）**：将 $r_t$ 投影为 query 侧和 output 侧的低秩修正，直接加到注意力计算中
35	3. **写（Write）**：用当前 token 的 key-value 信息通过 delta-rule 更新状态
36	
37	### 三种写入策略
38	
39	| 策略 | 粒度 | 适用场景 |
40	|------|------|----------|
41	| TSW（Token-State Write） | 每 token | 中等模型，需捕捉细粒度变化 |
42	| SSW（Sequence-State Write） | 每消息/段 | 大模型，平滑噪声效果更好 |
43	| MSW（Multi-State Write） | 多个并行子状态 | 小模型，减少信息干扰 |
44	
45	## 关键属性
46	
47	- **极致轻量**：仅 4.87M 可训练参数（骨干的 0.12%）
48	- **骨干冻结**：不修改原始模型参数
49	- **在线演化**：状态随序列动态更新，非静态 adapter
50	- **与注意力耦合**：记忆直接参与前向计算，不经过外部检索路径
51	- **通用保持**：在增强记忆的同时基本不损害通用能力（IFEval 基本不降）
52	
53	## 性能表现
54	
55	| 场景 | 提升幅度 |
56	|------|----------|
57	| 总平均分 | 1.10× 骨干、1.15× 最强基线 |
58	| MemoryAgentBench | 1.31× |
59	| LoCoMo | 1.20× |
60	| TTL（Test-time Learning） | 26.14 → 50.50（近翻倍） |
61	
62	## 相关概念
63	
64	- [[delta-rule-learning]] — 状态更新的核心学习规则
65	- [[低秩注意力修正]] — δ-mem 引导注意力的机制
66	- [[LLM记忆机制分类]] — TMM/OMM/PMM 三类范式
67	- [[LoRA]] — 同为低秩修正，但 LoRA 是静态的，δ-mem 是动态的
68	- [[Context-Rot]] — 解释了为什么需要更好的记忆机制
69	
70	## 来源
71	
72	- [[delta-mem-efficient-online-memory]] — δ-mem 的原始论文
73	
74	## 参见
75	
76	- [[Titans]] — 同为测试时记忆，但 Titans 是架构级创新（深度 MLP 记忆替换骨干），δ-mem 是插件级创新（8×8 矩阵附加在冻结骨干上）。两者共享 delta-rule + 遗忘的核心思想
77	- [[mem0-building-production-ready-ai-agents|Mem0]] — 文本级外部记忆的代表，与 δ-mem 的参数级内部记忆形成互补。Mem0 解决"存什么"和"怎么找"，δ-mem 解决"怎么记"和"怎么用"
78	- [[Memorizing-Transformers]] — 外部 kNN 记忆
79	- [[Model-Context-Protocol-MCP]] — 解决 LLM 与外部世界的交互问题，与 δ-mem 的内部记忆互补
80

toolCalls.result:
1	---
2	id: concept-pomdp
3	date: 2026-04-21
4	aliases: [POMDP, Partially Observable Markov Decision Process, 部分可观测马尔可夫决策过程]
5	tags: [概念]
6	---
7	
8	# POMDP（部分可观测马尔可夫决策过程）
9	
10	## 定义
11	MDP 的扩展，Agent 无法直接观察环境的完整状态，只能通过部分观测来推断。五元组 $(\mathcal{I}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, \delta)$ 分别表示意图空间、状态空间、动作空间、观测空间和状态转移函数。
12	
13	## 关键属性
14	- **意图空间 $\mathcal{I}$**：用户的潜在意图，Agent 从交互历史和环境反馈中逐步推断
15	- **状态分解**：$\mathcal{S} = \mathcal{S}^{env} \times \mathcal{S}^{dlg}$，环境状态（数据库、文件等）× 对话状态（历史、偏好）
16	- **动作双类型**：工具调用（tool）| 语言响应（resp）
17	- **部分可观测**：环境状态 $\mathcal{S}^{env}$ 不直接可见，必须从工具返回的结构化观测中间接推断
18	
19	## 在 Agent 训练中的应用
20	- 将多轮 Agent-环境交互形式化为 POMDP，为 RL 训练提供理论基础
21	- 环境状态不可见性解释了为什么 Agent 需要学习状态追踪（state tracking）能力
22	
23	## 相关概念
24	- [[M/M/C 排队模型]] — 同为随机过程建模方法，但排队论关注稳态性能分析，POMDP 关注序列决策
25	- [[Agent RL]] — POMDP 是 Agent RL 的理论框架
26	
27	## 来源
28	- [[agent-world]] — 用 POMDP 建模多环境 Agent 交互
29

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	---
2	id: concept-lora
3	date: 2026-05-06
4	aliases: [Low-Rank Adaptation, 低秩适配]
5	tags: [概念, 参数高效微调, 低秩分解, Transformer, 迁移学习]
6	---
7	
8	# LoRA（低秩适配）
9	
10	## 定义
11	
12	LoRA（Low-Rank Adaptation）是一种参数高效微调（Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning, PEFT）方法，核心思想是：**冻结预训练模型的权重，仅通过低秩矩阵分解来参数化权重更新**，从而以极少量可训练参数实现下游任务适配。
13	
14	由 Hu et al. (2021) 在 Microsoft 提出，发表于 ICLR 2022。
15	
16	## 核心思想
17	
18	### 直觉：权重更新的"内在低秩"
19	
20	预训练语言模型（如 GPT-3）的权重矩阵 $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ 通常具有满秩，维度巨大（$d$ 可达 12,288）。但受 Aghajanyan et al. (2020) 启发——过参数化模型实际上驻留在低内在维度的子空间中——LoRA 提出关键假设：
21	
22	> **适配过程中的权重变化 $\Delta W$ 也具有低的"内在秩"（intrinsic rank）。**
23	
24	这意味着，表达下游任务所需的信息，可能只需要一个极低秩的矩阵就能捕获。实验证实，即使 $d = 12,288$，$r = 1$ 或 $2$ 往往就已足够。
25	
26	### 类比：在已有地图上标注新路线
27	
28	想象预训练模型是一张详细的世界地图（$W_0$），适配新任务相当于在上面标注新的路线。LoRA 的洞察是：你不需要重绘整张地图，只需要一张透明的 overlays（$\Delta W$），在上面画几条关键的新路线。而且这张 overlay 不需要和原地图一样精细——用粗线条（低秩）就能表达足够的信息。
29	
30	## 数学原理
31	
32	### 基本形式
33	
34	对于预训练权重矩阵 $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$：
35	
36	$$W = W_0 + \Delta W = W_0 + BA$$
37	
38	其中：
39	- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ — 降维后的"输出"投影
40	- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ — "输入"到低维子空间的投影
41	- $r \ll \min(d, k)$ — 低秩约束
42	
43	前向传播：
44	
45	$$h = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + BAx$$
46	
47	### 初始化
48	
49	- $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ — 随机高斯初始化
50	- $B = 0$ — 零初始化
51	
52	训练开始时 $\Delta W = BA = 0$，模型完全等于预训练状态，确保平滑启动。
53	
54	### 缩放因子
55	
56	$$h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} BAx$$
57	
58	- $\alpha$ 是独立于 $r$ 的常数（通常设为首次尝试的 $r$ 值）
59	- 缩放使得改变 $r$ 时无需重新调学习率
60	- 在 Adam 优化器下，调节 $\alpha$ 近似等价于调节学习率
61	
62	### 参数效率
63	
64	对于 Transformer 自注意力的 $W_q$ 和 $W_v$：
65	
66	$$|\Theta| = 2 \times L \times d_{\text{model}} \times r$$
67	
68	其中 $L$ 是层数。以 GPT-3 175B 为例（$L=96, d_{\text{model}}=12288$）：
69	
70	| 配置 | 可训练参数 | 占比 |
71	|------|-----------|------|
72	| $r=1$, 仅 $W_q, W_v$ | 2.4M | 0.001% |
73	| $r=4$, 仅 $W_q, W_v$ | 4.7M | 0.003% |
74	| $r=8$, 仅 $W_q, W_v$ | 18.9M | 0.01% |
75	| 全量微调 | 175B | 100% |
76	
77	## 关键属性
78	
79	### 1. 零推理延迟
80	
81	部署时预计算 $W = W_0 + BA$ 并存储。推理时与普通矩阵乘法完全相同。切换任务只需减去 $BA$、加上 $B'A'$——快速内存操作。
82	
83	这与 Adapter（增加网络深度）和 Prefix-Tuning（减少可用序列长度）形成鲜明对比。
84	
85	### 2. 模块化与共享
86	
87	预训练模型可以共享，为不同任务构建不同的 LoRA 模块。100 个任务的存储：$350\text{GB} + 35\text{MB} \times 100 \approx 354\text{GB}$，而非 $350\text{GB} \times 100 = 35\text{TB}$。
88	
89	### 3. 训练效率
90	
91	无需为冻结参数计算梯度或维护优化器状态。GPT-3 175B 上：
92	- VRAM 从 1.2TB 降至 350GB（3× 减少）
93	- 训练吞吐量从 32.5 tokens/s/GPU 提升至 43.1（25% 提升）
94	
95	### 4. 与现有方法正交
96	
97	可与 Prefix-Tuning 等方法组合使用（论文附录 E 展示了 LoRA + Prefix 的组合）。
98	
99	## 消融实验洞察
100	
101	### 秩的选择
102	
103	实验中最惊人的发现：**$r = 1$ 往往已经足够**。
104	
105	| 权重 | $r=1$ | $r=2$ | $r=4$ | $r=8$ | $r=64$ |
106	|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------|
107	| $W_q, W_v$ (WikiSQL) | 73.4 | 73.3 | 73.7 | 73.8 | 73.5 |
108	| $W_q, W_v$ (MultiNLI) | 91.3 | 91.4 | 91.3 | 91.6 | 91.4 |
109	
110	增大 $r$ 的收益非常有限。子空间相似性分析（Grassmann 距离）表明：不同 $r$ 学到的**顶部奇异方向高度重叠**，其余方向主要是训练噪声。
111	
112	### 适配哪些矩阵？
113	
114	在固定参数预算下：
115	
116	| 配置 | WikiSQL | MultiNLI |
117	|------|---------|----------|
118	| 仅 $W_q$ | 70.4 | 91.0 |
119	| 仅 $W_v$ | 73.0 | 91.0 |
120	| $W_q + W_v$ | **73.7** | **91.3** |
121	| 全部四个 | 73.7 | 91.7 |
122	
123	**$W_q + W_v$ 是最佳性价比选择**。与其增大单个矩阵的秩，不如分散到多个矩阵。
124	
125	### $\Delta W$ 与 $W$ 的关系
126	
127	通过将 $W$ 投影到 $\Delta W$ 的奇异子空间：
128	
129	- $\Delta W$ 与 $W$ 的相关性**强于随机矩阵**
130	- 但 $\Delta W$ **没有重复** $W$ 的顶部奇异方向
131	- 而是**放大**了 $W$ 中已有但未被强调的方向
132	- 放大因子约 21.5 倍（$r=4$ 时）
133	
134	这说明 LoRA 不是在学习全新的特征，而是在**选择性放大**预训练模型中已存在、但对下游任务更重要的特征。
135	
136	## 局限
137	
138	1. **批量多任务推理困难**：如果合并了 $W = W_0 + BA$，同一 batch 中不同样本需要不同的 $A, B$ 时难以处理（不合并权重可以动态选择，但会引入延迟）
139	2. **秩的选择依赖启发式**：缺乏自动选择最优 $r$ 和适配矩阵的原则性方法
140	3. **不适合跨语言等剧烈分布偏移**：如果下游任务与预训练分布差异极大（如完全不同的语言），可能需要 $r = d_{\text{model}}$ 才能充分表达
141	4. **仅适用于稠密层**：对稀疏结构或特殊架构的适配尚待探索
142	
143	## 后续发展
144	
145	LoRA 已成为 PEFT 的事实标准，催生了大量变体：
146	
147	| 变体 | 核心改进 | 年份 |
148	|------|---------|------|
149	| **QLoRA** | 4-bit 量化 + LoRA，单卡微调 65B 模型 | 2023 |
150	| **DoRA** | 将权重分解为幅度和方向，仅对方向做低秩更新 | 2024 |
151	| **AdaLoRA** | 自适应秩分配，按重要性动态调整各层的 $r$ | 2023 |
152	| **LoRA-FA** | 冻结 $A$ 只训练 $B$，进一步减少显存 | 2023 |
153	| **PiSSA** | 用主奇异分量初始化 $A, B$，更快收敛 | 2024 |
154	| **rsLoRA** | 秩稳定化 LoRA，解决大 $r$ 时的训练不稳定 | 2023 |
155	
156	## 与 δ-mem 的对比
157	
158	| 维度 | LoRA | δ-mem |
159	|------|------|-------|
160	| **数学形式** | $\Delta W = BA$ | $\Delta q = W_q^\Delta r$，$\Delta o = W_o^\Delta r$ |
161	| **低秩投影矩阵** | $B, A$（训练后固定） | $W_q^\Delta, W_o^\Delta$（训练后固定） |
162	| **修正信号来源** | 静态参数 | **动态在线状态** $S_{t-1}$ |
163	| **是否随输入演化** | 否 | 是（通过 delta-rule） |
164	| **训练目标** | 下游任务适配 | 在线记忆/持续学习 |
165	| **参数更新** | 梯度下降优化 $A, B$ | delta-rule 更新状态 $S_t$ |
166	| **应用场景** | 任务微调 | 长序列记忆、多轮对话 |
167	
168	**本质区别**：LoRA 的低秩更新是**静态的**——训练完成后 $B$ 和 $A$ 永久固定。δ-mem 借用了 LoRA 的低秩修正**形式**，但将信号来源替换为运行时通过 delta-rule 持续演化的在线记忆状态。同一组投影矩阵在不同历史上下文中产生不同的修正效果——这是"静态形式 + 动态语义"的转化。
169	
170	## 相关概念
171	
172	- [[参数高效微调]] — LoRA 所属的方法大类
173	- [[低秩分解]] — LoRA 的数学基础
174	- [[Adapter]] — 插入瓶颈层的 PEFT 方法
175	- [[Prefix-Tuning]] — 优化输入提示的 PEFT 方法
176	- [[BitFit]] — 仅训练偏置的极简 PEFT 方法
177	- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — 借鉴 LoRA 形式但赋予动态语义的记忆机制
178	- [[delta-rule-learning]] — δ-mem 状态更新的核心规则
179	- [[内在维度]] — LoRA 的理论动机来源
180	
181	## 来源
182	
183	- [[lora-low-rank-adaptation]] — 原始论文
184	
185	## 参见
186	
187	- [[QLoRA]] — 量化版 LoRA
188	- [[DoRA]] — 权重分解低秩适配
189	- [[AdaLoRA]] — 自适应秩分配
190	- [[Context2LoRA]] — 将上下文编码为 LoRA 权重的静态记忆方案
191

# EvolveR: Self-Evolving LLM Agents Through an Experience-Driven Lifecycle

## 一句话总结

EvolveR 提出了一种闭环的经验驱动生命周期框架，让 LLM Agent 能够通过"在线交互 → 离线自蒸馏 → 策略演化"的循环，将原始交互轨迹提炼为可复用的策略原则，并通过 GRPO 强化学习内化这些原则，实现 Agent 的持续自我改进。

## 基本信息

| 项目 | 内容 |
|------|------|
| **标题** | EvolveR: Self-Evolving LLM Agents Through an Experience-Driven Lifecycle |
| **作者** | Rong Wu, Xiaoman Wang, Jianbiao Mei, Pinlong Cai, Daocheng Fu, Cheng Yang, Licheng Wen, Xuemeng Yang, Yufan Shen, Yuxin Wang, Botian Shi |
| **机构** | 浙江大学、上海人工智能实验室、华东师范大学、复旦大学、中南大学、上海创新研究院、上海交通大学、中国科学技术大学 |
| **日期** | 2025-10（arXiv v2: 2026-05-08） |
| **arXiv** | 2510.16079 |
| **代码** | https://github.com/Edaizi/EvolveR |

## 研究背景与动机

当前 LLM Agent 在工具使用方面表现出色，但存在一个根本性缺陷：**操作性失忆（operational amnesia）**——每次交互都被视为独立事件，Agent 无法从过去的成功或失败中系统性地学习。

已有方法尝试解决这一问题，但各有不足：

| 范式 | 代表方法 | 核心缺陷 |
|------|---------|---------|
| 无状态执行 | ReAct | 每次交互后丢弃经验，Agent 内在策略不变 |
| 原始轨迹学习 | ExpeL | 检索原始轨迹，难以抽象和泛化，仅模仿而非提炼 |
| 外部教师蒸馏 | Reflexion + 外部 LLM | 依赖更强的教师模型，Agent 自身策略仍未改变；且存在"认知不对齐"问题 |

EvolveR 的核心洞察是：**Agent 需要一个完整的闭环生命周期**——不仅要能从经验中提炼智慧，还要能将这种智慧内化为自身策略的一部分，形成"越用越强"的正向循环。

## 核心方法

### 总体架构

EvolveR 由三个相互连接的核心组件构成，形成闭环：

```
在线交互（Online Interaction）
    ↓ 生成高质量轨迹
策略演化（Policy Evolution via GRPO）
    ↓ 更新策略参数
离线自蒸馏（Offline Self-Distillation）
    ↓ 提炼策略原则 → 喂入在线交互
    ↑_________________________|
```

### 符号表

| 符号 | 含义 | 维度/说明 |
|------|------|----------|
| $\pi_\theta$ | Agent 策略模型 | 参数为 $\theta$ 的 LLM |
| $s_t$ | 状态 | Agent 在步骤 $t$ 所处的环境状态（部分可观测） |
| $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$ | 动作 | 三种动作之一 |
| $\mathcal{E}$ | 经验库（Experience Base） | 存储已蒸馏的策略原则集合 |
| $p$ | 原则（Principle） | 自然语言描述 + 结构化三元组 |
| $P_k$ | 检索到的原则集合 | Top-$k$ 条相关原则 |
| $\tau$ | 轨迹（Trajectory） | 一次完整交互的记录 |
| $c_{\text{succ}}(p)$ | 原则 $p$ 的成功次数 | 历史使用中引导成功的累计 |
| $c_{\text{use}}(p)$ | 原则 $p$ 的使用次数 | 历史被检索使用的累计 |
| $s(p)$ | 原则 $p$ 的度量分数 | 经验效用评估 |
| $\theta_{\text{sim}}$ | 语义相似度阈值 | 默认 0.85 |
| $\theta_{\text{prune}}$ | 剪枝阈值 | 默认 0.3 |
| $R(\tau)$ | 轨迹奖励 | 复合奖励函数 |
| $w_o, w_f$ | 奖励权重 | 结果奖励 / 格式奖励权重 |
| $G$ | GRPO 组大小 | 每个提示的采样轨迹数（默认 8） |

### 动作空间设计

Agent 的动作空间 $\mathcal{A}$ 包含三种动作：

1. **`<search experience>`**：查询内部经验库 $\mathcal{E}$，检索相关策略原则。环境返回检索到的原则作为观测。
2. **`<search knowledge>`**：查询外部知识库（搜索引擎），获取事实信息。环境返回检索到的信息作为观测。
3. **`<answer>`**：输出最终答案，结束交互。

关键区别：`<search experience>` 和 `<search knowledge>` 是两种本质不同的检索——前者检索的是 Agent 自己从过去经验中提炼的**策略性智慧**，后者检索的是外部**事实性信息**。

### 第一阶段：离线经验自蒸馏（Offline Self-Distillation）

此阶段 Agent 参数 $\theta$ 冻结，专注于从原始轨迹中提炼结构化知识。

#### 原则蒸馏

利用 Agent 自身的策略模型 $\pi_\theta$ 分析历史交互轨迹。通过精心设计的 prompt 让模型扮演"专家"角色，审查每条轨迹并提炼核心策略洞察：

- **成功轨迹** → 引导原则（Guiding Principle）：描述成功策略
- **失败轨迹** → 警示原则（Cautionary Principle）：描述应避免的陷阱

每条原则包含两部分：
1. **自然语言描述**：一句话概括核心建议
2. **结构化三元组**：$(subject, predicate, object)$ 格式的知识表示

直觉：这不是简单的"记录做了什么"，而是提炼"为什么这么做有效/无效"的抽象洞察。

#### 去重与整合

新原则不是简单添加，而是经过严格的两级整合流程：

**第一级：同题去重**

由于 GRPO 对同一问题采样多条轨迹（$G=8$），同一问题会产生多条相似原则。使用 $\pi_\theta$ 进行成对语义等价检查，每个语义等价簇仅保留一条代表。

**第二级：跨题整合**

对每条唯一原则，执行两阶段匹配：
1. 通过 embedding 相似度从 $\mathcal{E}$ 检索最相似的已有原则
2. 用 $\pi_\theta$ 做二元语义等价判断

更新规则：

$$\mathcal{E} \leftarrow \begin{cases} \mathcal{E} \cup \{p_{\text{cand}}\} & \text{if } \max_{p \in \mathcal{E}} \text{sim}(p_{\text{cand}}, p) < \theta_{\text{sim}} \\ \text{Merge}(\mathcal{E}, p^*, \tau_{\text{src}}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

其中 $p^* = \arg\max_{p \in \mathcal{E}} \text{sim}(p_{\text{cand}}, p)$，$\text{sim}(\cdot, \cdot)$ 为余弦相似度，$\theta_{\text{sim}} = 0.85$。

直觉：新知识要么作为独立条目加入，要么作为已有原则的新证据被合并——确保经验库是"精炼"的而非"膨胀"的。

#### 质量控制：动态评分

每条原则跟踪使用次数和成功次数，计算经验效用分数：

$$s(p) = \frac{c_{\text{succ}}(p) + 1}{c_{\text{use}}(p) + 2}$$

分子 +1、分母 +2 是 Laplace 平滑，防止早期样本量不足时的极端值。

定期剪枝：删除 $s(p) < \theta_{\text{prune}}$（默认 0.3）的原则。

### 第二阶段：在线交互（Online Interaction）

Agent 在 Think-Act-Observe 循环中工作，核心创新在于**检索到的原则如何改变 Agent 行为**：

- Agent 随时可发出 `<search experience>` 动作
- 检索到的原则 $P_k$ 提供的不是事实信息，而是**启发式引导**，塑造 Agent 的后续推理
- 例如，检索到"对于比较类问题，在得出结论前应收集双方数据"，Agent 的内部推理（`<thinking>`）和后续 `<search knowledge>` 动作都会被这一原则引导

**双重视角理解在线阶段**：
1. 当下层面：利用原则高效解决当前任务
2. 未来层面：原则引导下的高质量轨迹为下一轮离线蒸馏提供优质素材

### 第三阶段：策略演化（Policy Evolution via RL）

使用 GRPO 更新策略，将经验引导的交互内化为策略参数。

#### 复合奖励函数

$$R(\tau) = w_o R_{\text{outcome}}(\tau) + w_f R_{\text{format}}(\tau)$$

**结果奖励** $R_{\text{outcome}}$（稀疏、二元）：

$$R_{\text{outcome}}(\tau) = \text{EM}(a_{\text{pred}}, a_{\text{gold}})$$

即预测答案与标准答案的精确匹配。

**格式奖励** $R_{\text{format}}$（密集、塑形）：

$$R_{\text{format}}(\tau) = \frac{\mathbb{I}(\tau_{\text{complete}}) \cdot R_{\text{think}}(\tau) + R_{\text{search}}(\tau)}{2}$$

其中：
- $\mathbb{I}(\tau_{\text{complete}})$：轨迹完整性指示器（必须包含 `<thinking>`、至少一种搜索、`<answer>`）
- $R_{\text{think}}$：推理步数评分，鼓励 6 步推理（过多反而扣分）
- $R_{\text{search}}$：搜索评分 = 多样性分数（同时使用经验和知识搜索得 0.5，仅一种得 0.2）+ 数量奖励（每次额外搜索 +0.1，上限 0.5）

参数设置：$w_o = 1.0$，$w_f = 0.1$，即结果奖励权重远高于格式奖励。

#### GRPO 目标函数

$$J_{\text{GRPO}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \in \mathcal{D}} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^{|\tau|} \min\left( \rho_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t,\  \text{clip}(\rho_t(\theta), 1 - \epsilon, 1 + \epsilon) \hat{A}_t \right) - \beta D_{\text{KL}}[\pi_\theta \| \pi_{\text{ref}}] \right]$$

其中 $\rho_t(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t | h_t)}{\pi_{\text{old}}(a_t | h_t)}$ 为重要性采样比率，$\hat{A}_t$ 为优势估计。

关键：轨迹 $\mathcal{D}$ 是在经验原则引导下收集的，因此 GRPO 学到的不是一个通用的推理策略，而是**如何有效利用自身蒸馏智慧的策略**——这才是真正"关闭循环"的关键。

### 冷启动（Cold Start）

在 RL 训练前，用约 700 条 NQ + HotpotQA 训练集样本，由 GPT-4o 生成格式正确的 CoT 交互轨迹，通过 SFT 教会模型基本的交互格式。LoRA 微调，3 epochs，学习率 $1 \times 10^{-4}$。

### 实现细节

| 参数 | 值 |
|------|-----|
| 基座模型 | Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/3B/7B) |
| 最大序列长度 | 8192（输入）/ 1024（输出） |
| 嵌入模型 | BGE-M3 |
| 经验库后端 | Milvus（向量相似度搜索） |
| 每步采样 | 128 prompts, G=8 trajectories |
| RL 学习率 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| GRPO $\epsilon$ | 0.2 |
| KL 系数 $\beta$ | 0.001 |
| 训练硬件 | 8x A100 (80GB) |
| 3B 模型训练时间 | ~39.4 小时 |
| 知识检索 top-$k_d$ | 3 |
| 经验检索 top-$k_e$ | 3 |

## 实验结果

### 主实验（Table 1）

在 7 个 QA benchmark 上评估，包括域内（NQ、HotpotQA）和域外（TriviaQA、PopQA、2Wiki-MultiHopQA、Musique、Bamboogle）。

**Qwen2.5-3B 结果：**

| 方法 | NQ | HotpotQA | TriviaQA | PopQA | 2wiki | Musique | Bamboogle | 平均 |
|------|-----|----------|----------|-------|-------|---------|-----------|------|
| Direct Inference | 0.106 | 0.149 | 0.288 | 0.108 | 0.244 | 0.020 | 0.024 | 0.134 |
| RAG | 0.348 | 0.255 | 0.544 | 0.387 | 0.226 | 0.047 | 0.080 | 0.270 |
| Search-R1-instruct | 0.341 | 0.324 | 0.545 | 0.378 | 0.319 | 0.103 | 0.264 | 0.325 |
| Rejection Sampling | 0.294 | 0.240 | 0.488 | 0.332 | 0.233 | 0.059 | 0.210 | 0.265 |
| **EvolveR** | **0.434** | **0.373** | **0.584** | **0.434** | **0.381** | **0.137** | **0.328** | **0.382** |

**Qwen2.5-7B 结果：**

| 方法 | NQ | HotpotQA | TriviaQA | PopQA | 2wiki | Musique | Bamboogle | 平均 |
|------|-----|----------|----------|-------|-------|---------|-----------|------|
| Search-R1-instruct | 0.393 | 0.370 | 0.610 | 0.397 | 0.414 | 0.146 | 0.368 | 0.385 |
| Rejection Sampling | 0.360 | 0.331 | 0.592 | 0.380 | 0.296 | 0.123 | 0.355 | 0.348 |
| **EvolveR** | **0.462** | **0.411** | **0.620** | **0.473** | **0.395** | **0.168** | **0.392** | **0.417** |

关键发现：EvolveR 在两个模型规模上都取得了最高平均分，且优势在域外数据集上同样显著，说明其策略具有良好的泛化性。

## 消融实验/分析

### 1. 自蒸馏 vs 教师蒸馏（Table 2）

| 模型 | 自蒸馏平均 | GPT-4o-mini 教师蒸馏平均 | 结论 |
|------|-----------|------------------------|------|
| 0.5B | 0.150 | **0.220** | 小模型自蒸馏能力不足，教师更有效 |
| 1.5B | 0.270 | **0.290** | 差距缩小 |
| 3B | **0.382** | 0.370 | **反转！自蒸馏超越教师蒸馏** |

核心洞察：**认知对齐（Cognitive Alignment）**——当模型足够大时，由自身策略蒸馏出的原则与自身推理方式更"对齐"，比外部更强模型的蒸馏更有效。

### 2. 经验检索的必要性（Table 3）

| 模型 | 有检索 | 无检索 | 差距 |
|------|--------|--------|------|
| 0.5B | 0.150 | 0.078 | -48% |
| 1.5B | 0.270 | 0.123 | -54% |
| 3B | 0.382 | 0.340 | -11% |

所有规模上移除检索都导致显著下降，证明经验检索是不可或缺的。

### 3. 组件归因（Table 4）

| 原则蒸馏 | 原则检索 | RL | 平均分 |
|---------|---------|-----|--------|
| 否 | 否 | 否 | 0.134 |
| 否 | 否 | 是 | 0.325 |
| 是 | 是 | 否 | 0.357 |
| 是 | 否 | 是 | 0.340 |
| 是 | 是 | 是 | **0.382** |

关键发现：
- RL 单独提供基线提升（0.134 → 0.325）
- 经验检索与 RL 是互补的：检索提供推理引导，RL 优化利用检索原则的策略
- 三者协同效果最强

### 4. SFT vs RL 训练对比（Table 8）

| 方法 | 平均分 |
|------|--------|
| EvolveR (SFT) | 0.357 |
| EvolveR (RL/GRPO) | **0.382** (+7%) |

SFT 只学习表面动作模式，GRPO 从成功和失败轨迹中学习"什么该做、什么该避免"。

### 5. 原则质量分析（Table 6）

人工标注 100 条原则：

| 分数层级 | 理想（正确且可操作） | 模糊（正确但不可操作） | 错误/误导 |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|----------|
| 低分段 (0.30-0.44) | 26% | 50% | 24% |
| 高分段 (0.44-0.75) | **82%** | 10% | 8% |

动态评分机制有效过滤了低质量原则，高分段中可操作原则占比从 26% 提升至 82%。

### 6. 经验库扩展性（Table 7）

| 原则数 | 检索延迟 | 平均分 |
|--------|---------|--------|
| 4,213 | 0.04s | 0.301 |
| 13,435 | 0.06s | 0.372 |
| 23,448 | 0.12s | 0.382 |
| 32,805 | 0.16s | 0.396 |
| 45,083 | 0.19s | **0.410** |
| 49,934 | 0.20s | 0.387 |

经验库扩展至近 5 万条原则时，检索延迟仅 0.20s，性能保持稳定甚至在 45k 时达到峰值。

### 7. 经验内化尝试（Table 11）

尝试将检索到的原则直接"吸收"进模型参数（不解码器 loss mask），结果轻微下降（0.382 → 0.371）。原因是 top-k 检索中并非所有原则都相关，直接内化引入了噪声。

### 8. 训练动态分析

- **动作频率演化**：推理步数和搜索次数先增后收敛（约 6 次推理、4 次知识搜索、2 次经验搜索）
- **原则质量提升**：后期生成原则的平均分数（0.500）高于早期（0.462），说明 Agent 的蒸馏能力随训练改善

### 9. 模型规模泛化性

| 模型 | 平均分 |
|------|--------|
| 0.5B | 0.150 |
| 1.5B | 0.270 |
| 3B | 0.382 |
| 7B | 0.417 |

性能随基座模型规模单调增长，证明 EvolveR 是一个可泛化的范式，而非针对特定规模的技巧。

## 优点与局限

### 优点

1. **闭环自演化**：首次实现了"交互→蒸馏→策略更新"的完整闭环，而非单点改进
2. **认知对齐**：自蒸馏在 3B+ 规模超越教师蒸馏，避免了对更强外部模型的依赖
3. **经验库管理完善**：去重、评分、剪枝三级质量控制，确保知识库精炼而非膨胀
4. **即插即用**：作为通用框架可应用于不同规模的基座模型
5. **高效检索**：5 万条原则下延迟仅 0.20s，实用性强
6. **全面消融**：多层次消融实验清晰揭示了每个组件的贡献

### 局限

1. **蒸馏质量受限于基座模型**：小模型（0.5B）自蒸馏效果显著差于教师蒸馏，存在"能力天花板"
2. **仅验证 QA 任务**：未在具身交互、创意生成等其他任务类型上验证
3. **长期遗忘问题未解决**：虽然有剪枝机制，但真正的终身学习（数千任务）的可扩展性未验证
4. **经验内化失败**：尝试将原则直接内化到参数中未成功，说明当前方案的信息传递仍然是"检索式"而非"内化式"
5. **安全性担忧**：Agent 自行演化原则可能发展出不良策略，需要与价值对齐技术配合
6. **冷启动依赖外部模型**：初始 SFT 数据需要 GPT-4o 生成

## 提及的实体

- [[Rong-Wu]] — 共同一作，浙江大学 & 上海人工智能实验室
- [[Xiaoman-Wang]] — 共同一作，华东师范大学
- [[Botian-Shi]] — 通讯作者，上海人工智能实验室
- [[上海人工智能实验室]] — 主要机构
- [[浙江大学]] — 第一作者机构
- [[Mem0]] — 结构化记忆框架，启发 EvolveR 的原则表示
- [[Qwen]] — 基座模型族

## 讨论的概念

- [[经验驱动自演化（Experience-Driven Self-Evolution）]] — 本文核心范式
- [[Agent 强化学习（Agent RL）]] — EvolveR 使用 RL 作为策略演化引擎
- [[GRPO]] — 策略优化算法
- [[POMDP]] — Agent 交互的理论框架（部分可观测状态）
- [[LoRA]] — 冷启动阶段的参数高效微调
- [[自蒸馏（Self-Distillation）]] — Agent 从自身轨迹中提炼策略原则
- [[经验库管理（Experience Base Curation）]] — 去重、评分、剪枝的知识管理机制
- [[认知对齐（Cognitive Alignment）]] — 自蒸馏 vs 教师蒸馏的尺度依赖性

## 推荐参考文献

1. **Search-R1** (Jin et al., 2025) — 直接竞争者，同样用 RL 训练 Agent 搜索能力，但不涉及经验自蒸馏。arXiv: 2503.09516
2. **ExpeL** (Zhao et al., 2024) — 存储原始轨迹的最相关工作，EvolveR 的主要改进对象。arXiv: 2308.10144
3. **Reflexion** (Shinn et al., 2023) — 通过自我反思改进的语言 Agent，EvolveR 范式 3 的代表。arXiv: 2303.11366
4. **DeepSeek-R1** (Guo et al., 2025) — RL 推理模型，EvolveR 借鉴了其冷启动策略。arXiv: 2501.12948
5. **ReAct** (Yao et al., 2023) — 推理-行动交织的基础框架。arXiv: 2210.03629
6. **G-Memory** (Zhang et al., 2025) — 分层记忆系统，启发 EvolveR 的原则结构。arXiv: 2506.07398
7. **Memory-R1** (Yan et al., 2025) — RL 增强 LLM Agent 记忆管理。arXiv: 2508.19828
8. **O2-Searcher** (Mei et al., 2025) — 来自同一团队的搜索增强 Agent。arXiv: 2505.16582
9. **Memento** (Zhou et al., 2025) — 无需微调 LLM 即可增强 Agent 记忆。arXiv: 2508.16153
10. **EWC** (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) — 持续学习的经典方法，对比 EvolveR 的自演化路线。

## 关联

- [[Agent RL]] — EvolveR 是 Agent RL 的一个具体实例，将 RL 用于 Agent 策略演化
- [[GRPO]] — EvolveR 使用 GRPO 作为策略优化算法
- [[LoRA]] — 冷启动阶段使用 LoRA 进行参数高效微调
- [[POMDP]] — EvolveR 的 Agent 交互可以形式化为 POMDP
- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — 另一种 Agent 记忆机制：δ-mem 是参数级内部记忆，EvolveR 是文本级外部记忆 + RL 内化
- [[Titans]] — 测试时记忆的架构级方案，与 EvolveR 的经验库方案形成对比
- [[mem0-building-production-ready-ai-agents|Mem0]] — EvolveR 的原则结构设计受 Mem0 启发

input.content:
---
id: concept-experience-driven-self-evolution
date: 2026-05-06
aliases: [Experience-Driven Self-Evolution, 经验驱动自演化, EvolveR范式]
tags: [概念, Agent, 自演化, 经验学习, 闭环学习]
---

# 经验驱动自演化（Experience-Driven Self-Evolution）

## 定义

一种 LLM Agent 学习范式，通过"在线交互 → 离线自蒸馏 → 策略演化"的闭环循环，让 Agent 从自身交互经验中持续提炼可复用的策略原则，并通过强化学习将这些原则内化为策略参数，实现无需外部教师模型的持续自我改进。

## 核心思想

与现有的三种 Agent 学习范式形成对比：

| 范式 | 机制 | 缺陷 |
|------|------|------|
| **无状态执行** | 每次交互独立，不保留经验 | 操作性失忆，无法积累知识 |
| **原始轨迹学习** | 检索并模仿过去的完整轨迹 | 难以抽象和泛化，仅表面模仿 |
| **外部教师蒸馏** | 依赖更强 LLM 提炼经验 | 认知不对齐，Agent 自身策略未变 |
| **经验驱动自演化** | 自蒸馏 + RL 内化，完整闭环 | 蒸馏质量受限于基座模型能力 |

直觉：就像学生做数学题——做完题后反思"为什么做对/做错了"（自蒸馏），提炼出解题策略（原则），下次遇到类似问题时先回忆策略再解题（在线检索），不断练习使策略成为本能（RL 内化）。

## 关键属性

### 闭环生命周期

1. **在线交互**：Agent 在 Think-Act-Observe 循环中解决任务，可随时检索经验库中的策略原则引导推理，生成高质量轨迹
2. **离线自蒸馏**：冻结策略参数，从交互轨迹中提炼结构化的策略原则（成功→引导原则，失败→警示原则），并维护经验库质量
3. **策略演化**：通过 GRPO 等强化学习算法，利用经验引导下收集的轨迹更新策略参数，学会有效利用蒸馏智慧

### 经验库管理三机制

- **去重**：一级同题去重 + 二级跨题语义等价检查（embedding 相似度 + LLM 判断）
- **动态评分**：$s(p) = \frac{c_{\text{succ}}(p) + 1}{c_{\text{use}}(p) + 2}$（Laplace 平滑的经验效用分数）
- **剪枝**：定期删除低于阈值的低质量原则

### 认知对齐效应

关键发现：当基座模型足够大（~3B 参数）时，由 Agent 自身策略蒸馏的原则比外部更强教师模型蒸馏的原则更有效。原因：自蒸馏产生的原则与 Agent 自身的推理模式和失败模式更"对齐"。

## 与其他概念的关系

### 与 Agent RL 的关系

经验驱动自演化是 [[Agent RL]] 的一个具体应用场景。它将 RL 用于 Agent 策略的持续演化，而非常规的单任务优化。关键区别：RL 的训练数据是经验原则引导下的轨迹，学到的策略是"如何有效利用自身智慧"而非通用推理。

### 与记忆机制的关系

经验驱动自演化可视为一种**文本级外部记忆 + RL 内化**的方案：

| 方案 | 记忆载体 | 读写方式 | 与 Agent RL 的关系 |
|------|---------|---------|-------------------|
| [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] | 参数级（矩阵状态） | delta-rule 在线更新 | 无关，纯前向计算 |
| [[Titans]] | 架构级（深度 MLP） | 训练时学习 | 无关，测试时记忆 |
| **经验驱动自演化** | 文本级（原则库） | 自蒸馏 + 检索 | RL 用于策略内化 |

### 与 RAG 的区别

RAG 检索的是**外部事实性知识**，解决的是"不知道"的问题。经验驱动自演化检索的是**自身策略性智慧**，解决的是"不会做"的问题。两者互补而非替代。

## 局限

1. **能力天花板**：蒸馏质量受限于基座模型能力，小模型（<1.5B）的自蒸馏效果显著弱于教师蒸馏
2. **经验内化困难**：直接将检索到的原则通过梯度下降内化到参数中会引入噪声，当前方案仍然依赖"检索式"而非"内化式"
3. **任务泛化待验证**：目前仅在 QA 任务上验证，具身交互、创意生成等场景的适用性未知

## 相关概念

- [[Agent RL]] — 策略演化的技术基础
- [[GRPO]] — 常用的策略优化算法
- [[POMDP]] — Agent 交互的理论框架
- [[自蒸馏（Agent策略自蒸馏）]] — 从自身轨迹中提炼策略智慧的机制
- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — 参数级内部记忆方案，形成对比
- [[Titans]] — 架构级记忆方案，形成对比
- [[LoRA]] — 冷启动阶段的参数高效微调方法
- [[Mem0]] — 启发经验库结构设计的外部记忆框架

## 来源

- [[evolver-from-trajectories-to-principles]] — 提出经验驱动自演化范式的原始论文

## 参见

- [[能力-可靠性分解-aptitude-reliability]] — Agent 能力的另一种分析框架

---
id: concept-agent-self-distillation
date: 2026-05-06
aliases: [Self-Distillation, Agent策略自蒸馏, Agent Self-Distillation, 自蒸馏]
tags: [概念, Agent, 自蒸馏, 经验学习, 策略优化]
---

# 自蒸馏（Agent 策略自蒸馏）

## 定义

在 Agent 语境下，自蒸馏是指 Agent 利用**自身的策略模型**（而非外部更强的教师模型）分析过去的交互轨迹，从中提炼出抽象的、可复用的策略原则的过程。核心区别于传统知识蒸馏：这里没有教师-学生对齐的范式，而是 Agent 扮演"自我反思者"的角色。

## 核心机制

### 蒸馏流程

1. **轨迹审查**：Agent 的策略模型 $\pi_\theta$（冻结参数）审查每条历史交互轨迹
2. **专家角色扮演**：通过精心设计的 prompt 让模型以"专家"视角分析轨迹
3. **原则生成**：根据轨迹成败分别生成
   - **成功轨迹** → 引导原则（Guiding Principle）：描述"什么策略有效"
   - **失败轨迹** → 警示原则（Cautionary Principle）：描述"什么错误应避免"
4. **结构化表示**：每条原则 = 自然语言描述 + $(subject, predicate, object)$ 三元组列表

### 认知对齐（Cognitive Alignment）

自蒸馏最关键的特性是**认知对齐**——蒸馏出的原则与 Agent 自身的推理方式天然匹配。

实验证据（来自 EvolveR）：

| 模型规模 | 自蒸馏平均 | GPT-4o-mini 教师蒸馏平均 | 胜者 |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|------|
| 0.5B | 0.150 | 0.220 | 教师 |
| 1.5B | 0.270 | 0.290 | 教师 |
| 3B | **0.382** | 0.370 | **自蒸馏** |

在 3B 规模，自蒸馏反超教师蒸馏。原因分析：
- 自蒸馏的原则反映了 Agent **自身的失败模式和推理习惯**，针对性更强
- 外部教师模型（即使是 GPT-4o-mini）生成的原则是"旁观者视角"，可能包含 Agent 无法执行的策略
- 但小模型（<1.5B）自身分析能力不足，蒸馏出低质量原则，此时教师模型仍有优势

### 与传统知识蒸馏的区别

| 维度 | 传统知识蒸馏 | Agent 策略自蒸馏 |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **目标** | 模型压缩（大→小） | 策略知识提炼（经验→原则） |
| **教师** | 更大的预训练模型 | Agent 自身（冻结策略） |
| **知识类型** | 输出分布 / 特征表示 | 自然语言策略原则 |
| **传递方式** | 软标签 / 特征匹配 | Prompt 引导的文本生成 |
| **对齐问题** | 模型容量差异导致信息损失 | 认知不对齐（教师-学生视角不同） |
| **应用时机** | 训练时 | 离线反思阶段 |

## 关键属性

- **无需外部教师**：消除了对更强 LLM 的依赖，尤其在 3B+ 规模
- **策略抽象**：从具体轨迹到通用原则，跨越"表面模仿"到"深层理解"
- **双面学习**：既从成功中提取"该做什么"，也从失败中提取"不该做什么"
- **尺度依赖**：效果随基座模型能力增长，存在一个"自蒸馏超越教师蒸馏"的临界规模

## 相关概念

- [[经验驱动自演化]] — 自蒸馏是其中的离线阶段核心机制
- [[Agent RL]] — 自蒸馏为 RL 训练提供高质量的策略引导数据
- [[GRPO]] — 利用自蒸馏产出的轨迹进行策略优化
- [[Mem0]] — 其结构化记忆表示启发自蒸馏的原则格式设计
- [[δ-mem（在线关联记忆）]] — 参数级记忆方案，与自蒸馏的文本级原则方案形成对比

## 来源

- [[evolver-from-trajectories-to-principles]] — 系统性提出并验证 Agent 策略自蒸馏机制

## 参见

- [[图记忆]] — 另一种 Agent 记忆组织方式
- [[文本记忆]] — Agent 使用自然文本存储记忆的方式

# 浙江大学

## 概述
中国顶尖综合性大学，在人工智能和自然语言处理领域有重要研究贡献。EvolveR 论文共同第一作者 Rong Wu 的所属机构。

## 关键事实
- 位置：浙江省杭州市
- 核心领域：计算机科学、人工智能、自然语言处理
- 与 EvolveR 的关联：共同一作 Rong Wu 的博士生/研究生机构

Pages: 33
--- Page 1 ---
OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking
Yinjie Wang*, Xuyang Chen*, Xiaolong Jin*, Mengdi Wang†, Ling Yang†
https://github.com/Gen-Verse/OpenClaw-RL
Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal
or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live,
online learning source. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework that employs next-state signals
to optimize personal agents online through infrastructure and methodology innovations. On the
infrastructure side, we extend existing RL systems to a server–client architecture where the RL server
hosts the policy behind an inference API and user terminals stream interaction data back over HTTP.
From each observed next state, the system extracts two complementary training signals, evaluative
and directive, via a separate asynchronous server so that neither signal extraction nor optimization
blocks inference. On the methodology side, we introduce a hybrid RL objective that unifies both
signal types in a single update: directive signals provide richer, token-level supervision but are sparser,
while evaluative signals are more broadly available. To stabilize distillation under teacher–student
mismatch, we propose overlap-guided hint selection, which picks the hint whose induced teacher
distribution maximally overlaps with the student’s top-𝑘tokens, together with a log-probability-
difference clip that bounds per-token advantages. Applied to personal agents, OpenClaw-RL enables
an agent to improve simply by being used, recovering conversational signals from user re-queries,
corrections, and explicit feedback. Applied to general agents, OpenClaw-RL is the first RL framework
to unify real-world agent settings spanning terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call environments, where
we additionally demonstrate the utility of next-state signals in long-horizon settings.
Policy Server (SGLang)

200	OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking
201	1. Introduction
202	Agentic systems powered by large language models (LLMs) have been shown to significantly improve
203	productivity and are now widely deployed in industrial settings (Anthropic, 2025; OpenAI, 2025;
204	OpenClaw, 2026). The data generated through agent usage, such as user replies, tool execution results,
205	and GUI state transitions, constitutes a valuable source of insights for improving agent frameworks
206	(Lee et al., 2026; Zhang et al., 2024), building memory systems (Chhikara et al., 2025; Packer et al.,
207	2023; Rasmussen et al., 2025; Wang and Chen, 2025; Xu et al., 2025), and producing high-quality
208	training data for the models (Ouyang et al., 2022). However, both the infrastructure and methodology
209	for leveraging such usage data to improve language models in real time remain little explored.
210	The effectiveness of real-time learning for agentic models hinges on the careful design of both
211	infrastructure and methodology. On the infrastructure side, the RL server must be flexible enough to
212	integrate with users’ diverse and evolving agentic frameworks, and the optimization process must
213	run asynchronously so as not to block inference-time usage. On the methodology side, the algorithm
214	must fully exploit the extracted training signals while maintaining stability throughout optimization.
215	In this work, we propose OpenClaw-RL, a framework that combines infrastructure and algorithmic
216	innovations to achieve efficient and stable online optimization for personal agents.
217	We extend existing RL infrastructure (Fu et al., 2025; Hu et al., 2024; Sheng et al., 2025; Zhu
218	et al., 2025), which typically assumes batch data collection on the RL server, to support continuous
219	learning for agentic models. In our setting, the RL server hosts the model behind an inference API.
220	As user terminals query the model, the resulting interaction data is streamed back to the server
221	over HTTP and used to train the model online. To extract training signals online, we identify the
222	next state as a source of two complementary signal types: evaluative and directive. The evaluative
223	signal is produced by a process reward model (PRM) that implicitly scores the preceding action: a
224	user re-query signals dissatisfaction, a passing test signals success, and an error trace signals failure.
225	Beyond scoring, the next state often carries directive information. For instance, when a user says “you
226	should have checked the file first,” the message specifies not only that the response was wrong but
227	also how it should change at the token level. Directive signals are therefore extracted from such next
228	states as token-level hints, providing guidance rather than a scalar reward. By hosting the PRM as a
229	separate server, signal extraction runs asynchronously with respect to policy rollout, so training-signal
230	collection does not block policy inference.
231	While RLVR methods are limited to scalar rewards and thus cannot convert directive signals into
232	policy gradients (Guo et al., 2025; Hu et al., 2025; Shao et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2025a), on-policy
233	distillation methods (Agarwal et al., 2024; Hübotter et al., 2026; Shenfeld et al., 2026) offer an
234	alternative by allowing a teacher model, conditioned on a corrective hint, to provide token-level
235	supervision to the student. Hindsight relabeling approaches (Hübotter et al., 2026; Shi et al., 2026;
236	Zhang et al., 2023) show that adding structured correction information to the context can substantially
237	improve outputs, but these methods all operate on fixed datasets. In concurrent work, Buening et al.
238	(2026) improves online policy by directly prompting with next-state information, though the corrective
239	hints remain implicit in the prompt rather than serving as explicit signals.
240	These distillation methods suffer from training instability and reduced effectiveness due to the
241	distribution mismatch between teacher and student (Li et al., 2026), a problem that is exacerbated
242	when corrective hints are of low quality. We address this by proposing a novel hint-selection criterion
243	based on the top-𝑘token overlap between teacher and student distributions. Among candidate hints,
244	we select the one whose induced teacher distribution shares the most top-𝑘tokens with the student,
245	either per token or aggregated over the sequence. Because the selected teacher already overlaps with
246	the student in high-probability regions, distillation provides informative gradients while keeping the

298	We demonstrate the effectiveness of OpenClaw-RL by first conducting main experiments on Open-
299	Claw (OpenClaw, 2026). Specifically, we simulate settings in which users from different professions

300	use OpenClaw for their own work and evaluate how efficiently the model learns to align with their
301	preferences. We find that OpenClaw-RL outperforms memory and skill-evolution methods in opti-
302	mization efficiency. Furthermore, we extend the algorithm to a general agentic RL setting to study its
303	robustness. OpenClaw-RL achieves better optimization than RLVR methods while remaining stable.
304	Beyond personalization, OpenClaw-RL scales to large-scale general agent deployment. Built on the
305	asynchronous slime (Zhu et al., 2025) infrastructure, we support cloud-hosted parallel environments
306	across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings, covering the most common real-world agent
307	deployment scenarios. To our knowledge, this is the first open-source RL framework to unify these
308	diverse agent types under a single training infrastructure. Across these settings, OpenClaw-RL achieves
309	strong optimization with promising training dynamics, and we further demonstrate that next-state
310	signals are particularly valuable in long-horizon environments where sparse outcome rewards alone
311	provide insufficient learning signal.
312	Contributions.
313	• Infrastructure for real-time personal agentic RL. We extend existing RL infrastructure to a server-
314	client architecture that supports continuous, real-time learning from deployed agents. The system is
315	flexible enough to integrate with users’ diverse and evolving agent frameworks running on remote
316	terminals, automatically extracts two complementary training signals, evaluative and directive,
317	from observed next states, and runs signal extraction and policy optimization asynchronously so
318	that neither blocks inference-time usage.
319	• Stable hybrid RL objective with overlap-guided distillation. We propose a hybrid RL objective
320	that unifies evaluative and directive signals into a single update, exploiting their complementary
321	strengths. We introduce overlap-guided hint selection, which chooses the corrective hint whose
322	induced teacher distribution maximally overlaps with the student’s top-𝑘tokens, together with a
323	log-probability-difference clip that bounds per-token advantages. Together, these keep the off-policy
324	importance ratio close to unity and yield efficient, stable optimization.
325	• First unified RL infrastructure for real-world agent settings. OpenClaw-RL is the first open-

331	infrastructure with cloud-hosted parallel environments, covering the most common real-world
332	deployment scenarios and enabling direct comparison across agent types.
333	• Comprehensive evaluation across personalization and general agentic RL. We first simulate
334	users from different professions using OpenClaw simultaneously and show that OpenClaw-RL can
335	make the model align to per-user preferences within around 10.3 sessions, more efficiently than
336	memory and skill-evolution methods. We further extend the algorithm to a general agentic RL
337	setting, where it achieves better optimization than RLVR baselines while remaining stable.
338	2. OpenClaw-RL Infrastructure: Unified System for Personal and General Agents
339	We unify automatic optimization of personal OpenClaw agents and large scale agentic RL for general
340	agents, including terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings, within a single framework.
341	We first describe the server–client architecture that enables integration with diverse and evolving
342	agent frameworks through a stateless API (§2.1). We then present the fully decoupled asynchronous
343	pipeline that extracts evaluative and directive signals from next states without blocking inference
344	(§2.2). Finally, we show how the same infrastructure scales from sparse, session-based personal agent
345	streams to dense, parallelized general agent across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings (§2.3).
346	2.1. Flexible Server–Client Architecture for Live Agents
347	OpenClaw-RL is built around an inference-API server–client architecture (Figure 1). The RL server
348	hosts the policy 𝜋𝜃behind a stateless completion API; users’ agent frameworks, which may run on
349	personal devices, terminals, or cloud instances, query the policy through this API and stream their
350	interaction data back to the server over HTTP. This design imposes no constraint on the structure of
351	the user-side framework: any agent that can issue API requests can act as a data source, and that
352	framework may evolve, change tools, or be replaced entirely without reconfiguring the server.
353	Each API request is classified as either a main-line turn or a side turn. Main-line turns comprise the
354	agent’s primary responses and tool execution results, which form trainable samples. Side turns cover
355	auxiliary queries, memory organization, and environment transitions; they are forwarded to the model
356	but do not produce training data. Each request also carries a session identifier, allowing the server to
357	demultiplex concurrent interaction streams from multiple users and attribute each turn to the correct
358	conversation session. This classification allows the RL framework to precisely identify which turns
359	belong to which sessions, enabling targeted training on main-line turns only. For personal agents, the
360	model is connected through a confidential API for private and secure deployment, and multiple users
361	can be served simultaneously without cross-session interference. For large-scale training of general
362	agents, cloud-hosted environments connect through the same API, enabling scalable parallelization
363	without architectural changes.
364	2.2. Asynchronous Signal Extraction from Next States
365	The core architectural principle of OpenClaw-RL is full decoupling: policy serving, environment
366	hosting, reward judging, and policy training run as four completely independent asynchronous
367	components with no blocking dependencies between them. The model serves the next user request
368	while the PRM judges previous responses and the trainer applies gradient updates. Weight updates
369	are pushed to the serving engine at well-defined boundaries, so live users always see a consistent
370	policy and never wait for training. The message of each new main-line request contains the reaction
371	to the previous turn, whether a user’s reply or an environment’s execution result. This becomes
372	5
373	
374	--- Page 6 ---
375	OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking
376	the next-state signal 𝑠𝑡+1 for the previous turn. The PRM is hosted as a separate inference server
377	and is responsible for automatic signal extraction: given an action 𝑎𝑡and the next state 𝑠𝑡+1, it
378	produces both an evaluative score and, when applicable, a directive hint. Because PRM judging is
379	decoupled from policy serving, signal extraction can use a stronger model and run multiple votes
380	per sample without affecting user-facing latency. This decoupling is what makes continuous training
381	from live, heterogeneous interaction streams practical: no stream needs to be paused or batched to
382	accommodate another component’s schedule.
383	2.3. Scalability: From Personal Agents to Real-World Agent Deployment
384	OpenClaw-RL is designed to operate across the full spectrum from single-user personal agents to
385	large-scale multi-environment general agent deployment. For personal agents, the environment is
386	a single user’s device and the interaction stream is sparse, session-based, and highly personalized.
387	Built on slime (Zhu et al., 2025), OpenClaw-RL inherits a scalable training infrastructure for general
388	agents, and we further support cloud-hosted environments across diverse agent settings. Hundreds
389	of parallel environments hosted on cloud services produce a dense stream of structured execution
390	signals, enabling scalable RL training.
391	Specifically, OpenClaw-RL supports a broad set of general-agent scenarios that cover the most
392	common real-world deployment settings in our open-source implementation (Table 1). Terminal
393	agents are a core component of computer-use systems: they are efficient, cheap to scale, and naturally
394	aligned with the text-based interface of LLMs (Anthropic, 2026; OpenAI, 2026; Shen et al., 2026).
395	GUI agents cover capabilities that terminal agents cannot access directly, such as visual interfaces and
396	pointer-based interactions, making them necessary for more general computer-use tasks (Qin et al.,
397	2025; Wang et al., 2025a,c; Xue et al., 2026). SWE agents represent a particularly important class of
398	coding agents, where the environment provides rich executable feedback through tests, diffs, and
399	static analysis (Cao et al., 2026). Tool-call agents are also critical, since external tools improve both
400	reasoning capability and factual accuracy (Feng et al., 2025a).
